# Basics of Failures — Section 5: KTU 2024 Examination Question Bank & Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define failure. Distinguish between sudden failure and gradual failure with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> Failure is the inability of an item to perform its required function under stated conditions of use for a specified period of time.

| Aspect | Sudden Failure | Gradual Failure |
|--------|----------------|------------------|
| Onset | Abrupt, no prior warning | Progressive, predictable |
| Detectability | Evident immediately | Requires condition monitoring |
| Typical cause | Overload, brittle fracture, electrical short | Wear, corrosion, fatigue |
| Industrial example | Snap-fracture of a turbine blade at rated RPM | Gradual pitting on gear teeth over months |

*(3 marks awarded for: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for tabular distinction, 1 mark for valid examples.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**What is the bathtub curve? Name the three regions and state the dominant failure type in each.**

**Model Answer:**

The bathtub curve is a graphical representation of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of a population of items versus time.

| Region | Name | Dominant Failure Type |
|--------|------|------------------------|
| I | Infant Mortality | Early-life failures due to manufacturing defects, poor installation |
| II | Useful Life | Random, chance failures at a roughly constant rate |
| III | Wear-out | Age-related failures due to fatigue, corrosion, wear, creep |

The shape resembles a bathtub — high on both ends and low in the middle. *(3 marks — 1 for shape explanation, 1 for region naming, 1 for correct failure types.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Define MTBF and MTTR. Derive the expression for steady-state availability in terms of these two quantities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The failure data of an industrial motor is observed to follow an exponential distribution with $\lambda = 0.0008$ failures/hour. Compute (i) MTBF, (ii) the reliability for a 500-hour mission, and (iii) the design life $t_{0.9}$ for which reliability is 0.90. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition of MTBF:**
> MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) is the average elapsed time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. For a non-repairable item the analogous metric is MTTF (Mean Time To Failure).

**Definition of MTTR:**
> MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) is the average time required to restore a failed system to operational status, including active repair time, logistics delay, and administrative delay.

**Derivation of steady-state availability:**

Consider one complete duty cycle consisting of operating time $T_{op}$ and repair time $T_{rep}$. Over many cycles:

$$
E[T_{op}] = MTBF, \qquad E[T_{rep}] = MTTR
$$

Availability is defined as the long-run fraction of time the system is operational:

$$
A = \frac{E[T_{op}]}{E[T_{op}] + E[T_{rep}]} = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $1/MTBF$:

$$
A = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF}}
$$

This shows availability is dominated by the ratio $\dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF}$. Reducing MTTR (faster repairs) is often more cost-effective than increasing MTBF (more reliable hardware). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

Given $\lambda = 0.0008$ failures/hour, exponential model.

**(i) MTBF:**

$$
MTBF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0008} = 1250 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Reliability for $t = 500$ hours:**

$$
R(500) = e^{-0.0008 \times 500} = e^{-0.4} \approx 0.6703
$$

So there is a **67.03 %** probability that the motor will operate failure-free for 500 hours.

**(iii) Design life for $R = 0.90$:**

Setting $R(t_{0.9}) = 0.90$:

$$
e^{-\lambda t_{0.9}} = 0.90
$$

Taking the natural log of both sides:

$$
-\lambda t_{0.9} = \ln(0.90) = -0.10536
$$

Solving for $t_{0.9}$:

$$
t_{0.9} = \frac{0.10536}{0.0008} \approx 131.7 \text{ hours}
$$

This is the **B10 life** — the time by which 10 % of the population would have failed. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the different categories of failure based on (i) extent of function loss, (ii) origin, and (iii) detectability. Give two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC machine centre has an MTBF of 600 hours and an MTTR of 12 hours. Calculate (i) availability, (ii) the expected downtime in a 30-day month assuming 24-hour operation, and (iii) the failure rate $\lambda$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**(i) By Extent of Function Loss:**

- **Complete failure** — total loss of function; the system cannot perform any part of its intended task.
  - *Examples:* snapped drive shaft, burnt-out motor winding.
- **Partial failure** — reduced capability; the system still operates but below acceptable performance limits.
  - *Examples:* worn pump delivering 80 % of rated flow, valve leaking 15 % of flow.

**(ii) By Origin:**

- **Primary failure** — failure of an item due to its own internal mechanisms, independent of any other equipment failure.
  - *Examples:* bearing fatigue failure, gear tooth pitting.
- **Secondary (consequential) failure** — failure directly induced by the primary failure of another item.
  - *Examples:* engine seizure due to coolant-pump failure, transformer burnout due to upstream short circuit.

**(iii) By Detectability:**

- **Evident failure** — discovered by the operator during normal duty without special inspection.
  - *Examples:* machine stops abruptly, alarm triggered, leak visible.
- **Hidden failure** — not discovered until a periodic inspection or until another failure occurs.
  - *Examples:* fire-suppression system failure discovered only when needed, emergency power generator failure discovered during testing. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b)

Given: $MTBF = 600$ h, $MTTR = 12$ h.

**(i) Availability:**

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{600}{600 + 12} = \frac{600}{612} \approx 0.9804 \;\;(\text{or } 98.04\%)
$$

**(ii) Expected downtime in 30 days at 24-hour operation:**

Total time in month $= 30 \times 24 = 720$ hours.

$$
\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 720 = 0.0196 \times 720 \approx 14.12 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Failure rate:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{600} \approx 0.001667 \text{ failures/hour}
$$

This corresponds to roughly **1.33 failures per 800 hours**, useful for spares forecasting. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose easy marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF.** MTBF applies to *repairable* systems; MTTF applies to *non-repairable* items. Writing "MTBF of a bulb" will lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting units.** Always write `hours`, `failures/hour`, or `dimensionless` alongside numerical answers. The KTU answer key explicitly checks units.
> 3. **Wrong availability formula.** Students often write $A = MTBF \times MTTR$. The correct expression is the *ratio* $A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)$.
> 4. **Treating failure rate as constant everywhere.** The bathtub curve explicitly shows $\lambda(t)$ is **not** constant across all three regions. The constant-rate assumption applies *only* to Region II.
> 5. **Not drawing/labeling the bathtub curve** when asked. Examiners expect a labelled sketch with Regions I, II, III clearly named and the failure-rate axis ($y$-axis) annotated.
> 6. **Skipping the integration step in derivations.** Step-by-step working earns full marks; jumping from definition to final result loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for "Basics of Failures" — KTU Module 1**

- **Definition of failure** is *context-dependent* — performance below acceptable standard for the stated application.
- **Five classifications of failure** to remember: by time (infant / random / wear-out), by rate (sudden / gradual), by extent (complete / partial), by origin (primary / secondary), by detectability (evident / hidden).
- **Failure mode vs mechanism**: mode = *how* it fails (observable), mechanism = *why* it fails (physical/chemical).
- **Reliability function**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (constant failure rate).
- **Unreliability**: $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$.
- **MTBF** $= 1/\lambda$; **MTTR** $= 1/\mu$.
- **Steady-state availability**: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- **Weibull shape parameter $\beta$**: $< 1$ = infant, $= 1$ = random, $> 1$ = wear-out.
- **Bathtub curve** has three regions; the failure rate is high at start, low and constant in the middle, rising again at end-of-life.
- **Units to always write**: hours (for time), failures/hour (for $\lambda$), dimensionless (for $R$, $A$).
- **Design life formula**: $t_{R^*} = -\dfrac{\ln(R^*)}{\lambda}$.
- **Reduce MTTR first** — often more cost-effective than improving MTBF for the same availability gain.
- **Hidden failures** are dangerous because they are not detected until a secondary failure occurs; hence the importance of scheduled inspection.
- **Wear-out failures** dominate after the design life; preventive replacement schedules aim to operate *before* Region III begins.

---

> **End of Module 1 — Basics of Failures Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Defects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sources of Defects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Maintenance Engineering and Management, a **defect** is defined as any deviation of a component, assembly, sub-system, or system from its stipulated design specifications, functional performance parameters, or expected service life that compromises its intended operational capability, reliability, or safety. The **sources of defects** refer to the root causes — originating at various life-cycle stages of an asset — that initiate or propagate such deviations, ultimately leading to failure if not detected and corrected.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, defects are classified not merely as random events but as **traceable phenomena** with identifiable origins. The discipline of maintenance engineering groups these origins into structured categories to enable systematic **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** and **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OEIET613 Module 1):**
> A defect is the *symptom* of an underlying cause. The "source" of a defect is the **causal agent** that initiated the deviation. Effective maintenance management demands identifying the *source*, not merely treating the *symptom*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you buy a brand-new ceiling fan. After six months, you notice a rattling sound and reduced airflow. The **defect** is the rattling/loss of performance. Where did this defect *come from*?

- Was it a **design defect**? (e.g., the blade geometry was never aerodynamic in the first place).
- Was it a **manufacturing defect**? (e.g., the motor winding had a hairline crack from the factory).
- Was it a **material defect**? (e.g., the bearing was made of sub-standard steel that wore out fast).
- Was it an **operational defect**? (e.g., the fan was run at a higher voltage than rated).
- Was it a **maintenance defect**? (e.g., lubricant was never changed, so the bearing seized).

**The "source" is the "why behind the what."** Just as a doctor doesn't just treat a fever (symptom) without finding the infection (source), a maintenance engineer must trace defects to their source to prevent recurrence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> A defect is something *wrong* with equipment. The *source* is **where that wrong came from** — was it built wrong, used wrong, or maintained wrong? The answer changes how you fix it.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Used in KTU Valuation

| Term | Meaning in Maintenance Context |
|---|---|
| Defect | Any non-conformance to specification |
| Source | Origin/cause of the defect |
| Failure | Inability of an item to perform its required function |
| Fault | State of an item characterized by inability to perform |
| Degradation | Gradual impairment of functional capability |

### 1.4 Physical & Operational Standards

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize that defect sources are evaluated against standard reliability and quality metrics such as **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**, **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)**, **Availability**, and **Reliability (R(t))**. These metrics — typically expressed in **hours**, **percentage (%)**, or **dimensionless probability** — quantify the impact of defect sources on system performance.

> [!TIP]
> When describing any source of defect in your KTU answer script, always tie it back to its effect on **Reliability**, **Availability**, **Maintainability**, or **Safety (RAMS)** to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Sources of Defects — The Master Framework

Maintenance engineering textbooks (and KTU Module 1 content) classify defect sources into **seven primary categories**, each linked to a specific life-cycle phase of the asset. Understanding this taxonomy is essential for any Part B (14-mark) question on this topic.

#### 2.1.1 Design Defects
- **Origin:** Conceptualization and engineering drawing stage.
- **How they occur:** Incorrect stress analysis, poor tolerances, inadequate material selection at the design table, failure to account for operating environment, ergonomic oversights.
- **Why they matter:** These are *latent defects* — built into the asset before it is even manufactured. They manifest repeatedly in every unit produced from that design.
- **Engineering example:** A pump impeller designed without considering cavitation margin → repeated cavitation pitting.

#### 2.1.2 Manufacturing / Fabrication Defects
- **Origin:** Production floor (machining, casting, forging, welding, heat treatment).
- **How they occur:** Tooling errors, incorrect machine settings, improper heat treatment, weld porosity, surface roughness beyond spec, dimensional out-of-tolerance conditions.
- **Why they matter:** These are *inherent defects* introduced during construction. Often invisible at delivery and detected only under service load.
- **Engineering example:** Misaligned bearing seat on a shaft leading to cyclic loading and premature fatigue failure.

#### 2.1.3 Material Defects
- **Origin:** Raw material procurement and metallurgical processing.
- **How they occur:** Inclusions, voids, micro-cracks, incorrect alloy composition, improper hardness, residual stresses, hydrogen embrittlement.
- **Why they matter:** The "DNA" of the component. Material defects are typically the most catastrophic and least predictable.
- **Engineering example:** Sulfide inclusions in a crankshaft leading to fatigue crack initiation.

#### 2.1.4 Assembly / Installation Defects
- **Origin:** On-site erection and commissioning.
- **How they occur:** Improper alignment, loose fasteners, incorrect torque, contamination during assembly, missing parts, reversed polarities in electrical systems.
- **Why they matter:** Even a perfectly designed and manufactured component can fail if assembled incorrectly.
- **Engineering example:** Shaft misalignment of just 0.05 mm causing bearing failure within weeks.

#### 2.1.5 Operational / Human-Induced Defects
- **Origin:** Day-to-day use of the equipment by operators.
- **How they occur:** Overloading, over-speeding, operating outside design envelope, unauthorized modifications, negligence, inadequate operator training.
- **Why they matter:** Many industrial surveys cite operator error as the **single largest cause** of equipment defects.
- **Engineering example:** Running a motor above rated current continuously, leading to insulation breakdown.

#### 2.1.6 Maintenance-Induced Defects
- **Origin:** Poorly executed or omitted maintenance activities.
- **How they occur:** Improper lubrication (wrong grade, over/under-lubrication), incorrect reassembly, foreign object debris (FOD) left inside equipment, over-tightening, use of non-OEM spares.
- **Why they matter:** **Paradox of maintenance** — sometimes the act of maintaining *introduces* the defect it was meant to prevent.
- **Engineering example:** Contaminated hydraulic fluid introduced during oil change damaging servo valves.

#### 2.1.7 Environmental / External Defects
- **Origin:** Surrounding operating environment.
- **How they occur:** Corrosion, erosion, thermal cycling, vibration, dust ingress, humidity, chemical exposure, seismic activity.
- **Why they matter:** Often unavoidable; must be designed-for or mitigated via protective maintenance.
- **Engineering example:** Salt-laden coastal atmosphere corroding switchgear contacts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:**
> In a 14-mark question asking to "discuss the sources of defects," examiners expect you to cover **at least five to six categories** with a **real industrial example for each**. Citing examples like bearings, pumps, or motors earns you extra marks.

### 2.2 The Life-Cycle Linkage Model

Sources of defects are not random — they are **time-stamped** to the asset's life cycle:

$$
\text{Life Cycle Phases} = \{\text{Concept} \rightarrow \text{Design} \rightarrow \text{Manufacture} \rightarrow \text{Install} \rightarrow \text{Operate} \rightarrow \text{Maintain} \rightarrow \text{Decommission}\}
$$

Each phase contributes its own characteristic defect sources. A powerful way to remember them is via the acronym **D-M-M-A-O-M-E**:
- **D**esign
- **M**anufacturing
- **M**aterial
- **A**ssembly
- **O**perational
- **M**aintenance
- **E**nvironmental

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Metric | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability (exponential) | $R(t)$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda$ = failure rate |
| Failure Rate | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | failures/hour | Constant failure rate region |
| MTBF | $\text{MTBF}$ | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Mean Time Between Failures |
| Availability (Inherent) | $A_i$ | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | fraction (0-1) | Ideal case |
| Availability (Achieved) | $A_a$ | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | fraction (0-1) | Real-world |
| Defect Rate | $D_r$ | $D_r = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | defects/unit | Six-Sigma metric |
| P-F Interval | $t_{P-F}$ | Time between Potential failure detection and Functional failure | hours | Drives preventive maintenance |
| Bath-Tub Curve Phases | $h(t)$ | Early-life $\downarrow$, Useful life $= \lambda$, Wear-out $\uparrow$ | — | Visualizes defect-source dominance over time |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing formulas in KTU answer scripts, **always state the units** and **define every symbol**. Examiners deduct marks for undefined variables.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace:** Design and material defects are aggressively screened (NDT, FMEA) because failure is catastrophic.
- **Power Plants:** Operational and maintenance-induced defects dominate; scheduled overhauls target these.
- **Manufacturing Industries:** Manufacturing and material defects are controlled via Six-Sigma and Statistical Process Control (SPC).
- **Civil Infrastructure:** Environmental defects (corrosion, cracking) drive inspection intervals.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reliability Function and the Defect-Source Connection

The exponential reliability function models the probability that a component survives up to time $t$ under a *constant failure rate* (the "useful life" region of the bath-tub curve where most operational and environmental defects dominate).

**Starting premise:**
A component has a constant probability of failure per unit time, denoted $\lambda$ (failures per hour).

**Step 1 — Express the probability of survival in a small interval $dt$:**

$$
P(\text{survive in } dt) = 1 - \lambda \, dt
$$

This means: in a vanishingly small time interval $dt$, the chance of *not* failing is $1$ minus the tiny failure chance $\lambda \, dt$.

**Step 2 — Extend to a finite interval $t$ by integrating:**

Surviving for time $t$ requires surviving every infinitesimal slice $dt$ from $0$ to $t$:

$$
R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \lambda \frac{t}{n}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard limit $\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 + \frac{x}{n}\right)^{n} = e^{x}$:**

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — Link to Mean Time Between Failures:**

For the exponential distribution, the mean (expected) lifetime is:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \left[ \frac{-1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{0 - (-1/\lambda)}{1} = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

Hence:

$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for defect sources:** If a particular defect source (say, corrosion) accelerates $\lambda$, the reliability $R(t)$ drops exponentially, meaning the asset's useful life shrinks. This is why controlling defect sources directly extends MTBF.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inherent Availability and the Cost of Defects

Inherent availability $A_i$ measures the fraction of time the equipment *could be* operating if downtime were only due to *corrective maintenance* actions (i.e., it excludes logistics, supply, and administrative delays).

**Step 1 — Define MTBF and MTTR:**

- $\text{MTBF}$ = Mean Time Between Failures (operating time / number of failures)
- $\text{MTTR}$ = Mean Time To Repair (sum of repair times / number of repairs)

**Step 2 — Total cycle time for one failure-repair cycle:**

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability is the fraction of the cycle spent operating:**

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in percentage form for KTU answers:**

$$
A_i (\%) = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defect-source insight:** A *manufacturing defect* tends to lower MTBF (failures happen more often), while a *maintenance-induced defect* tends to raise MTTR (repairs take longer). Both reduce $A_i$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Each Defect Source to its Engineering Signature

| Source of Defect | Typical Failure Mode | Diagnostic Technique | Prevention Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design | Fatigue, over-stress | FMEA, FEA simulation | Design review, derating |
| Manufacturing | Crack, dimensional error | NDT (Dye penetrant, MPI, UT) | SPC, quality gates |
| Material | Brittle fracture, corrosion | Metallography, spectroscopy | Material certification |
| Assembly | Misalignment, loose fit | Laser alignment, vibration analysis | Torque procedures, checklists |
| Operational | Overheat, overload | SCADA trending, IR thermography | Operator training, SOPs |
| Maintenance | FOD, contamination | Oil analysis, borescope inspection | Training, OEM procedures |
| Environmental | Corrosion, erosion | Thickness gauging, visual | Coatings, enclosures, derating |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:**
A centrifugal pump operates with an MTBF of 2000 hours and MTTR of 8 hours. Calculate (a) Inherent Availability, (b) Failure Rate, (c) Reliability at 500 hours.

**Given:**
$\text{MTBF} = 2000$ h, $\text{MTTR} = 8$ h, $t = 500$ h.

**Solution:**

(a) Inherent Availability:

$$
A_i = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008} = 0.9960
$$

In percentage: $A_i = 99.60\%$.

(b) Failure Rate:

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{2000} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

(c) Reliability at 500 hours:

$$
R(500) = e^{-500/2000} = e^{-0.25} = 0.7788
$$

> [!TIP]
> This means the pump has a **77.88% probability** of running continuously for 500 hours without failure. KTU examiners love asking such direct numerical problems — practice these formula drills.

### 3.5 Derivation 3 — Bathtub Curve Phases and Defect-Source Dominance

The hazard (failure) rate $h(t)$ over the life of a component follows a characteristic **bathtub shape** with three regions:

- **Phase I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Dominated by *manufacturing* and *material* defects. $h(t)$ is *decreasing* with time.

- **Phase II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** Dominated by *operational* and *environmental* defects. $h(t)$ is *constant* (exponential model applies).

- **Phase III — Wear-out:** Dominated by *design* (insufficient margin), *maintenance* (deferred), and cumulative *environmental* defects. $h(t)$ is *increasing* with time.

Mathematically, the Weibull distribution captures this:

$$
R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

where:
- $\eta$ = characteristic life (scale parameter)
- $\beta$ = shape parameter
  - $\beta < 1$: Phase I (decreasing hazard)
  - $\beta = 1$: Phase II (constant hazard, reduces to exponential)
  - $\beta > 1$: Phase III (increasing hazard)

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU 2024:**
> You do not need to memorize the full Weibull derivation, but you **must** be able to *sketch* the bathtub curve and *label each phase* with the dominant defect source.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Defect Sources (Mermaid)

The following block-level architecture maps the seven primary sources of defects to their respective life-cycle stages and dominant failure modes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Equipment Defect Detected] --> B1{Root Source Identification}
    B1 --> C1[Design Defects]
    B1 --> C2[Manufacturing Defects]
    B1 --> C3[Material Defects]
    B1 --> C4[Assembly Defects]
    B1 --> C5[Operational Defects]
    B1 --> C6[Maintenance Defects]
    B1 --> C7[Environmental Defects]
    C1 --> D1[Failure Modes: Fatigue Over-stress]
    C2 --> D2[Failure Modes: Cracks Dimensional Errors]
    C3 --> D3[Failure Modes: Brittle Fracture Corrosion]
    C4 --> D4[Failure Modes: Misalignment Looseness]
    C5 --> D5[Failure Modes: Overheat Overload]
    C6 --> D6[Failure Modes: FOD Contamination]
    C7 --> D7[Failure Modes: Corrosion Erosion]
    D1 --> E1[Impact: Low MTBF High lambda]
    D2 --> E2[Impact: Low MTBF High lambda]
    D3 --> E3[Impact: Low MTBF High lambda]
    D4 --> E4[Impact: High MTTR Low Availability]
    D5 --> E5[Impact: Low MTBF Safety Risk]
    D6 --> E6[Impact: High MTTR Low Availability]
    D7 --> E7[Impact: Degrading MTBF High lambda]
    E1 --> F1[Result: Reduced Reliability and Availability]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    E6 --> F1
    E7 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Maintenance Action: RCA and Corrective Action]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Life-Cycle Mapping of Defect Sources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LC1[Concept Phase] --> LC2[Design Phase]
    LC2 --> LC3[Manufacturing Phase]
    LC3 --> LC4[Installation Phase]
    LC4 --> LC5[Operation Phase]
    LC5 --> LC6[Maintenance Phase]
    LC6 --> LC7[Decommission Phase]
    LC2 --> S1[Source: Design Defects]
    LC3 --> S2[Source: Manufacturing Defects]
    LC3 --> S3[Source: Material Defects]
    LC4 --> S4[Source: Assembly Defects]
    LC5 --> S5[Source: Operational Defects]
    LC6 --> S6[Source: Maintenance Defects]
    LC1 --> S7[Source: Requirement Defects]
    LC7 --> S8[Source: Aging and Obsolescence]
    S1 --> BT1[Bath-Tub Phase 3 Wear Out]
    S2 --> BT2[Bath-Tub Phase 1 Infant Mortality]
    S3 --> BT3[Bath-Tub Phase 1 Infant Mortality]
    S4 --> BT4[No Fixed Phase Random]
    S5 --> BT5[Bath-Tub Phase 2 Useful Life]
    S6 --> BT6[Bath-Tub Phase 3 Wear Out]
    S7 --> BT7[Bath-Tub Phase 1 Infant Mortality]
    S8 --> BT8[Bath-Tub Phase 3 Wear Out]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Diagnostic-Treatment Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Defect Observed] --> Q1{Is the defect repeatable?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, every unit| R1[Design Defect Source]
    Q1 -->|No, sporadic| Q2{Does the defect appear in early life?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, within commissioning| R2[Manufacturing or Material Defect Source]
    Q2 -->|No, after settling in| Q3{Did the defect appear after maintenance?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, post PM| R3[Maintenance Induced Defect Source]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Is the defect correlated with operating conditions?}
    Q4 -->|Yes, with load or environment| R4[Operational or Environmental Defect Source]
    Q4 -->|No| R5[Assembly Defect Source]
    R1 --> A1[Action: Engineering Change Order]
    R2 --> A2[Action: Supplier Quality Audit]
    R3 --> A3[Action: Maintenance Procedure Review]
    R4 --> A4[Action: Operator Training and Environment Control]
    R5 --> A5[Action: Installation and Commissioning Review]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Bathtub Curve with Defect-Source Overlay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Equipment Age] --> Y1[Phase 1 Infant Mortality]
    X --> Y2[Phase 2 Useful Life]
    X --> Y3[Phase 3 Wear Out]

    Y1 --> Z2[Source: Manufacturing and Material]
    Y2 --> Z3[Lambda Constant]

    Y3 --> Z6[Source: Design Insufficiency and Cumulative Wear]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define the term "defect" in the context of maintenance engineering. How is it different from "failure"?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **defect** is any deviation of a component or system from its stipulated design specification, performance parameter, or expected service condition that compromises intended function. (1.5 marks)
A **failure**, in contrast, is the *inability* of the item to perform its required function — i.e., the defect has progressed to the point of functional loss. (1 mark)
In short: a *defect* is the cause/origin; a *failure* is the *consequence* of an unaddressed defect. (0.5 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]**
List any **three** sources of defects that arise during the operational phase of an equipment's life cycle.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Overloading** — operating the equipment beyond its rated capacity.
2. **Operator error** — incorrect start/stop sequences, failure to monitor gauges.
3. **Unauthorized modification** — altering set-points or bypassing safety interlocks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]*

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **sources of defects** in industrial equipment, classifying them according to the life-cycle stage at which they originate. Provide **at least one engineering example** for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **bathtub curve**. Discuss how each phase of the bathtub curve is linked to a specific type of defect source. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Sources of Defects (7 marks):**

The sources of defects in industrial equipment can be broadly classified into the following categories based on the life-cycle stage:

1. **Design Defects (1 mark):** Originate at the engineering drawing stage — incorrect stress analysis, poor material selection, inadequate tolerances, missing design margins. *Example:* Improperly sized gear teeth leading to pitting failure.

2. **Manufacturing Defects (1 mark):** Originate at the production stage — wrong machining dimensions, improper heat treatment, weld defects, surface roughness. *Example:* Misaligned bearing journal causing premature fatigue.

3. **Material Defects (1 mark):** Originate in the raw material — inclusions, voids, incorrect alloy, hydrogen embrittlement. *Example:* Sulfide inclusions in steel shafts causing fatigue cracks.

4. **Assembly / Installation Defects (1 mark):** Loose fasteners, shaft misalignment, contamination, reversed connections. *Example:* Coupling misalignment of 0.1 mm halving bearing life.

5. **Operational Defects (1 mark):** Overload, over-speed, improper use, untrained operators. *Example:* Running a motor at 110% rated voltage.

6. **Maintenance-Induced Defects (1 mark):** Contamination during oil change, wrong spares, over-tightening. *Example:* Hydraulic servo-valve failure from contaminated oil.

7. **Environmental Defects (1 mark):** Corrosion, erosion, dust, humidity, temperature extremes. *Example:* Coastal corrosion of switchgear.

> Each example should be tied back to its specific life-cycle phase. Use bullet points and bold category names for clarity in the answer script.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Bathtub Curve (7 marks):**

**Sketch (2 marks):**

```
Failure Rate (lambda)
   |
   |\
   | \         /
   |  \       /
   |   \-----/        <-- Constant
   |    \   /
   |     \ /
   |______\/___________________
        Phase 1  Phase 2  Phase 3
       Infant  Useful   Wear-out
       Mortality  Life
       |
   ----+-------------------------------> Time
```

**Explanation (5 marks — 1 mark per phase + linking):**

- **Phase I — Infant Mortality:** Failure rate is high but *decreasing*. Dominated by **manufacturing** and **material** defects (latent flaws that fail early under service stress). Burn-in testing is used to eliminate these.

- **Phase II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is *constant* and low. Dominated by **operational** and **environmental** defects (random over-stress, corrosion onset). Reliability follows $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

- **Phase III — Wear-out:** Failure rate is *increasing*. Dominated by **design insufficiency** (inadequate service-life margin) and **maintenance-deferred** defects (cumulative wear, fatigue). Preventive maintenance and overhaul are scheduled here.

> The **defect source dominance shifts** with the phase — which is why the same equipment may require *different maintenance strategies* at different ages.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **MTBF**, **MTTR**, and **Inherent Availability** with suitable formulas. Show how **manufacturing defects** and **maintenance-induced defects** affect these parameters differently. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A critical motor in a process plant has an MTBF of 3000 hours and an MTTR of 12 hours. Calculate its **(i) failure rate**, **(ii) inherent availability**, and **(iii) reliability at 1000 hours of continuous operation**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — MTBF, MTTR, Availability (7 marks):**

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operating time between successive failures.

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}
$$

*Unit: hours.* (1 mark)

- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time required to restore equipment after a failure.

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}
$$

*Unit: hours.* (1 mark)

- **Inherent Availability ($A_i$):** Fraction of time equipment is *capable* of operating (excluding logistics delays). (1 mark)

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

*Unit: dimensionless (0 to 1) or percentage.* (1 mark)

- **Effect of Manufacturing Defects (1.5 marks):**
Manufacturing defects cause *early* failures → **MTBF decreases** (failures happen sooner and more often). $\lambda$ increases. **MTTR** may also rise if the failures are severe (e.g., catastrophic fracture).

- **Effect of Maintenance-Induced Defects (1.5 marks):**
These defects arise *during* repair → **MTTR increases** (repairs take longer, or have to be redone). MTBF may also drop if the introduced defect causes early re-failure. Both effects drive $A_i$ *downward*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students *conflate* MTBF and MTTR. **MTBF is the time *between* failures (operating time); MTTR is the time *to repair* (downtime).** Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> - In numerical answers, students often forget to convert $A_i$ to **percentage**. Examiners in KTU 2024 scheme explicitly expect the unit to be stated.
> - For 14-mark questions, **always support formulas with a one-line definition**. A naked formula without context loses marks.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $\text{MTBF} = 3000$ h, $\text{MTTR} = 12$ h, $t = 1000$ h.

**(i) Failure Rate (2 marks):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{3000} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

**(ii) Inherent Availability (2 marks):**

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{3000}{3000 + 12} = \frac{3000}{3012} = 0.9960
$$

In percentage: $A_i = 99.60\%$.

**(iii) Reliability at 1000 hours (3 marks):**

Using the exponential reliability model:

$$
R(1000) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}} = e^{-1000/3000} = e^{-0.3333}
$$

$$
R(1000) = 0.7166
$$

Thus, the motor has a **71.66% probability** of operating continuously for 1000 hours without failure.

> [!TIP]
> Always show the *substitution step* and *final numerical value with units* in numerical KTU answers. Skipping the substitution costs 1 mark; forgetting units costs 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Sources of Defects:**

- ✅ **Definition:** A *defect* is deviation from specification; a *failure* is loss of function. A *source* is the causal origin.
- ✅ **Seven Categories (D-M-M-A-O-M-E):** **D**esign, **M**anufacturing, **M**aterial, **A**ssembly, **O**perational, **M**aintenance, **E**nvironmental.
- ✅ **Life-Cycle Linkage:** Each defect source maps to a *specific life-cycle phase* of the asset.
- ✅ **Reliability Formula:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ — define all symbols and units in exam answers.
- ✅ **Inherent Availability:** $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ — state as a fraction AND a percentage.
- ✅ **Failure Rate:** $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$, unit is **failures per hour**.
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve:** Three phases — Infant Mortality (manufacturing/material), Useful Life (operational/environmental, constant $\lambda$), Wear-out (design insufficiency/maintenance deferred).
- ✅ **Manufacturing defects** ↓ MTBF; **Maintenance defects** ↑ MTTR — both ↓ $A_i$.
- ✅ **Industrial Examples to Memorize:** Bearing misalignment (assembly), motor insulation breakdown (operational), pump cavitation (design), hydraulic contamination (maintenance), switchgear corrosion (environmental).
- ✅ **Always link answers to RAMS:** **R**eliability, **A**vailability, **M**aintainability, **S**afety.
- ✅ **Draw sketches wherever possible:** The bathtub curve sketch alone is worth 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.
- ✅ **P-F Interval concept:** Time between potential failure detection and functional failure — drives preventive maintenance scheduling.
- ✅ **Remember FMEA, RCA, SPC, NDT** as the corresponding analysis/prevention tools for each defect source category.
- ✅ **For numerical problems:** Show formula → substitution → answer with units. Examiners reward step-by-step working.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Failure Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Failure Rate (λ)**: In reliability engineering, the **failure rate** is defined as the *instantaneous rate at which a component, system, or equipment fails*, expressed as the number of failures per unit time, conditional upon the item having survived up to that specific time. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the **probability density function of failures** $f(t)$ to the **reliability function** $R(t)$.

Formally, for a continuous non-negative random variable $T$ representing the time-to-failure:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{f(t)}{1 - F(t)}
$$

Where:
- $f(t)$ = Probability Density Function (PDF) of failure
- $R(t)$ = Reliability function (probability of survival up to time $t$)
- $F(t)$ = Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Units of Failure Rate**: The standard engineering unit is **failures per hour** or **failures per million hours (FPMH)**, also known as **FIT (Failures In Time)**, where 1 FIT = $1 \times 10^{-9}$ failures/hour.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine you are tracking the health of a population of **100 identical electric bulbs** switched on simultaneously:

- After 100 hours, 10 bulbs have failed → 90 still glowing
- The **failure rate** at 100 hours tells you: *"Out of the 90 surviving bulbs, how many are expected to fail in the next hour, per bulb?"*
- If 2 out of 90 fail in the next hour → $\lambda(100) = 2/90 = 0.0222$ **failures/bulb-hour**

### The "Car Maintenance" Analogy

Think of your car engine:
- **Early life**: The engine has "infant mortality" — manufacturing defects surface in the first few hundred kilometers (early failures)
- **Useful life**: The engine runs smoothly, and failures are random — a bolt may loosen, a hose may burst, but it's unpredictable
- **Wear-out phase**: After 150,000+ km, piston rings wear out, bearings fail — failure rate climbs steeply

This three-stage behavior is captured beautifully by the **Bathtub Curve**, the most iconic visual in reliability engineering.

---

## 1.3 Three Types of Failure Rate Behavior

| Behavior | Description | Engineering Phase |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Decreasing Failure Rate (DFR)** | $\lambda(t)$ falls with time | Infant mortality / Burn-in |
| **Constant Failure Rate (CFR)** | $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ = constant | Useful life / Random failures |
| **Increasing Failure Rate (IFR)** | $\lambda(t)$ rises with time | Wear-out phase |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: When asked to define failure rate, always mention the **conditional probability** nature — it is "given survival up to time $t$, what is the chance of failure in the next instant $dt$?"

---

## 1.4 Conditional Probability Foundation

The failure rate is fundamentally a **conditional probability density**:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t + \Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}
$$

This means: *"Given that the item is still working at time $t$, what is the probability that it fails in the next infinitesimal interval $dt$?"*

This conditional nature is what distinguishes failure rate from the ordinary PDF $f(t)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deriving the Mathematical Foundation

Starting from the basic definition of probability:

**Step 1 — Cumulative Failure Probability (CDF):**
$$
F(t) = P(T \leq t) = \int_0^t f(u) \, du
$$
This is the probability that failure has occurred by time $t$.

**Step 2 — Reliability Function (Survival Function):**
$$
R(t) = P(T > t) = 1 - F(t)
$$
This is the probability of *no failure* up to time $t$.

**Step 3 — Probability Density of Failure (PDF):**
$$
f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Failure Rate (Hazard Function):**
$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-dR(t)/dt}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 5 — Exponential Form of Reliability:**
Multiplying both sides by $dt$ and integrating:
$$
\int_0^t \lambda(u) \, du = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)
$$
Since $R(0) = 1$:
$$
R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(u) \, du}
$$

For **constant failure rate** ($\lambda(t) = \lambda$):
$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all essential failure rate formulas** required for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Definition of failure rate | failures/time |
| 2 | $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(u)\,du}$ | Reliability from failure rate | dimensionless (probability) |
| 3 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability (constant $\lambda$) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | PDF (constant $\lambda$ — exponential) | 1/time |
| 5 | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure (constant $\lambda$) | hours |
| 6 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures (constant $\lambda$, repairable) | hours |
| 7 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{downtime}}{\text{failures}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| 8 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Operational availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 9 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | CDF (constant $\lambda$) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Weibull failure rate (general) | failures/time |
| 11 | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | Weibull reliability | dimensionless |
| 12 | $f(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$ | Weibull PDF | 1/time |
| 13 | $\lambda = \dfrac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}$ | Empirical failure rate | failures/time |
| 14 | $\text{R}(t_1+t_2) = R(t_1) \cdot R(t_2)$ | Multiplicative property (constant $\lambda$) | dimensionless |

> **Critical Note on Pipes**: Notice the use of `\vert` style in the Weibull row above (using $\vert$ implicitly via parentheses) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Avoid raw `|` in table cells.

---

## 2.3 The Bathtub Curve — Three Regions Explained

### Region I: Infant Mortality (Burn-in)
- **Behavior**: $\lambda(t)$ is **decreasing**
- **Causes**: Manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, material impurities, inadequate quality control
- **Engineering Action**: Run-in testing, burn-in, screening
- **Example**: Newly manufactured IC chips tested under elevated stress before shipping

### Region II: Useful Life (Random Failures)
- **Behavior**: $\lambda(t)$ is **constant** ($\lambda = \lambda_0$)
- **Causes**: Random shocks, overloading, human error, environmental stress
- **Engineering Action**: Preventive maintenance, redundancy design
- **Example**: Aircraft engine during its normal operational lifespan

### Region III: Wear-out
- **Behavior**: $\lambda(t)$ is **increasing** (often exponentially)
- **Causes**: Corrosion, fatigue, wear, aging, erosion
- **Engineering Action**: Scheduled replacement, refurbishment
- **Example**: Car brake pads wearing thin after 50,000 km

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Aerospace**: Failure rate data drives aircraft component replacement schedules (e.g., turbine blades replaced every N flight hours)
2. **Electronics**: FIT rates determine warranty periods for semiconductors
3. **Nuclear Power**: Reliability block diagrams (RBD) use failure rates to compute system unavailability
4. **Manufacturing**: TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) targets failure rate reduction in CNC machines
5. **Automotive**: Weibull analysis used to predict warranty claims
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability from Constant Failure Rate

### Given:
A system has a constant failure rate $\lambda$.

### To Derive:
The reliability function $R(t)$.

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1**: Start with the definition of failure rate as a conditional probability over a small interval $\Delta t$:

$$
P(\text{failure in } [t, t+\Delta t] \mid \text{survived to } t) \approx \lambda \Delta t
$$

**Step 2**: The probability of **surviving** the small interval is:

$$
P(\text{survive } [t, t+\Delta t] \mid \text{survived to } t) \approx 1 - \lambda \Delta t
$$

**Step 3**: The probability of surviving from $0$ to $t+\Delta t$ is the product of surviving from $0$ to $t$ and from $t$ to $t+\Delta t$:

$$
R(t + \Delta t) = R(t) \cdot (1 - \lambda \Delta t)
$$

**Step 4**: Rearrange to isolate the change in reliability:

$$
R(t + \Delta t) - R(t) = -\lambda R(t) \Delta t
$$

$$
\frac{R(t + \Delta t) - R(t)}{\Delta t} = -\lambda R(t)
$$

**Step 5**: Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda R(t)
$$

**Step 6**: This is a first-order linear ODE. Separate variables:

$$
\frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

**Step 7**: Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt
$$

**Step 8**: Evaluate the integrals (using $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$):

$$
\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t
$$

$$
\ln R(t) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 9**: Exponentiate both sides:

$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}
$$

This is the **exponential reliability function** — the cornerstone of constant failure rate analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MTTF from Constant Failure Rate

### Given:
$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for a non-repairable component.

### To Derive:
The Mean Time To Failure (MTTF).

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1**: The expected value (mean) of the time-to-failure random variable is defined as:

$$
\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute $f(t) = -dR/dt$ (from the relationship between PDF and reliability):

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \left(-\frac{dR}{dt}\right) dt
$$

**Step 3**: Integrate by parts using $\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$, with $u = t$ and $dv = -dR$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \left[ -t \cdot R(t) \right]_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty (-1) \cdot R(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 4**: The boundary term vanishes because:
- At $t = 0$: $-0 \cdot R(0) = 0$
- At $t = \infty$: $R(\infty) = 0$ (eventual failure), so the term is $0$

**Step 5**: This leaves:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty R(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 6**: Substitute $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt
$$

**Step 7**: Evaluate the integral:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \left[ -\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t} \right]_0^\infty
$$

**Step 8**: Apply limits:

$$
\text{MTTF} = \left(0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right)\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Weibull Failure Rate Behavior

The **Weibull distribution** is the most general model used in KTU to represent all three regions of the bathtub curve via the **shape parameter** $\beta$.

### Given:
- Weibull reliability: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$
- Scale parameter: $\eta > 0$
- Shape parameter: $\beta > 0$

### To Derive:
The Weibull failure rate $\lambda(t)$.

### Step-by-Step Proof:

**Step 1**: Take the derivative of $R(t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}
$$

**Step 2**: Apply the chain rule ($e^u$ derivative is $e^u \cdot du/dt$):

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^\beta\right]
$$

**Step 3**: Differentiate the inner term:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^\beta\right] = -\beta \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta}
$$

**Step 4**: Combine:

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = -e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta} \cdot \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

**Step 5**: Compute the PDF $f(t) = -dR/dt$:

$$
f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}
$$

**Step 6**: Apply the failure rate definition $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{\dfrac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}}{e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}}
$$

**Step 7**: The exponentials cancel:

$$
\boxed{\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}}
$$

### Behavior Analysis of $\lambda(t)$ based on $\beta$:

**Case 1: $\beta < 1$ (Infant Mortality)**
$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta^\beta} t^{\beta-1} \quad \text{with } \beta-1 < 0
$$
As $t$ increases, $t^{\beta-1} \to 0$, so $\lambda(t)$ **decreases** — DFR behavior.

**Case 2: $\beta = 1$ (Useful Life / Random Failures)**
$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{1}{\eta} = \text{constant}
$$
The Weibull reduces to the **exponential distribution** — CFR behavior.

**Case 3: $\beta > 1$ (Wear-out)**
With $\beta - 1 > 0$, the term $t^{\beta-1}$ grows without bound, so $\lambda(t)$ **increases** — IFR behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight**: The shape parameter $\beta$ is the "switch" that controls whether the Weibull models infant mortality ($\beta < 1$), random failures ($\beta = 1$), or wear-out ($\beta > 1$). This makes the Weibull distribution the **single most versatile reliability model**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem:
A batch of 1000 identical components is tested. The following failure data is observed:

| Time interval (hours) | Failures in interval | Components at risk at start |
|----------------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| 0 – 100 | 50 | 1000 |
| 100 – 200 | 30 | 950 |
| 200 – 300 | 25 | 920 |
| 300 – 400 | 20 | 895 |

**Compute:**
(a) The failure rate for each interval  
(b) The reliability at $t = 400$ hours  
(c) The MTTF (approximate)

### Solution:

**(a) Failure rate per interval** ($\lambda = \text{failures} / (\text{components at risk} \times \Delta t)$, with $\Delta t = 100$):

$$
\lambda_1 = \frac{50}{1000 \times 100} = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = \frac{30}{950 \times 100} = 3.16 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

$$
\lambda_3 = \frac{25}{920 \times 100} = 2.72 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

$$
\lambda_4 = \frac{20}{895 \times 100} = 2.23 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

**Observation**: Failure rate is **decreasing** — characteristic of **infant mortality (DFR)**.

**(b) Reliability at $t = 400$ hours**:
$$
R(400) = \frac{\text{components surviving at 400 hrs}}{\text{initial components}} = \frac{895 - 20}{1000} = \frac{875}{1000} = 0.875
$$

**(c) Approximate MTTF using interval midpoint method**:

$$
\text{MTTF} \approx \sum (\text{midpoint} \times \text{fraction surviving at midpoint}) \times \Delta t
$$

- Midpoints: 50, 150, 250, 350 hours
- Fractions surviving at midpoints: 975/1000, 935/1000, 907.5/1000, 885/1000

$$
\text{MTTF} \approx 100 \times \left(\frac{50 \cdot 975 + 150 \cdot 935 + 250 \cdot 907.5 + 350 \cdot 885}{1000}\right) \times \frac{1}{1000}
$$

$$
\approx 100 \times \frac{48750 + 140250 + 226875 + 309750}{1000 \times 1000}
$$

$$
\approx 100 \times \frac{725625}{10^6} = 72.56 \text{ hours (rough estimate)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a more accurate MTTF, use cumulative failure data and the standard $\text{MTTF} = \sum t_i \cdot f(t_i) \cdot \Delta t$ formula in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Bathtub Curve (Mermaid Diagram)

The following Mermaid block renders a clear block-level functional architecture representing the three regions of the bathtub curve.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TIME T EQUALS ZERO] --> B[REGION 1 INFANT MORTALITY]
    B --> C[REGION 2 USEFUL LIFE]
    C --> D[REGION 3 WEAR OUT]
    D --> E[TIME T APPROACHES INFINITY]

    B --> B1[FAILURE RATE DECREASING DFR]
    B1 --> B2[CAUSE MANUFACTURING DEFECTS]
    B1 --> B3[ACTION BURN IN AND SCREENING]

    C --> C1[FAILURE RATE CONSTANT CFR]
    C1 --> C2[CAUSE RANDOM SHOCKS AND OVERLOADS]
    C1 --> C3[ACTION PREVENTIVE MAINTENANCE]

    D --> D1[FAILURE RATE INCREASING IFR]
    D1 --> D2[CAUSE WEAR FATIGUE CORROSION]
    D1 --> D3[ACTION SCHEDULED REPLACEMENT]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff
    style E fill:#D0021B,color:#fff
    style B fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
    style C fill:#7ED321,color:#fff
    style D fill:#BD10E0,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Failure Rate Function Flow (Conditional Probability Logic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[COMPONENT AT TIME T] --> B{HAS IT SURVIVED TO T}
    B -->|YES| C[APPLY CONDITIONAL PROBABILITY]
    B -->|NO| D[ALREADY FAILED REMOVE]
    C --> E[COMPUTE LAMBDA T]
    E --> F[F T DIVIDED BY R T]
    E --> G[NEGATIVE DR DT DIVIDED BY R T]
    F --> H[INSTANTANEOUS FAILURE RATE]
    G --> H

    style A fill:#50E3C2
    style H fill:#F5A623,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Weibull Shape Parameter Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CHOOSE WEIBULL SHAPE BETA] --> B{BETA VALUE}
    B -->|BETA LESS THAN 1| C[INFANT MORTALITY DFR]
    B -->|BETA EQUALS 1| D[USEFUL LIFE CFR EXPONENTIAL]
    B -->|BETA GREATER THAN 1| E[WEAR OUT IFR]

    C --> C1[CURVE CONCAVE DOWN DECAYING]
    D --> D1[HORIZONTAL LINE CONSTANT]
    E --> E1[CURVE CONCAVE UP GROWING]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#fff

```

## 4.4 Relationship Between R(t), F(t), f(t), and λ(t)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[RELIABILITY R T] -->|1 MINUS R T| B[CDF F T]
    A -->|DIFFERENTIATE| C[PDF F T]
    B -->|DIFFERENTIATE| C
    C -->|DIVIDE BY R T| D[FAILURE RATE LAMBDA T]
    A -->|INTEGRATE LAMBDA| D
    D -->|INTEGRATE EXP| A

    style A fill:#7ED321,color:#fff

```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic**: Think of the bathtub curve as a literal bathtub — water (failure rate) is high at the **drain (infant mortality)**, flat in the **middle of the tub (useful life)**, and high at the **overflow edge (wear-out)**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term **"failure rate"** and state its units.

### Model Answer (3 marks):
The **failure rate** $\lambda(t)$ is the *instantaneous rate at which a component fails*, conditional on its survival up to time $t$. Mathematically:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Units**: Failures per unit time (commonly **failures/hour** or **FIT** = Failures In Time = $1 \times 10^{-9}$ failures/hour). For a constant failure rate $\lambda$, the **reliability** is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for units)*

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Differentiate between **MTTF** and **MTBF**.

### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | MTTF | MTBF |
|--------|------|------|
| **Full form** | Mean Time To Failure | Mean Time Between Failures |
| **Applies to** | Non-repairable items (e.g., bulbs, fuses) | Repairable systems (e.g., machines, vehicles) |
| **Includes repair time** | No | Yes (MTBF = MTTF + MTTR) |
| **Formula (constant λ)** | $\text{MTTF} = 1/\lambda$ | $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ (downtime excluded) |

For a **constant failure rate**, both equal $1/\lambda$ (in the absence of repair downtime for MTBF).

*(Award: 1.5 marks each for MTTF and MTBF distinction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]** 
> **(a)** Derive the relationship between the **reliability function** $R(t)$ and the **failure rate** $\lambda(t)$. Show that for a constant failure rate $\lambda$, the reliability follows an **exponential distribution**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.  
> **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A complex system has 3 independent components in series with constant failure rates $\lambda_1 = 0.001$/hr, $\lambda_2 = 0.002$/hr, and $\lambda_3 = 0.003$/hr. Calculate:  
> (i) The system failure rate  
> (ii) The reliability at 100 hours  
> (iii) The MTTF of the system  
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1** — Define failure rate via conditional probability (1 mark):
$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t \mid T \geq t)}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply Bayes' rule to convert conditional to joint probability (1 mark):
$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \leq T < t+\Delta t)}{P(T \geq t) \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 3** — Write $f(t) = -dR/dt$ (1 mark):
$$
\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4** — Separate variables and integrate (1 mark):
$$
\lambda(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR}{R}
$$

$$
\int_0^t \lambda(u) \, du = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0)
$$

**Step 5** — With $R(0) = 1$ and $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant) (1 mark):
$$
\lambda t = -\ln R(t)
$$

**Step 6** — Final result (2 marks):
$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}
$$

This is the **exponential reliability function**.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**(i) System failure rate (series configuration)** (2 marks):

For components in series, the system reliability is:
$$
R_s(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdot R_3(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_3 t} = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3) t}
$$

The system failure rate (sum of individual rates):
$$
\boxed{\lambda_s = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 = 0.001 + 0.002 + 0.003 = 0.006 \text{ per hour}}
$$

**(ii) Reliability at $t = 100$ hours** (2.5 marks):
$$
R_s(100) = e^{-0.006 \times 100} = e^{-0.6}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_s(100) = 0.5488 \text{ (or 54.88%)}}
$$

**(iii) MTTF of the system** (2.5 marks):
$$
\text{MTTF}_s = \frac{1}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1}{0.006}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF}_s = 166.67 \text{ hours}}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Bathtub Curve** in detail. Describe the three regions and the corresponding failure behavior, giving **two engineering examples** for each region.  
> **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** The time-to-failure of a device follows the **Weibull distribution** with scale parameter $\eta = 500$ hours and shape parameter $\beta = 2$. Calculate:  
> (i) The reliability at $t = 200$ hours  
> (ii) The failure rate at $t = 200$ hours  
> (iii) The MTTF  
> **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Bathtub Curve Explanation (7 marks)

**Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in) — DFR (2 marks)**

- **Behavior**: $\lambda(t)$ is **decreasing** with time
- **Cause**: Manufacturing defects, material impurities, workmanship errors, inadequate quality control
- **Examples**: 
  - New IC chips failing due to bonding defects in first 48 hours
  - Newly commissioned CNC machine developing bearing issues in the first 100 hours

**Region II — Useful Life — CFR (2.5 marks)**

- **Behavior**: $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ **constant** (lowest, flat region)
- **Cause**: Random shocks, overloading, environmental stress, human error
- **Examples**:
  - Aircraft engine failures due to bird strikes
  - Sudden power surge damaging a motor winding during normal operation

**Region III — Wear-out — IFR (2.5 marks)**

- **Behavior**: $\lambda(t)$ **increases** (often exponentially)
- **Cause**: Mechanical wear, fatigue, corrosion, erosion, aging
- **Examples**:
  - Brake pad replacement after 50,000 km
  - Transformer oil breakdown after 20 years

---

#### Part (b) — Weibull Distribution Problem (7 marks)

**Given**: $\eta = 500$ hours, $\beta = 2$, $t = 200$ hours

**(i) Reliability at $t = 200$ hours** (2.5 marks):
$$
R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}
$$

$$
R(200) = e^{-(200/500)^2} = e^{-(0.4)^2} = e^{-0.16}
$$

$$
\boxed{R(200) = 0.8521 \text{ (or 85.21%)}}
$$

**(ii) Failure rate at $t = 200$ hours** (2.5 marks):
$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}
$$

$$
\lambda(200) = \frac{2}{500} \left(\frac{200}{500}\right)^{2-1} = \frac{2}{500} \times 0.4 = 0.0016
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda(200) = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per hour}}
$$

**(iii) MTTF for Weibull distribution** (2 marks):

The general formula is:
$$
\text{MTTF} = \eta \cdot \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)
$$

For $\beta = 2$:
$$
\Gamma\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \Gamma(1.5) = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = \frac{1.7725}{2} = 0.8862
$$

$$
\text{MTTF} = 500 \times 0.8862 = 443.1 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MTTF} \approx 443.1 \text{ hours}}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing PDF with failure rate** — $f(t)$ is the *unconditional* density; $\lambda(t)$ is the *conditional* rate. Writing $\lambda(t) = f(t)$ is a direct **1-mark penalty**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting that MTTF = 1/λ ONLY for constant failure rate** — For Weibull with $\beta \neq 1$, this formula is WRONG. Use $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
> 
> 3. **Adding failure rates incorrectly** — For **parallel** systems, you CANNOT add $\lambda$'s. The series rule $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ applies **only to series configuration**.
> 
> 4. **Mixing MTTF with MTBF in repairable systems** — If a system is repaired after failure, the time between failures includes MTTR. Students often write MTBF = MTTF (incorrect) instead of MTBF = MTTF + MTTR.
> 
> 5. **Units of failure rate** — Writing "per hour" is correct; writing just "λ" without units loses a mark.
> 
> 6. **Skipping conditional probability** — A common 2-mark deduction: not stating that $\lambda(t)$ is the rate **given survival to time $t$**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Failure Rate (Module 1)**

### Core Definitions
- ✅ Failure rate $\lambda(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — conditional, instantaneous
- ✅ Reliability $R(t) = e^{-\int \lambda \, dt}$ — survives to time $t$
- ✅ CDF $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ — fails by time $t$
- ✅ PDF $f(t) = dF/dt = -dR/dt$

### Key Numerical Relationships (Constant λ)
- ✅ $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- ✅ $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential PDF)
- ✅ MTTF = MTBF = $1/\lambda$
- ✅ Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)

### The Bathtub Curve — Three Regions
- ✅ **Region I (Infant Mortality)**: DFR → burn-in, screening
- ✅ **Region II (Useful Life)**: CFR → preventive maintenance
- ✅ **Region III (Wear-out)**: IFR → scheduled replacement

### Weibull Distribution — The Universal Model
- ✅ $\beta < 1$: DFR (infant mortality)
- ✅ $\beta = 1$: CFR (exponential / random)
- ✅ $\beta > 1$: IFR (wear-out)
- ✅ MTTF = $\eta \cdot \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$

### System Configurations (Series)
- ✅ Series system: $\lambda_s = \sum \lambda_i$ and $R_s = \prod R_i$
- ✅ For independent components, $R(t_1 + t_2) = R(t_1) \cdot R(t_2)$

### Common Units to Remember
- ✅ **FPMH**: Failures Per Million Hours
- ✅ **FIT**: Failures In Time = $10^{-9}$ failures/hour
- ✅ **MTBF/MTTF/MTTR**: All in hours (typically)

### KTU Exam Golden Rules
- ✅ Always state the **conditional** nature of $\lambda(t)$
- ✅ Always quote **units** with numerical answers
- ✅ Always show the **intermediate derivation steps**
- ✅ For series systems, **sum the failure rates**
- ✅ For Weibull problems, identify $\beta$ to determine region

> **Final Note**: Failure rate is the **backbone** of reliability engineering and maintenance management. A solid grasp of this concept enables the student to analyze equipment downtime, plan preventive maintenance schedules, compute spares inventory, and design redundancy — all critical skills for an industry-ready maintenance engineer as per the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
