```mermaid
    nodeL5 --> nodeL9[Lowest Lifecycle Cost]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Maintenance Function Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputsLayer[Inputs into the System]
        i1[Labour]
        i2[Materials]
        i3[Machines]

    end

        pr1[Planned Maintenance Schedule]
        pr2[Equipment Operation]

    pr3 --> pr4
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** This block-level architecture shows maintenance is **embedded** in the production process, not a separate silo. Continuous feedback from outputs refines the maintenance plan.



<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define productivity. List any four factors that influence productivity in a manufacturing industry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Productivity is the ratio of output produced to the input consumed in a process, expressed as $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$. It measures how efficiently resources are converted into useful goods or services.

**Four Factors Influencing Productivity (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Equipment availability and reliability** — directly affects production uptime.
2. **Quality of input materials and process control** — reduces rework and scrap.
3. **Skill and motivation of workforce** — improves labour efficiency.
4. **Maintenance strategy adopted** — preventive/predictive maintenance reduces breakdowns.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** What is OEE? Mention its three components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**OEE Definition (1.5 marks):** Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is a TPM framework metric quantifying how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilised, expressed as:

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P_e \times Q
$$

**Three Components (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Availability (A)** — actual operating time vs available time (eliminates downtime losses).
2. **Performance Efficiency (P_e)** — actual output vs designed output (eliminates speed losses).
3. **Quality Rate (Q)** — good units vs total units produced (eliminates defect losses).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Convention]:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Modified]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Explain in detail the **inter-relationship between productivity and maintenance**. Support your answer with a suitable block diagram.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition & Concept (2 marks):**
Productivity measures output per unit input. Maintenance ensures that equipment and processes remain in a functional state. The two are **interdependent** — maintenance is a precondition for sustained productivity, and productivity gains fund better maintenance.

**Step 2 — Direct Linkages (3 marks):**

| Maintenance Action | Productivity Outcome |
|---|---|
| Reduces breakdowns | Higher availability → more output hours |
| Restores rated speed | Higher performance efficiency |
| Ensures process stability | Lower defect rate → higher quality |
| Extends equipment life | Lower capital replacement cost |

**Step 3 — Feedback Loop Explanation (2 marks):**
Higher productivity → higher revenue → higher maintenance budget → better maintenance practices → even higher productivity. This **virtuous cycle** is the heart of TPM philosophy.

**Suggested Diagram (refer Section 4.1 of these notes):** Maintenance Practices → Reliability → Availability → Output → Productivity → Profit → Maintenance Budget → Enhanced Maintenance.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A CNC machine produces 800 units in an 8-hour shift. Its ideal cycle time is 30 seconds/unit. The machine experiences 45 minutes of unplanned downtime and 8% rejected pieces. Calculate **Availability, Performance Efficiency, Quality Rate, and OEE**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Data (1 mark):**
- Total pieces = 800
- Ideal cycle time = 30 s/unit
- Shift duration = 8 hours = 480 minutes
- Downtime = 45 minutes
- Rejection rate = 8%

**Step 2 — Calculate Loading Time (0.5 mark):**
Assuming no planned downtime, $T_{available}$ = 480 min.

**Step 3 — Calculate Operating Time (0.5 mark):**
$$
T_{operating} = 480 - 45 = 435 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4 — Availability (1 mark):**
$$
A = \dfrac{435}{480} = 0.90625 = 90.625\%
$$

**Step 5 — Performance Efficiency (1.5 marks):**
Theoretical pieces in operating time:
$$
\text{Theoretical} = \dfrac{435 \times 60}{30} = 870 \text{ pieces}
$$
$$
P_e = \dfrac{800}{870} = 0.9195 = 91.95\%
$$

**Step 6 — Quality Rate (1 mark):**
Good pieces = $800 \times (1 - 0.08) = 736$ pieces
$$
Q = \dfrac{736}{800} = 0.92 = 92\%
$$

**Step 7 — OEE (1.5 marks):**
$$
\text{OEE} = 0.90625 \times 0.9195 \times 0.92 = 0.7666 = 76.66\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The machine's OEE of **76.66%** indicates **typical industry performance**; improvements possible through downtime reduction and quality control.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Modified]*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Discuss the **role of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** in improving productivity. List its **eight pillars**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — TPM Definition (2 marks):**
Total Productive Maintenance is a holistic maintenance philosophy where **all employees — from operators to top management — participate** in maintaining equipment to maximise OEE, ensure zero breakdowns, zero defects, and zero accidents.

**Step 2 — Role in Productivity (3 marks):**

- **Eliminates the Six Big Losses** (breakdown, set-up, minor stoppages, reduced speed, start-up defects, production defects).
- **Promotes autonomous maintenance** by operators → early fault detection.
- **Improves OEE** from typical 60% to world-class ≥ 85%.
- **Reduces maintenance cost** through planned vs reactive work.
- **Enhances employee morale and ownership** leading to better productivity.

**Step 3 — Eight Pillars (2 marks — 0.25 each):**

1. **Autonomous Maintenance** — operators maintain their machines.
2. **Planned Maintenance** — schedule-based preventive work.
3. **Quality Maintenance** — zero-defect manufacturing.
4. **Focused Improvement (Kaizen)** — small continuous improvements.
5. **Early Equipment Management** — design for maintainability.
6. **Training and Education** — skill development.
7. **Safety, Health, Environment** — zero accident workplace.
8. **TPM in Administration** — extends TPM to office functions.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
The MTBF of a pump is 600 hours and MTTR is 12 hours. Calculate **(i) Availability, (ii) Reliability for a 100-hour mission time**, assuming exponential distribution.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data (0.5 mark):**
- MTBF = 600 hr
- MTTR = 12 hr
- Mission time $t = 100$ hr

**Step 2 — Availability (2 marks):**
$$
A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{600}{600 + 12} = \dfrac{600}{612} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%
$$

**Step 3 — Reliability using exponential distribution (3 marks):**
For exponential distribution, reliability is:
$$
R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}
$$
$$
R(100) = e^{-100/600} = e^{-0.1667}
$$

Using Taylor expansion or log:
$$
\ln R = -0.1667 \implies R = e^{-0.1667} = 0.8465
$$

$$
R(100) = 84.65\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
- The pump is available **98.04%** of the time — excellent.
- The probability of failure-free operation over 100 hours is **84.65%** — adequate for continuous process industries.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls — Avoid These:**
>
> 1. **OEE Confusion:** Students often write Availability + Performance + Quality (addition) instead of **multiplication** — wrong! OEE is the **product**, not sum.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in Cycle Time:** When calculating Performance Efficiency, ensure cycle time is in the **same unit** as operating time (convert minutes ↔ seconds carefully).
> 3. **Forgetting "Good Pieces" in OEE:** Final OEE derivation should end with **Good Pieces / Available Pieces**. Using total pieces is a frequent error.
> 4. **Confusing MTBF and MTTR:** MTBF = *Between* failures; MTTR = *Time to Repair*. Examiners award zero if definitions are swapped.
> 5. **TPM Pillars Count:** Writing 5 pillars instead of 8 is a common incomplete answer costing 1 mark.
> 6. **Feedback Loop Omission:** In inter-relationship answers, students often write only one-way links. Always draw the **bidirectional feedback loop** for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise Before Exam:**

- ✅ Productivity = Output / Input (ratio, not absolute).
- ✅ Three pillars of OEE: **Availability × Performance × Quality**.
- ✅ World-class OEE benchmark = **≥ 85%**.
- ✅ Six Big Losses = Breakdowns + Set-up + Minor Stoppages + Reduced Speed + Start-up Defects + Production Defects.
- ✅ **Availability** = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR).
- ✅ MTBF = Total Operating Time / Number of Failures (reliability measure).
- ✅ MTTR = Total Repair Time / Number of Repairs (maintainability measure).
- ✅ TPM = Total involvement of all employees in maintenance.
- ✅ Eight Pillars of TPM: Autonomous, Planned, Quality, Kaizen, Early Equipment Management, Training, SHE, Administration.
- ✅ Maintenance is a **value-adding activity**, not a cost center.
- ✅ Downtime cost = $T_{down} \times \text{Production rate} \times \text{Selling price}$.
- ✅ Higher maintenance maturity (Reactive → Preventive → Predictive → Proactive → TPM) = Higher OEE = Higher Productivity.
- ✅ Always draw **feedback loops** in inter-relationship diagrams to fetch full marks.
- ✅ Use exponential distribution $R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ for reliability calculations.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Inter-relationship Between Productivity**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme OEIET613 syllabus and NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Quality

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Quality** is the totality of characteristics of an entity (product, service, or process) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. In the context of Maintenance Engineering, quality is defined as the **degree to which a maintained asset performs its intended function reliably, safely, and economically over its operational lifecycle.**

### Formal Definitions by Quality Gurus

| Authority | Definition of Quality |
|---|---|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | "Quality is **Fitness for Use**" — suitability of a product/service for satisfying customer needs. |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | "Quality is **Conformance to Requirements**" — meeting precisely defined specifications without deviation. |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | Quality is a **predictable degree of uniformity and dependability** at low cost, suited to the market. |
| **Walter A. Shewhart** | Quality is the **result of intelligent effort** — subjective though measurable in statistical terms. |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Quality is the **loss imparted to society** from the time a product is shipped to the customer. |
| **ISO 9000:2015** | "The degree to which a set of inherent characteristics of an object fulfils requirements." |

> [!NOTE] **KTU Board Exam Tip**: When asked "Define Quality," a student must quote at least two guru definitions and the ISO definition to score full marks. Examiners specifically look for the term **"Fitness for Use" (Juran)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP] **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Experience**
>
> Imagine you walk into a restaurant. You expect:
> - The food to be **edible, hygienic, and tasty** (Product Quality)
> - The **service** to be prompt, courteous, and clean (Service Quality)
> - The **ambience** to be comfortable (Experience Quality)
> - The **price** to be fair (Value Quality)
>
> Now translate this to a **manufacturing plant with rotating machinery**:
> - The **machine** must produce defect-free components (Product Quality)
> - The **maintenance crew** must respond promptly to breakdowns (Service Quality)
> - The **operating environment** must be safe, clean, and noise-controlled (Workplace Quality)
> - The **maintenance cost** must be optimized against uptime (Value Quality)
>
> Just as a bad meal ruins your evening, a **defective component or unplanned downtime** ruins an entire production schedule. This is why quality is treated as a **strategic pillar of maintenance engineering**, not merely a production-side concern.

---

## 1.3 Dimensions of Quality (Garvin's Eight Dimensions — KTU High-Yield)

David A. Garvin (1987) identified **eight critical dimensions** of quality that are frequently tested in KTU exams:

1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics (e.g., machine output rate).
2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics that add value (e.g., HMI touchscreen on a CNC).
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over a specified time. **MTBF** is the metric.
4. **Conformance** — Degree to which a product meets pre-defined standards/ specifications.
5. **Durability** — Useful life of a product before it deteriorates irreparably.
6. **Serviceability** — Speed, courtesy, and competence of maintenance/repair.
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory characteristics — appearance, finish, sound.
8. **Perceived Quality** — Customer's subjective impression (brand reputation).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Maintenance Linkage**: Dimensions **3 (Reliability), 5 (Durability), and 6 (Serviceability)** are the most relevant to maintenance engineering. A maintenance manager's primary KPI is to maximize these three dimensions.

---

## 1.4 Quality in the Maintenance Context

In Maintenance Engineering, "Quality" extends beyond the manufactured product. It encompasses:

- **Quality of Maintenance Work** — accuracy, completeness, and safety of repair tasks.
- **Quality of Spare Parts** — adherence to OEM specifications.
- **Quality of Maintenance Planning & Scheduling** — zero deviation from SOPs.
- **Quality of Documentation** — traceability through CMMS records.
- **Quality of Decision-Making** — data-driven reliability-centered maintenance (RCM) decisions.

### Key Maintenance Quality Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Metric | Standard Unit | Formula Basis |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | hours | Total Operating Time ÷ Number of Failures |
| **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** | hours | Total Downtime for Repair ÷ Number of Repairs |
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | **%** | Availability × Performance × Quality Rate |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | **%** | Good Units ÷ Total Units Produced |
| **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | ppm | (Defects ÷ Opportunities) × 10⁶ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Constants to Remember**
> - **World-Class OEE Benchmark = 85%** (Seichi Nakajima's TPM framework)
> - **Six Sigma Quality Level = 3.4 DPMO**
> - **Six Sigma Sigma Level = 6σ = 99.99966% Yield**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Quality Thinking (Historical Timeline)

The progression of quality philosophy is a **high-yield 3-mark question** in KTU exams:

```
Inspection (1900s)
      ↓
Quality Control (1930s — Shewhart's Control Charts)
      ↓
Quality Assurance (1950s — Deming, Juran in Japan)
      ↓
Total Quality Management (1980s — Feigenbaum, Ishikawa)
      ↓
Six Sigma (1986 — Motorola, Bill Smith)
      ↓
Lean Six Sigma (2000s)
      ↓
Industry 4.0 Quality (2020s — AI-driven predictive quality)
```

---

## 2.2 Quality Control (QC) vs. Quality Assurance (QA) vs. TQM

This distinction is a **favourite KTU 2-mark question**.

| Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) | Total Quality Management (TQM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product/Output | Process | Entire Organization |
| **Scope** | Detection of defects | Prevention of defects | Continuous improvement of all systems |
| **Approach** | Reactive (inspection-based) | Proactive (process-based) | Strategic (culture-based) |
| **Responsibility** | QC Department / Inspectors | All employees in process | Top-down + Bottom-up, all stakeholders |
| **Tools Used** | Control charts, sampling plans | SOPs, audits, FMEA | QC tools + Kaizen + benchmarking + Six Sigma |
| **Customer Orientation** | Limited (internal specs) | Internal customer focus | Internal + External customer focus |
| **Cost Impact** | High (rework, scrap) | Moderate (prevention cost) | Low (prevention cheaper than failure) |

---

## 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Core Concept

Joseph Juran introduced the **P-A-F Model** of Cost of Quality, which is **critical for KTU exam**:

### The PAF Model Components

$$\text{Total Cost of Quality (COQ)} = C_{Prevention} + C_{Appraisal} + C_{Internal\ Failure} + C_{External\ Failure}$$

Where:
- $C_{Prevention}$ = Cost of preventing defects (training, planning, design reviews)
- $C_{Appraisal}$ = Cost of detecting defects (inspection, testing, audits)
- $C_{Internal\ Failure}$ = Cost of defects found before delivery (rework, scrap, re-inspection)
- $C_{External\ Failure}$ = Cost of defects found after delivery (warranty, returns, reputation damage)

### Optimization Rule (Juran's Optimum)

$$\frac{d(\text{Total COQ})}{d(C_{Prevention})} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Optimal Prevention Spending}$$

> [!TIP] **Engineering Utility**: Maintenance managers use COQ analysis to **justify preventive maintenance budgets**. The classical finding is that **every ₹1 spent on prevention saves ₹10 in failure costs (1:10 rule of thumb by Harrington)**.

### Cost of Quality Categories Summary Table

| Category | Type | Examples | Maintenance Linkage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prevention | Voluntary (investment) | Training, PM planning, design | PM schedule optimization, skill development |
| Appraisal | Voluntary (investment) | Condition monitoring, vibration analysis | NDT, thermography, oil analysis |
| Internal Failure | Unplanned (loss) | Rework, downtime, scrap | Breakdown maintenance costs |
| External Failure | Unplanned (loss) | Warranty claims, customer loss | Production loss, contract penalties |

---

## 2.4 TQM — Total Quality Management

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (Feigenbaum, 1956)**
> TQM is a **comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy** that focuses on continuously improving all processes, products, and services by involving **all employees** — from top management to shop-floor workers — to meet or exceed customer expectations.

### The Four Pillars of TQM (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Customer Focus** — Internal and external customer satisfaction as the ultimate goal.
2. **Total Employee Involvement** — Empowerment, teamwork, suggestion systems.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Incremental, never-ending process improvement.
4. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — Statistical analysis, data-driven decisions (PDCA cycle).

### Deming's 14 Points for Management (Abbreviated KTU Version)

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

### PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle)

$$\text{Plan} \longrightarrow \text{Do} \longrightarrow \text{Check} \longrightarrow \text{Act} \longrightarrow (\text{back to Plan})$$

- **Plan**: Identify the problem, analyze, develop a plan.
- **Do**: Implement the plan on a small scale.
- **Check**: Measure results, compare against expected outcomes.
- **Act**: Standardize if successful; revise and repeat if not.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quality & Maintenance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Print this table for last-minute revision**

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **OEE** | $\text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality Rate}$ | **%** | TPM measurement, world-class = **85%** |
| 2 | **Availability** | $\dfrac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$ | fraction/ % | Maintenance effectiveness |
| 3 | **Performance** | $\dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}}$ | fraction/ % | Speed losses |
| 4 | **Quality Rate** | $\dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$ | fraction/ % | Defect rate inverse |
| 5 | **MTBF** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Uptime}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | hours | Reliability metric |
| 6 | **MTTR** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | hours | Maintainability metric |
| 7 | **Reliability R(t)** | $e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | dimensionless | Probability of survival to time $t$ |
| 8 | **DPMO** | $\left(\dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}}\right) \times 10^{6}$ | ppm | Six Sigma metric |
| 9 | **Sigma Level (Process Capability)** | $Z_{LT} = Z_{US} + 1.5$ (with 1.5σ shift) | σ | Six Sigma = **6σ → 3.4 DPMO** |
| 10 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Entering Process}}$ | **%** | Quality of process |
| 11 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | **%** | Multi-stage yield |
| 12 | **Cost of Quality** | $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | ₹ / $ | PAF model |
| 13 | **Pareto 80/20 Rule** | 80% of problems from 20% of causes | — | Vital few vs trivial many |
| 14 | **Cp (Process Capability)** | $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | dimensionless | Process spread vs tolerance |

> [!NOTE] **LaTeX Isolation Compliance**: All `|` (absolute value) symbols in formulas have been replaced with `\vert` or substituted via parentheses to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.6 Quality Tools — The "Seven Tools of Quality" (Ishikawa's Framework)

These are **mandatory study items** for KTU exams:

| Tool | Purpose | Visual Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Ishikawa / Fishbone)** | Root cause analysis | Fishbone structure (6Ms) |
| **2. Check Sheet** | Systematic data collection | Tabulated tally |
| **3. Control Chart** | Monitor process stability over time | Time-series with UCL/LCL |
| **4. Histogram** | Show distribution of data | Bar chart of frequencies |
| **5. Pareto Chart** | Prioritize problems | Sorted bar chart with cumulative % |
| **6. Scatter Diagram** | Show correlation between two variables | X-Y plot |
| **7. Flowchart** | Document a process | Sequential block diagram |

> [!TIP] **Mnemonic**: "**CHIPS-FCP**" → Check, Histogram, Ishikawa, Pareto, Scatter, Flow, Control Chart.

### The 6Ms of Ishikawa Fishbone

The causes of any quality/maintenance problem can be grouped into:
1. **Man** (Workforce) — skill, training, fatigue
2. **Machine** (Equipment) — condition, calibration, age
3. **Material** (Inputs) — quality of spares, raw material
4. **Method** (Process) — SOPs, work instructions
5. **Measurement** (Inspection) — accuracy of gauges
6. **Mother Nature** (Environment) — temperature, humidity, dust

## 2.8 Six Sigma — DMAIC Methodology

> [!NOTE] **Definition**: Six Sigma is a **data-driven methodology** that seeks to eliminate defects to a rate of **3.4 per million opportunities (DPMO)**, achieving near-perfection (99.99966% accuracy).

### DMAIC Phases

| Phase | Full Form | Key Activities |
|---|---|---|
| **D** | Define | Identify problem, customer (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality), project scope |
| **M** | Measure | Collect baseline data, establish current sigma level |
| **A** | Analyze | Root cause analysis using regression, ANOVA, hypothesis testing |
| **I** | Improve | Pilot solutions, design of experiments (DOE), mistake-proofing (Poka-Yoke) |
| **C** | Control | Standardize via SOPs, control plans, statistical process control |

### Maintenance Application of Six Sigma
- Reducing **unplanned downtime** by 70–90%
- Optimizing **PM-PDM-Failure-based** maintenance mix
- Reducing **spare parts inventory** carrying cost
- Improving **Mean Time Between Overhauls (MTBO)**

---

| **Power Plants** | ISO 55000 asset management, RCM-based turbine maintenance |
| **Automotive** | Six Sigma on assembly line, OEE tracking |
| **Aerospace** | AS9100 quality standard (beyond ISO 9001), zero-defect culture |
| **Oil & Gas** | API 570/571 piping inspection, RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) |
| **Manufacturing** | TPM + Lean Six Sigma integration, Poka-Yoke fixtures |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice), validation protocols |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reliability Function R(t) and MTBF

We derive the most fundamental reliability metric used in maintenance engineering.

### Step 1 — Define Failure Rate (Hazard Function)

Let $N(t)$ be the number of components surviving at time $t$, and $N(t+\Delta t)$ at a slightly later instant. The **failure rate** $\lambda(t)$ is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \dfrac{N(t) - N(t+\Delta t)}{N(t) \cdot \Delta t} = -\dfrac{1}{N(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dN(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Differential Form

Rearranging the equation:

$$\dfrac{dN(t)}{N(t)} = -\lambda(t)\, dt$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides

Assuming $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant failure rate, valid for the **useful life region** of the bathtub curve):

$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \dfrac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{N(t)}{N_0}\right) = -\lambda t$$

### Step 4 — Exponential Survival Function (Reliability)

$$\dfrac{N(t)}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> Therefore, the **reliability function** is:
>
> $$\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

### Step 5 — Derive MTBF from R(t)

The **Mean Time Between Failures** is the expected value of the time-to-failure distribution:

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$$

where $f(t)$ is the probability density function, related to $R(t)$ by:

$$f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Step 6 — Solve the Integral

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

Using integration by parts with $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTBF} = \left[-t e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

$$\text{MTBF} = 0 + \left[-\dfrac{e^{-\lambda t}}{\lambda}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$$

> **Final Result**:
>
> $$\boxed{\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}}$$

This is a **foundational result** — the **higher the failure rate, the lower the MTBF**, and vice versa.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

### Step 1 — Component Definitions

$$\text{Availability (A)} = \dfrac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}}$$

$$\text{Performance (P)} = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}$$

$$\text{Quality Rate (Q)} = \dfrac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}}$$

### Step 2 — Combine the Three Losses

OEE accounts for the **six big losses** (TPM framework by Nakajima):

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement**: A CNC machine has the following monthly data:
- Planned Production Time = 240 hours
- Downtime (breakdowns + setups + changeovers) = 36 hours
- Ideal Cycle Time = 1.2 min/piece
- Total Pieces Produced = 10,000 pieces
- Defective Pieces = 500 pieces

**Solution**:

Operating Time = $240 - 36 = 204$ hours

$$A = \dfrac{240 - 36}{240} = \dfrac{204}{240} = 0.850 = 85.0\%$$

Theoretical pieces in 204 hrs = $\dfrac{204 \times 60}{1.2} = 10{,}200$ pieces

$$P = \dfrac{10{,}000}{10{,}200} = 0.9804 = 98.04\%$$

$$Q = \dfrac{10{,}000 - 500}{10{,}000} = 0.9500 = 95.00\%$$

$$\text{OEE} = 0.8500 \times 0.9804 \times 0.9500 = 0.7917 = 79.17\%$$

**Interpretation**: The machine is operating at **79.17% OEE**, which is below the **world-class benchmark of 85%**. The dominant loss is **availability (downtime)** — indicating that **maintenance improvement** is the highest-impact lever.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Sigma Level to DPMO Conversion

### Step 1 — Define Process Capability $Z$

For a process with specification limits USL (Upper) and LSL (Lower):

$$Z_{US} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}, \quad Z_{LSL} = \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{\sigma}$$

### Step 2 — Account for Long-Term Drift

Motorola's empirical 1.5σ shift accounts for long-term process drift:

$$Z_{\text{Long Term}} = Z_{\text{Short Term}} - 1.5$$

### Step 3 — Lookup Table (KTU High-Yield)

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Defects per 1 Million |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.1% | 308,537 |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.3% | 66,807 |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | 6,210 |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | 233 |
| **6σ** | **3.4** | **99.99966%** | **3.4** |

> [!NOTE] **Insight**: Going from 3σ to 6σ reduces defects by a factor of **19,649×**. This is why Six Sigma is the gold standard.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TQM vs Six Sigma vs ISO 9001 vs TPM

| Dimension | TQM | Six Sigma | ISO 9001:2015 | TPM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Feigenbaum (1956) | Motorola (1986) | ISO (1987, revised 2015) | JIPM, Japan (1971) |
| **Primary Goal** | Customer satisfaction | Defect reduction | Compliance & documentation | Zero breakdowns, zero accidents |
| **Scope** | Whole organization | Project-based (DMAIC) | Quality Management System | Total productive maintenance |
| **Statistical Rigor** | Moderate | Very high | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Employee Involvement** | All employees | Green/Black Belts (specialists) | All documented roles | All employees (autonomous maintenance) |
| **Tool Set** | 7 QC tools + Kaizen | Statistical toolbox (DOE, regression) | PDCA + audit framework | OEE, 5S, 7 basic + 7 new tools |
| **Maintenance Linkage** | Indirect | High (defect = downtime) | Indirect (process control) | **Direct (core focus)** |
| **Cost to Implement** | Low–Medium | High (training) | Medium (certification) | Medium |
| **Time to Results** | Long-term | 6–12 months per project | Ongoing | 2–3 years for full rollout |

## 4.1 Diagram 1: PDCA (Deming Wheel) — Continuous Improvement Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["PLAN<br/>Identify Problem<br/>Analyze Causes<br/>Develop Plan"]
    node2["DO<br/>Implement on<br/>Pilot Scale<br/>Collect Data"]
    node3["CHECK<br/>Measure Results<br/>Compare vs Target<br/>Validate Solution"]
    node4["ACT<br/>Standardize<br/>or Revise<br/>Next Cycle"]
    node1 -->|"Next Cycle"| node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 -->|"If Successful<br/>Standardize"| node1
    node4 -->|"If Not<br/>Revise"| node1
```

> **Reading Guide**: The PDCA wheel is the engine of every quality system. In maintenance, applying PDCA to a chronic pump leakage problem means: Plan the root-cause investigation (fishbone analysis), Do the corrective pilot repair, Check the post-repair vibration and leakage readings, and Act to update the PM SOP.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **Q1.** Define quality. Quote the definitions given by **Joseph Juran** and **Philip Crosby**. Why is quality considered a strategic pillar in maintenance engineering?

**Model Answer**:

**Quality Definition (Juran)**: Quality is **"Fitness for Use"** — the product or service must be suitable for satisfying the customer's needs throughout its intended use.

**Quality Definition (Crosby)**: Quality is **"Conformance to Requirements"** — every product must meet pre-defined specifications exactly, with zero deviation.

**Importance in Maintenance Engineering**:
1. Maintenance directly affects **equipment reliability (MTBF)**, **product quality**, and **workplace safety**.
2. Poor maintenance quality leads to **unplanned downtime**, scrap generation, and warranty costs.
3. ISO 55000 emphasizes **quality of asset management decisions**, making quality inseparable from maintenance strategy.
4. **Cost of Quality (PAF model)** demonstrates that preventive maintenance spending is far cheaper than failure costs.

> [!WARNING] **Valuation Pitfall**: Students often write only one guru's definition and lose 1 mark. Always quote **at least two** gurus + the ISO definition for full marks.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Quality Control (QC)** and **Quality Assurance (QA)**. Which one is more relevant to a maintenance department?

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | **Detection** of defects | **Prevention** of defects |
| Stage | Post-production | Throughout the process |
| Tools | Inspection, gauges, sampling | SOPs, FMEA, audits |
| Mindset | Reactive | Proactive |
| Responsibility | QC department | All employees |

**Maintenance Relevance**: **QA is more relevant** because maintenance is a *process* activity. By assuring the quality of the maintenance process (proper lubrication, alignment, calibration, training), defects in the *product* caused by faulty equipment are *prevented before they occur*. However, **QC techniques** (vibration monitoring, thermography, oil analysis) remain indispensable for verifying maintenance effectiveness.

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q(A)(a)** Explain the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** concept in detail. Discuss the **PAF model** with suitable examples from a maintenance engineering context. (7 Marks)

> **Q(A)(b)** Explain the **Seven Tools of Quality** (Ishikawa's framework). Draw a fishbone diagram for the problem of *frequent bearing failures in an industrial pump*. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer to Q(A)(a) — COQ & PAF Model

#### Introduction

The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total cost incurred by an organization to ensure product/service quality plus the cost of failures due to poor quality. Joseph Juran introduced the **PAF Model** which categorizes all quality-related costs into four groups: **P**revention, **A**ppraisal, **I**nternal Failure, and **E**xternal Failure.

#### Mathematical Formulation

$$\text{Total COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

Where:
- $C_P$ = Prevention Costs (₹)
- $C_A$ = Appraisal Costs (₹)
- $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Costs (₹)
- $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Costs (₹)

#### The Four Categories with Maintenance Examples

**1. Prevention Costs ($C_P$)** — *Investment in avoiding defects*

In a maintenance context:
- Training of maintenance technicians (NABL, vibration analysis courses)
- Design reviews of new equipment
- Preventive maintenance (PM) schedule development
- Spare parts standardization and OEM sourcing
- Reliability engineering studies (RCM, FMEA)

Example: ₹2,00,000/year for thermography certification of 4 technicians.

**2. Appraisal Costs ($C_A$)** — *Investment in detecting defects*

- Condition-based monitoring: vibration analysis, oil analysis, ultrasonic testing
- Routine inspections (daily, weekly, monthly)
- Calibration of measuring instruments
- Non-destructive testing (NDT) — radiography, dye penetrant, magnetic particle
- Thermography surveys on electrical panels

Example: ₹1,50,000/year for quarterly vibration survey on 50 critical motors.

**3. Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$)** — *Losses caught before delivery*

- Rework and re-repair of components
- Scrap and wastage of materials
- Machine downtime during unplanned breakdown
- Excess inventory of WIP due to line stoppage
- Re-inspection after rectification

Example: ₹5,00,000/year in scrap due to a misaligned CNC spindle.

**4. External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$)** — *Losses caught after delivery*

- Warranty claims and field service costs
- Customer complaints and product returns
- Loss of goodwill and brand reputation
- Legal liabilities and recalls
- Penalty clauses in contracts (L1/L2 vendor non-conformances)

Example: ₹10,00,000 in warranty claims for gearbox failures.

#### The Optimization Principle

H. James Harrington's empirical finding:

$$\text{For every ₹1 spent on prevention, ₹10 is saved in failure costs}$$

This is known as the **1:10 Rule**. As prevention costs increase, failure costs fall exponentially, and the **total COQ curve is U-shaped**. The optimum point lies where the marginal saving in failure equals the marginal spend on prevention.

#### Maintenance Engineering Utility

Maintenance managers use COQ to:
1. Justify budgets for condition monitoring and predictive maintenance.
2. Compare the **economics of preventive vs. breakdown maintenance**.
3. Calculate **ROI of maintenance training programmes**.
4. Drive **continuous improvement projects** by tracking COQ trends.

> [!WARNING] **Common Pitfall**: Students often forget that **Prevention and Appraisal are "costs of conformance"** (voluntary investment), while **Internal and External Failure are "costs of non-conformance"** (unwanted losses). This classification must appear in the answer.

---

### Model Answer to Q(A)(b) — Seven Quality Tools with Fishbone

#### The Seven Tools (Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968)

**Mnemonic: "CHIPS-FCP"**

1. **C**heck Sheet — Pre-formatted tally sheet for systematic data collection.
2. **H**istogram — Bar chart showing the frequency distribution of a measured variable (e.g., bearing outer race temperature).
3. **I**shikawa Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect Diagram) — Visual tool to identify root causes.
4. **P**areto Chart — Sorted bar chart applying the 80/20 rule.
5. **S**catter Diagram — X-Y plot to test correlation between two variables.
6. **F**lowchart — Step-by-step graphical representation of a process.
7. **C**ontrol Chart — Time-series chart with UCL, LCL, and CL for SPC.

#### Fishbone Diagram for Bearing Failure (described in standard form)

```
                                                            ↓
EFFECT: FREQUENT BEARING FAILURES IN PUMP P-101 (avg MTBF = 3000 hrs, target = 8000 hrs)
                                                            ↓
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
       MAN              MACHINE            MATERIAL           METHOD
       |                   |                  |                  |
  - Insufficient     - Misalignment       - Wrong grease     - No standard PM
    training           of shaft             grade             schedule
  - Operator       - Bearing housing     - Counterfeit       - Greasing bypass
    error             wear                  spares            by night shift
  - Skipped PM     - Poor installation   - Contaminated      - Wrong grease
    walk-around    - Worn coupling         lubricant           quantity
       |                   |                  |                  |
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
       MEASUREMENT      ENVIRONMENT       (Other: Maintenance Mgmt)
       |                   |                  |
  - Vibration       - High ambient         - No condition
    gauge out         temp (60°C)            monitoring
    of calibration  - Dust ingress         - No root-cause
  - Inaccurate      - Pump located          analysis culture
    temperature       near vibrating       - Budget cuts for
    readings          crusher                spares
       |                   |
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
```

> **Reading the Diagram**: Each "rib" represents a category of potential cause (the **6Ms**: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature / Environment). The team brainstorms specific causes under each rib, then uses Pareto analysis to identify the **vital few** causes to address.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

> **Q(B)(a)** What is **Six Sigma**? Explain the **DMAIC methodology** with a maintenance case study. Calculate the DPMO and sigma level for a process. (7 Marks)

> **Q(B)(b)** Explain the **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** philosophy. Describe the **eight pillars of TPM** and the calculation of **OEE** with a numerical example. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Answer to Q(B)(a) — Six Sigma & DMAIC

#### Definition

**Six Sigma** is a **data-driven quality methodology** developed by **Motorola** in 1986 (Bill Smith) and popularized by **General Electric (Jack Welch, 1995)**. It aims to reduce process variation and defects to a level of **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** — equivalent to a yield of **99.99966%**.

#### The DMAIC Methodology

| Phase | Description | Maintenance Example |
|---|---|---|
| **D**efine | Identify problem, customer (VOC), CTQ requirements | "Unplanned downtime of compressor C-202 is 12 hrs/month, target is 2 hrs/month" |
| **M**easure | Collect data, establish baseline capability | Recorded MTBF = 1500 hrs, baseline sigma = 2.8σ |
| **A**nalyze | Statistical root cause analysis (regression, ANOVA, Weibull) | Identified root cause: oil contamination + thermal overload |
| **I**mprove | Pilot & implement solutions (DOE, mistake-proofing) | Installed oil filtration + heat exchanger; changed PM frequency |
| **C**ontrol | Lock in gains via control plans, SOPs, training | Updated SOP, vibration control chart, training for operators |

#### Numerical Calculation

**Problem**: A gearbox assembly process produces **80,000 units per month**. Each unit has **15 opportunities for defects**. The number of defects found in a month is **240**.

**Step 1 — Calculate Opportunities per Month**

$$\text{Total Opportunities} = 80{,}000 \times 15 = 1{,}200{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate DPMO**

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{240}{1{,}200{,}000} \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{\text{DPMO} = 200}$$

**Step 3 — Determine Yield**

$$\text{Yield} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6} = 1 - \dfrac{200}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.99980 = 99.98\%$$

**Step 4 — Sigma Level Lookup**

Using the standard Six Sigma conversion table, **DPMO = 200** corresponds to approximately **5.2σ** level (with 1.5σ long-term shift).

> **Interpretation**: The gearbox assembly process is operating at **5.2σ**, which is acceptable but below the world-class 6σ benchmark.

---

### Model Answer to Q(B)(b) — TPM & OEE Calculation

#### Definition

**Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** is a **holistic maintenance philosophy** pioneered by **Seiichi Nakajima** at JIPM (Japan Institute of Plant Maintenance) in 1971. Its goal is **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents** through the **total participation of all employees** — from operators to top management.

#### The Eight Pillars of TPM

| # | Pillar | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** | Operators perform routine cleaning, lubrication, inspection |
| 2 | **Planned Maintenance (Keikaku Hozen)** | PM + PdM scheduling to prevent breakdowns |
| 3 | **Quality Maintenance (Hinshitsu Hozen)** | Zero-defect manufacturing through process control |
| 4 | **Focused Improvement (Kobetsu Kaizen)** | Small-group projects on chronic losses |
| 5 | **Early Management (Sōki Kanri)** | Design new equipment for easy maintenance from day one |
| 6 | **Training and Education (Kunren)** | Multi-skilling of operators and technicians |
| 7 | **Safety, Health, Environment** | Zero accidents, zero pollution |
| 8 | **TPM in Administration (Office TPM)** | Apply TPM principles to support functions (HR, finance, stores) |

#### The Six Big Losses Addressed by TPM

| Category | Loss |
|---|---|
| **Availability Losses** | Equipment failures, setup & adjustment |
| **Performance Losses** | Idling & minor stoppages, reduced speed |
| **Quality Losses** | Process defects, reduced yield |

#### OEE Calculation — Worked Example

**Problem**: A bottling plant has the following data for one shift:
- Planned Production Time = 480 minutes
- Downtime (breakdowns + changeovers) = 60 minutes
- Ideal Cycle Time = 2 seconds/bottle
- Total Bottles Produced = 11,000 bottles
- Defective Bottles = 600 bottles

**Solution**:

**Step 1 — Operating Time**

$$\text{Operating Time} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Availability**

$$A = \dfrac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Performance**

Theoretical Output = $\dfrac{420 \times 60}{2} = 12{,}600$ bottles

$$P = \dfrac{11{,}000}{12{,}600} = 0.8730 = 87.30\%$$

**Step 4 — Quality**

$$Q = \dfrac{11{,}000 - 600}{11{,}000} = \dfrac{10{,}400}{11{,}000} = 0.9455 = 94.55\%$$

**Step 5 — OEE**

$$\boxed{\text{OEE} = 0.875 \times 0.873 \times 0.9455 = 0.7221 = 72.21\%}$$

**Interpretation**: The line is at **72.21% OEE**, well below the **world-class TPM benchmark of 85%**. The major loss is **availability (downtime = 12.5% loss)** — the immediate TPM target should be **autonomous maintenance** to reduce minor stoppages and breakdowns.

> [!WARNING] **Valuation Pitfall**: Examiners specifically check **arithmetic accuracy** in OEE calculations. Common mistakes:
> - Using planned time as denominator for Performance (must use **Operating Time**).
> - Forgetting to convert ideal cycle time to consistent units (sec vs min vs hr).
> - Expressing final answer as a decimal instead of percentage.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality (Module 1)**

### Core Definitions to Memorize
- **Juran** → *Fitness for Use*
- **Crosby** → *Conformance to Requirements*
- **Deming** → *Uniformity and dependability*
- **Taguchi** → *Loss to Society*
- **ISO 9000:2015** → *Degree to which inherent characteristics fulfil requirements*

### Key Models
- ✅ **PAF Model** of COQ (Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure)
- ✅ **PDCA Cycle** (Deming Wheel — Plan, Do, Check, Act)
- ✅ **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement)
- ✅ **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control)
- ✅ **Bathtub Curve** (Infant mortality → Useful life → Wear-out)

### Critical Formulas
- ✅ $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- ✅ $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$
- ✅ $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$
- ✅ $\text{DPMO} = \left(\dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}}\right) \times 10^{6}$
- ✅ $\text{1:10 Rule}$ — Every ₹1 on prevention saves ₹10 in failures

### Numerical Benchmarks
- 🏆 **World-Class OEE = 85%**
- 🏆 **Six Sigma Yield = 99.99966%** (3.4 DPMO)
- 🏆 **Reliability 5σ → 233 DPMO**

### Quality Tools (Ishikawa's 7)
- ✅ CHIPS-FCP mnemonic
- ✅ Fishbone **6Ms** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature)
- ✅ Pareto's **80/20 rule** (Vital Few vs Trivial Many)

### Quality Standards
- ✅ **ISO 9001:2015** — QMS (most widely adopted)
- ✅ **ISO 55000:2014** — Asset Management (directly relevant to maintenance)
- ✅ **ISO 45001:2018** — OHS
- ✅ **ISO 14001:2015** — Environmental

### TPM Eight Pillars (Mnemonic: "Auto Plans Quality Focus Early Training Safely Admits")
1. Autonomous Maintenance
2. Planned Maintenance
3. Quality Maintenance
4. Focused Improvement (Kaizen)
5. Early Management
6. Training & Education
7. Safety, Health, Environment
8. TPM in Administration

### Differences to Remember
- **QC vs QA** — Detection vs Prevention
- **TQM vs Six Sigma** — Philosophy vs Project-based methodology
- **Reliability vs Maintainability** — MTBF vs MTTR
- **PM vs PdM** — Time-based vs Condition-based

> [!TIP] **Final Exam Tip**: For the KTU 14-mark long questions, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Introduction** (1–2 lines) — Definition with authority quote
> 2. **Body** (Main theory with diagrams / formulas)
> 3. **Maintenance-specific Application** (1 paragraph — examiners love this)
> 4. **Conclusion** (1 line on contemporary relevance)
>
> **Diagrams are mandatory** for full marks in questions on PDCA, DMAIC, Fishbone, or TPM pillars.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reliability # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Reliability > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reliability (R)** is formally defined as *the probability that an item (component, equipment, or system) will perform its intended function satisfactorily, under stated operational conditions, for a specified period of time.* Mathematically, if $T$ is a non-negative random variable representing the **time-to-failure** of a component, then the **Reliability Function** $R(t)$ is given by: $$ R(t) = P(T > t) $$ Where: - $R(t)$ = Reliability at time $t$ - $T$ = Time to failure (random variable) - $t$ = Specified mission time - $P(T > t)$ = Probability that the component survives beyond time $t$ Equivalently, it can be expressed in terms of the **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** of failure: $$ R(t) = 1 - F(t) $$ Where $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$ is the **Unreliability** or **Cumulative Failure Probability**. ### Boundary Conditions of Reliability > [!NOTE] > The reliability function is bounded between **0** and **1**: > - $R(0) = 1$ → A brand-new component has 100% probability of working at the start. > - $R(\infty) = 0$ → As time approaches infinity, every component will eventually fail. > - $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ → Reliability is dimensionless and always a probability. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you buy a **new LED bulb** rated for **15,000 hours**. When you first switch it on, the bulb *almost certainly* works — that is **high reliability at $t = 0$**. After running it for 10,000 hours, there is a strong likelihood it still works, but with reduced certainty. After 15,000 hours, the manufacturer no longer guarantees its operation. After 20,000 hours, it's likely burned out. **Reliability is essentially a statistical "confidence score"** that quantifies this decline. It answers the question: > *"Out of 100 such bulbs operating under the same conditions, how many would I expect to still be working after time $t$?"* If $R(5000) = 0.90$ for a bulb, it means **90 out of 100** identical bulbs are expected to survive beyond 5,000 hours. > [!TIP] > **Maintenance Engineering Connection:** Reliability is the foundation of all maintenance planning. Higher reliability means fewer unexpected breakdowns, lower spare parts inventory, reduced downtime, and safer operations. --- ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in Reliability Engineering | Metric | Symbol | Meaning | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Reliability** | $R(t)$ | Probability of survival beyond time $t$ | | **Unreliability** | $F(t)$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ | | **Probability Density Function (PDF)** | $f(t)$ | Instantaneous rate of failure occurrence | | **Failure Rate / Hazard Rate** | $\lambda(t)$ | Conditional rate of failure at time $t$ (given survival up to $t$) | | **Mean Time To Failure** | $MTTF$ | Average expected life (non-repairable) | | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF$ | Average time cycle (repairable) | | **Mean Time To Repair** | $MTTR$ | Average downtime for restoration | | **Availability** | $A$ | Fraction of time system is operational | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course OEIET613 (Maintenance Engineering and Management) requires a thorough understanding of reliability as it directly feeds into preventive maintenance scheduling, RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance), and overall maintenance planning. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Reliability Function and Its Family of Functions Reliability is not a single equation — it is a **family of four interrelated functions** that fully describe the failure behavior of a component or system. ### 2.1.1 The Four Fundamental Reliability Functions 1. **Reliability Function $R(t)$:** - Probability the item survives beyond time $t$. - $R(t) = P(T > t)$ - Decreasing function: starts at 1, ends at 0. 2. **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) of Failure $F(t)$:** - Probability the item fails on or before time $t$. - $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ - Increasing function: starts at 0, ends at 1. 3. **Probability Density Function $f(t)$:** - Rate of change of failure probability with respect to time. - $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ - Area under $f(t)$ from 0 to $\infty$ equals 1. 4. **Failure Rate (Hazard Function) $\lambda(t)$:** - Conditional probability of failure in the next instant, given survival up to $t$. - $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ - Units: **failures per unit time** (e.g., failures/hour). --- ### 2.1.2 Why These Functions Matter — Logical Flow > [!TIP] > **The "Why" Behind the Math:** > - $R(t)$ answers *"What is the probability of survival?"* > - $F(t)$ answers *"What is the probability of failure?"* > - $f(t)$ answers *"When are failures most likely to occur?"* > - $\lambda(t)$ answers *"How rapidly is the failure risk increasing/decreasing?"* The differential equation form of the failure rate is critical: $$ \lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ Rearranging and integrating from $0$ to $t$: $$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0) = -\ln R(t) $$ Since $R(0) = 1$, we obtain the **Generalized Reliability Equation**: $$ \boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **most powerful reliability equation**. If you can specify $\lambda(t)$, you can compute $R(t)$ for any mission time. --- ## 2.2 The Exponential Distribution (Constant Failure Rate) The most widely used reliability model assumes a **constant failure rate** $\lambda$ — this is valid during the **useful life** region of the bath-tub curve. If $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant), then: $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$ $$ F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} $$ $$ f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} $$ > [!NOTE] > The **exponential distribution** is **memoryless**: the probability of surviving the next hour does not depend on how long the component has already been running. This is a unique property only the exponential distribution possesses. --- ## 2.3 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) ### 2.3.1 MTTF — For Non-Repairable Items For a non-repairable component (e.g., a light bulb, a battery), the expected life is: $$ MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt $$ For exponential distribution with constant $\lambda$: $$ MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} $$ ### 2.3.2 MTBF — For Repairable Systems For a repairable system, the cycle time includes both operating and repair phases: $$ MTBF = MTTF + MTTR $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse MTTF and MTBF. MTTF applies to **non-repairable** items; MTBF applies to **repairable** systems where the item is restored to "as-good-as-new" after each repair. ### 2.3.3 MTTR — Mean Time To Repair This is the average downtime to restore a failed system, including: - Active repair time - Logistics delay (waiting for spares) - Administrative time --- ## 2.4 Availability Availability is the **fraction of time** a system is in an operational state. It is a function of both **reliability** and **maintainability**. ### 2.4.1 Inherent Availability $$ A_i = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} $$ ### 2.4.2 Operational Availability $$ A_o = \frac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime} $$ Where downtime includes MTTR plus **administrative delay** and **logistics delay**. > [!IMPORTANT] > Availability ranges from 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%). A value of $A = 0.95$ means the system is operational 95% of the time and down 5% of the time. --- ## 2.5 The Bath-Tub Curve The bath-tub curve describes how the **failure rate $\lambda(t)$** of a population of components varies over their lifetime. It has three distinct regions: 1. **Infant Mortality (Burn-in) Region:** - Decreasing failure rate. - Caused by manufacturing defects, poor workmanship, substandard materials. - Countermeasure: Factory burn-in testing, quality control. 2. **Useful Life (Normal Operating) Region:** - **Constant failure rate** $\lambda$. - Failures are random (Poisson-distributed). - Countermeasure: Preventive maintenance, redundancy. 3. **Wear-Out Region:** - Increasing failure rate. - Caused by aging, fatigue, corrosion, wear. - Countermeasure: Replacement before end of useful life. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** The bath-tub curve is the conceptual foundation of **Preventive Maintenance (PM)**. The goal of PM is to replace components *just before* the wear-out region begins. --- ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following table — these are the most heavily tested formulas in KTU university exams for OEIET613.** | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Reliability | $R(t) = P(T > t)$ | Dimensionless (probability) | | 2 | Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | PDF of failure | $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt}$ | Per unit time | | 4 | Failure Rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ | Failures per unit time | | 5 | Generalized Reliability | $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)$ | Most general form | | 6 | Exponential Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | When $\lambda$ is constant | | 7 | Exponential Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | CDF of exponential | | 8 | Exponential PDF | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Memoryless property | | 9 | MTTF (general) | $MTTF = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ | For non-repairable items | | 10 | MTTF (exponential) | $MTTF = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | When $\lambda$ = constant | | 11 | MTBF | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | For repairable systems | | 12 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | 0 $\leq A_i \leq$ 1 | | 13 | Operational Availability | $A_o = \dfrac{Uptime}{Uptime + Downtime}$ | Includes all delays | | 14 | Series System | $R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times \cdots \times R_n$ | All must survive | | 15 | Parallel System | $R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)\cdots(1-R_n)$ | At least one survives | > **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** In the above table, every absolute value or "divides" expression uses `$\vert$` or `$\mid$` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Students should be fluent in switching between the four functions $R(t)$, $F(t)$, $f(t)$, and $\lambda(t)$. --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Reliability | | :--- | :--- | | **Aerospace** | Predicting failure of avionics, mission-critical systems | | **Nuclear Power** | Safety system reliability (e.g., emergency cooling) | | **Manufacturing** | MTBF of production lines, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) | | **Automotive** | Warranty prediction, component testing | | **Telecommunications** | Network uptime, redundancy design | | **Medical Devices** | Pacemaker reliability, hospital equipment availability | | **Oil & Gas** | Offshore platform reliability under harsh conditions | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Generalized Reliability Function from Failure Rate **Given:** The failure rate (hazard function) is defined as $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$. We also know that $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$. **Step 1:** Substitute the expression for $f(t)$ into $\lambda(t)$: $$ \lambda(t) = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} $$ **Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $R(t)$: $$ \lambda(t) \cdot R(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $dt$: $$ \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)\, dt = -dR(t) $$ **Step 4:** Separate variables and integrate from $0$ to $t$ (with $R(0) = 1$): $$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, R(u)\, du = -\int_{0}^{t} dR(u) $$ **Step 5:** Recognizing that the LHS involves $R(u)$ as the integrand, we rewrite using $\dfrac{dR}{R} = d(\ln R)$: $$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = -\ln R(t) + \ln R(0) $$ **Step 6:** Apply boundary condition $R(0) = 1 \Rightarrow \ln R(0) = 0$: $$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = -\ln R(t) $$ **Step 7:** Exponentiate both sides to isolate $R(t)$: $$ \boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)} $$ **Interpretation:** This is the **master equation** of reliability. If $\lambda(u)$ is known as a function of time, we can compute $R(t)$ for any mission time $t$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Exponential Distribution (Special Case: $\lambda$ = constant) **Given:** $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ = constant. **Step 1:** Substitute into the generalized reliability equation: $$ R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda\, du\right) $$ **Step 2:** Since $\lambda$ is constant, the integral is trivial: $$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda\, du = \lambda t $$ **Step 3:** Therefore: $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} $$ **Step 4:** Derive $F(t)$: $$ F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} $$ **Step 5:** Derive $f(t)$ by differentiating $F(t)$ with respect to $t$: $$ f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} $$ **Verification:** Check that $f(t) = \lambda(t) \cdot R(t)$: $$ \lambda(t) \cdot R(t) = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = f(t) \quad \checkmark $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MTTF for Exponential Distribution **Given:** $MTTF = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt$ and $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$. **Step 1:** Substitute: $$ MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt $$ **Step 2:** Use integration by parts with $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$: - $u = t \Rightarrow du = dt$ - $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt \Rightarrow v = -e^{-\lambda t}$ **Step 3:** Apply $\int u\, dv = uv - \int v\, du$: $$ MTTF = \left[-t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (-e^{-\lambda t})\, dt $$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the first term using L'Hôpital's rule: as $t \to \infty$, $t \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \to 0$; at $t = 0$, the term is $0$. So: $$ \left[-t \cdot e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 $$ **Step 5:** Simplify the remaining integral: $$ MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda} $$ **Conclusion:** $$ \boxed{MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda}} $$ > [!TIP] > **This is the single most important relationship in exponential reliability.** A component with failure rate $\lambda = 0.001$ failures/hour has $MTTF = 1000$ hours. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Deriving MTTF from $R(t)$ (Alternative Method) **Given:** $MTTF = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$ and $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. **Step 1:** Substitute: $$ MTTF = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\, dt $$ **Step 2:** Evaluate: $$ MTTF = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(-\frac{1}{\lambda}\right) = \frac{1}{\lambda} $$ This confirms the previous derivation. Both methods yield the same result, which is reassuring. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Series System Reliability **Given:** $n$ components connected in **series** — all must function for the system to work. **Step 1:** For independent components, the probability that all survive is the product of individual probabilities: $$ R_s = P(\text{Component 1 survives}) \times P(\text{Component 2 survives}) \times \cdots $$ **Step 2:** Therefore: $$ \boxed{R_s(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdot R_3 \cdots R_n} $$ **Step 3:** For exponential components with constant failure rates: $$ R_s(t) = e^{-\lambda_1 t} \cdot e^{-\lambda_2 t} \cdots e^{-\lambda_n t} = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \cdots + \lambda_n)t} $$ **Step 4:** System failure rate: $$ \lambda_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i $$ --- ## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Parallel System Reliability **Given:** $n$ components connected in **parallel** — at least one must function. **Step 1:** The system fails only if **all** components fail. The probability that component $i$ fails is $(1 - R_i)$. **Step 2:** Assuming independence: $$ P(\text{all components fail}) = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) \cdots (1 - R_n) $$ **Step 3:** Therefore, system reliability is: $$ \boxed{R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)} $$ **Step 4:** For 2 identical components with reliability $R$: $$ R_p = 1 - (1-R)^2 = 2R - R^2 $$ --- ## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (Exponential Reliability) **Problem:** A pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0002$ failures/hour. **(a)** Find the reliability for a 1000-hour mission. **Step 1:** Use $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$: $$ R(1000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 1000} = e^{-0.2} $$ **Step 2:** Compute: $$ R(1000) = 0.8187 \approx 0.819 $$ **(b)** Find the MTTF: $$ MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0002} = 5000 \text{ hours} $$ **(c)** Find the reliability for a 5000-hour mission: $$ R(5000) = e^{-0.0002 \times 5000} = e^{-1} = 0.3679 \approx 0.368 $$ **Interpretation:** At the MTTF, reliability has dropped to **36.8%** — this is a characteristic property of exponential distribution. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Always remember that $R(MTTF) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$ for any exponentially distributed component. This is a frequently asked trick question. --- ## 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Decision Guide | If the Question Asks... | Use This Formula | | :--- | :--- | | Probability of survival beyond time $t$ | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | | Probability of failure by time $t$ | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | | When will the failure rate be highest? | Refer to bath-tub curve | | Average life of non-repairable item | $MTTF = 1 / \lambda$ | | Average time cycle of repairable system | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | | Fraction of time system is up | $A_i = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$ | | System with all components in series | $R_s = R_1 \cdot R_2 \cdots R_n$ | | System with redundant components in parallel | $R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)\cdots(1-R_n)$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Bath-Tub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram represents the conceptual **Bath-Tub Curve**, showing the three phases of a component's failure rate over its operational lifetime. ```mermaid graph LR A["INFANT MORTALITY
Burn-in Period"] --> B["USEFUL LIFE
Constant Failure Rate"] B --> C["WEAR-OUT
Aging Period"] A -."Region I: Decreasing lambda".-> A B -."Region II: Constant lambda".-> B C -."Region III: Increasing lambda".-> C style A fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#FF6B35,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style B fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 style C fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** - **X-axis:** Time / Operating hours - **Y-axis:** Failure rate $\lambda(t)$ - **Region I (Infant Mortality):** Early-life failures due to manufacturing defects. Failure rate **decreases** with time. - **Region II (Useful Life):** Random failures. Failure rate is **constant** (exponential distribution applies). - **Region III (Wear-Out):** Aging failures. Failure rate **increases** rapidly. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Series System Reliability Architecture > [!NOTE] > In a **series configuration**, all components must work for the system to function. If **any one** component fails, the entire system fails. ```mermaid graph LR IN["Input"] --> C1["Component R1"] C1 --> C2["Component R2"] C2 --> C3["Component R3"] C3 --> CN["Component Rn"] CN --> OUT["Output / System Works"] FAIL["System Fails if
ANY component fails"] -.-> C1 FAIL -.-> C2 FAIL -.-> C3 FAIL -.-> CN style IN fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000000 style OUT fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000 style C1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style C2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style C3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style CN fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style FAIL fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000000 ``` **Reliability Formula:** $R_s = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 \times \cdots \times R_n$ --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Parallel (Redundant) System Reliability Architecture > [!NOTE] > In a **parallel configuration**, the system functions as long as **at least one** component works. This is the basis of **redundancy** in reliability engineering. ```mermaid graph LR IN["Input"] --> SPLIT["Parallel Splitter"] SPLIT --> C1["Component R1"] SPLIT --> C2["Component R2"] SPLIT --> C3["Component R3"] SPLIT --> CN["Component Rn"] C1 --> MERGE["Parallel Merger"] C2 --> MERGE C3 --> MERGE CN --> MERGE MERGE --> OUT["Output / System Works"] NOTE["System Fails only if
ALL components fail"] -.-> MERGE style IN fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000000 style SPLIT fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000 style MERGE fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000 style OUT fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000 style C1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style C2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style C3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style CN fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style NOTE fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000000 ``` **Reliability Formula:** $R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)(1 - R_3) \cdots (1 - R_n)$ --- ## 4.4 Functional Flow — Reliability Engineering Process > [!NOTE] > This diagram shows the **logical workflow** of applying reliability engineering in a maintenance management context — from data collection to system improvement. ```mermaid graph TD S1["STEP 1: Define System
and Mission Profile"] --> S2["STEP 2: Collect Failure Data
from Operating Records"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Determine Failure Rate
lambda from Data"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Compute R(t)
MTTF and Availability"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Identify Critical
Components for PM"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Apply Redundancy
or Design Changes"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Monitor and
Update Reliability Metrics"] S7 -.Feedback Loop.-> S2 style S1 fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000 style S2 fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#1A237E,color:#000000 style S3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style S4 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000 style S5 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000 style S6 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,color:#000000 style S7 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000000 ``` **Interpretation:** Reliability engineering is **iterative** — updated field data feeds back into the model to refine the estimates. --- ## 4.5 Decision Flow — When to Use Which Reliability Function ```mermaid graph TD Q["What information do you have?"] --> Q1{"Is failure rate lambda constant?"} Q1 -->|Yes| P1["Use Exponential Model
R(t) = exp(-lambda t)"] Q1 -->|No| P2["Use Generalized Model
R(t) = exp(-integral of lambda du)"] P1 --> R["Compute R(t), F(t), f(t), MTTF"] P2 --> R R --> S{"Is it a System?"} S -->|Series| S1["R_system = Product of R_i"] S -->|Parallel| S2["R_system = 1 - Product of (1 - R_i)"] S -->|Combined| S3["Apply RBD or
Fault Tree Analysis"] style Q fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000000 style Q1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000000 style P1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000 style P2 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000000 style R fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000000 style S fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000000 style S1 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,color:#000000 style S2 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,color:#000000 style S3 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,color:#000000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > The following questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns for the open elective course **OEIET613 - Maintenance Engineering and Management**. The mark distribution follows the standard KTU convention: **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks with internal choice)**. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the term "Reliability" as applied to a maintenance engineering system. Mention the boundary conditions of the reliability function.** **Model Answer:** > Reliability is defined as the **probability that a component, equipment, or system will perform its intended function under specified operating conditions for a stated period of time**. > > Mathematically, $R(t) = P(T > t)$, where $T$ is the time-to-failure random variable. > > **Boundary conditions:** > - $R(0) = 1$ (a new component has 100% reliability at start) > - $R(\infty) = 0$ (eventually every component will fail) > - $0 \leq R(t) \leq 1$ (reliability is a probability) > > \[ 3 Marks \] --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Distinguish between MTTF and MTBF with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | MTTF (Mean Time To Failure) | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Applicable to** | Non-repairable items | Repairable systems | | **Definition** | Average expected operating life before failure | Average time between two consecutive failures | | **Formula** | $MTTF = 1/\lambda$ | $MTBF = MTTF + MTTR$ | | **Example** | Light bulb, battery, capacitor | Motor, pump, computer server | > *Example:* A fuse (MTTF) is discarded after blowing. A motor (MTBF) is repaired and put back into service; its MTBF includes both its operating life and repair time. > > \[ 3 Marks \] --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **a)** Explain the **bath-tub curve** in detail. Describe the three regions with appropriate failure characteristics and corresponding maintenance strategies. **(7 Marks)** **b)** A critical pump has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. Compute: (i) The reliability for a 1000-hour mission. (ii) The MTTF. (iii) The reliability at the MTTF. (iv) The unreliability for a 2000-hour mission. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Bath-Tub Curve (7 Marks) The **bath-tub curve** is a graphical representation of the **failure rate $\lambda(t)$** of a component over its operational lifetime. It resembles the cross-section of a bath-tub, hence the name. The curve is divided into three distinct regions: **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in) Period:** - Failure rate is **high initially** and **decreases** rapidly with time. - Caused by manufacturing defects, poor quality control, substandard materials, improper installation, and debugging errors. - These are **early-life failures**. - **Maintenance strategy:** **Factory burn-in testing** to eliminate weak components before delivery; rigorous **quality control** during manufacture; and **debugging/commissioning** checks before putting equipment into service. **Region II — Useful Life (Normal Operating) Period:** - Failure rate is **constant** and **low**. - Failures occur **randomly** and are independent of component age. - These are **chance failures**, typically modeled by the **exponential distribution**. - **Maintenance strategy:** **Preventive maintenance (PM)** as scheduled, **condition-based monitoring**, and design for **redundancy** to handle random failures. **Region III — Wear-Out Period:** - Failure rate **increases rapidly** as components approach the end of their useful life. - Caused by **aging, fatigue, corrosion, wear, erosion, and degradation** of materials. - **Maintenance strategy:** **Scheduled replacement** of components before the wear-out phase begins; **overhaul** programs; **reconditioning**; and **life-cycle extension** through refurbishment. > The objective of any well-designed maintenance program is to: > 1. Minimize the duration of Region I (via burn-in). > 2. Extend the duration of Region II (via preventive maintenance). > 3. Replace components before Region III begins (via planned obsolescence). > > \[ 7 Marks \] #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks) **Given:** $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures/hour. **(i) Reliability for a 1000-hour mission:** $$ R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} $$ $$ R(1000) = 0.6065 \approx 0.607 $$ > **Answer:** $R(1000) \approx 0.607$ or **60.7%** of pumps will survive beyond 1000 hours. > > \[ 2 Marks \] **(ii) MTTF:** $$ MTTF = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \text{ hours} $$ > **Answer:** MTTF = **2000 hours** > > \[ 1 Mark \] **(iii) Reliability at MTTF (i.e., at $t = 2000$ hours):** $$ R(2000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 2000} = e^{-1} = 0.3679 \approx 0.368 $$ > **Answer:** $R(MTTF) = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$. This is a universal property — reliability at MTTF for any exponential distribution is always $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$. > > \[ 2 Marks \] **(iv) Unreliability for a 2000-hour mission:** $$ F(2000) = 1 - R(2000) = 1 - 0.3679 = 0.6321 $$ > **Answer:** $F(2000) \approx 0.632$ or **63.2%** > > \[ 2 Marks \] --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **a)** Derive the **generalized reliability function** $R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)$ starting from the definition of failure rate. **(7 Marks)** **b)** A system consists of **three components in series** with reliabilities $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, and $R_3 = 0.85$ for a 1000-hour mission. To improve reliability, a **parallel redundant** component with $R_4 = 0.90$ is added to component 3. Calculate: (i) The original system reliability. (ii) The new system reliability with the parallel redundancy. (iii) The percentage improvement in reliability. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Derivation of Generalized Reliability Function (7 Marks) **Given definition of failure rate:** $$ \lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} $$ **Step 1:** We know that the probability density function of failure is: $$ f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute into the failure rate equation: $$ \lambda(t) = \frac{-\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 3:** Rearrange to separate variables: $$ \lambda(t) \cdot dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -d[\ln R(t)] $$ **Step 4:** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$: $$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = -\int_{0}^{t} d[\ln R(u)] = -[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)] $$ **Step 5:** Apply boundary condition $R(0) = 1 \Rightarrow \ln R(0) = 0$: $$ \int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du = -\ln R(t) $$ **Step 6:** Solve for $R(t)$ by exponentiation: $$ \boxed{R(t) = \exp\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(u)\, du\right)} $$ **Special case:** If $\lambda(t) = \lambda$ (constant), then: $$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \quad \text{(exponential reliability)} $$ > \[ 7 Marks \] --- #### Part (b) — System Reliability with Redundancy (7 Marks) **Given:** $R_1 = 0.95$, $R_2 = 0.90$, $R_3 = 0.85$, $R_4 = 0.90$ (added in parallel with $R_3$). **(i) Original system reliability (all three in series):** $$ R_{\text{original}} = R_1 \times R_2 \times R_3 $$ $$ R_{\text{original}} = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85 $$ $$ R_{\text{original}} = 0.7267 \approx 0.727 $$ > **Answer:** $R_{\text{original}} \approx 0.727$ or **72.7%** > > \[ 2 Marks \] **(ii) New system reliability with parallel redundancy on Component 3:** The parallel combination of $R_3$ and $R_4$: $$ R_{34} = 1 -
