topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basics, Advantages and Disadvantages, Condition Based Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Maintenance? — The Formal KTU Definition

According to **BS EN 13306:2017** (the standard referenced by KTU 2024 Scheme for Maintenance Engineering), Maintenance is defined as:

> **"The combination of all technical, administrative, and managerial actions during the life cycle of an item, intended to retain it in, or restore it to, a state in which it can perform the required function."**

In simpler KTU-board-friendly language:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maintenance** = All actions taken to **keep equipment working** (preventive) or **fix it when it breaks** (corrective), so that the equipment continuously performs its intended function safely and economically.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body" Analogy 🏥

Think of maintenance like **taking care of your own body**:

| Human Health Activity | Equivalent Maintenance Strategy |
|---|---|
| Waiting until you collapse, then going to a doctor | **Reactive / Breakdown Maintenance** |
| Going for a full health check-up every 6 months (even if healthy) | **Preventive / Scheduled Maintenance** |
| Wearing a smartwatch that monitors heart rate and alerts you BEFORE a heart attack | **Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)** |
| Eating right, exercising daily to AVOID getting sick | **Proactive / Predictive Maintenance** |
| A complete lifestyle overhaul after a major heart attack | **Reliability Centered Maintenance (RCM)** |

The smartwatch analogy perfectly captures **CBM** — you monitor the *condition* of your body continuously and act *only when* an abnormal pattern is detected, rather than running to the doctor on a fixed calendar schedule.

## 1.3 Classification of Maintenance — The Big Picture

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups maintenance strategies into **two generations**:

### 🔹 A. Traditional (First Generation) Strategies
1. **Reactive / Breakdown / Run-to-Failure Maintenance** — Fix only when it breaks.
2. **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** — Servicing on a fixed schedule regardless of condition.
   - *Scheduled PM* (calendar-based)
   - *Planned PM* (usage-based: hours, cycles, km)

### 🔹 B. Modern (Second Generation) Strategies
3. **Predictive / Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)** — Service based on actual health indicators.
4. **Proactive Maintenance** — Eliminate the **root cause** of failure (not just symptoms).
5. **Reliability Centered Maintenance (RCM)** — Logic-tree based decision system (MSG-3).
6. **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** — Company-wide employee-involved maintenance (Japan-origin).
7. **Risk Based Maintenance (RBM)** — Maintenance prioritised by risk & criticality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 specifically emphasises **Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)** as the bridge strategy between scheduled PM and full predictive maintenance.

## 1.4 Definition — Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)** is a maintenance strategy that **monitors the actual operating condition** of an equipment using sensors and diagnostic techniques, and **performs maintenance only when predefined indicators (trends / thresholds) show that degradation has reached an unacceptable level**.

### Formal KTU-board phrasing:
> *"CBM is the set of maintenance actions initiated as a consequence of the continuous or periodic monitoring of one or more parameters indicative of the degradation state of an item."* — derived from **BS EN 13306**

### Key Terminology You MUST Know:
- **P-F Interval** — Time gap between *Potential Failure* (detectable) and *Functional Failure* (breakdown). **This is the CBM action window.**
- **Condition Monitoring (CM)** — The *measurement* activity inside CBM.
- **Diagnostic** — Identifies **what** is wrong.
- **Prognostic** — Predicts **when** failure will occur (Remaining Useful Life — RUL).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Maintenance Decision-Making

Every maintenance strategy answers **three fundamental questions**:

1. **WHEN** to maintain? (calendar, usage hours, or condition?)
2. **WHAT** to do? (clean, lubricate, replace, overhaul?)
3. **HOW MUCH** budget / downtime can we afford?

| Strategy | WHEN is maintenance done? | Information Source |
|---|---|---|
| Reactive | After failure | None — failure itself |
| Preventive (Scheduled) | At fixed time intervals | Calendar / run-hours |
| Condition Based (CBM) | When condition indicator crosses threshold | Sensor data, oil analysis, vibration |
| Predictive (advanced CBM) | When AI/ML model forecasts failure | Trend analysis + prognostics |
| Proactive | When root-cause is identified | Failure-mode analysis (FMEA) |

## 2.2 Why Maintenance Matters — The "Why" Behind It

Modern industries lose approximately **5–15 % of production capacity** to downtime. Key drivers pushing industries from reactive to CBM:

- **Safety** — Catastrophic failures (aircraft engines, nuclear pumps) cannot be tolerated.
- **Cost** — Unplanned downtime costs **10× more** than planned downtime (industry rule of thumb).
- **Quality** — A worn machine produces defective products.
- **Asset Life Extension** — Well-maintained machines last **2–3× longer**.
- **Environmental Compliance** — Leaks from worn seals cause pollution.

## 2.3 Comparison of Strategies — Advantages & Disadvantages

### A. Reactive (Breakdown) Maintenance

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **Zero initial planning cost**
- ✅ **Lowest possible upfront expenditure**
- ✅ **Suitable for non-critical, low-cost, easily-replaceable items** (e.g., light bulbs, fuses)

**Disadvantages:**
- ❌ **Unpredictable downtime** — production schedules disrupted
- ❌ **Higher long-run cost** — emergency labour + spare parts cost **3–5× more**
- ❌ **Safety hazard** — catastrophic failures may injure personnel
- ❌ **Secondary damage** — one failed part destroys others (e.g., metal chip in gearbox)
- ❌ **Inventory chaos** — must stock every possible spare

### B. Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **Planned downtime** — production schedules honoured
- ✅ **Reduced emergency breakdowns** by 30–70 %
- ✅ **Better spare parts inventory control**
- ✅ **Extended equipment life** through regular lubrication/tightening
- ✅ **Improved safety** through systematic inspections

**Disadvantages:**
- ❌ **Over-maintenance** — parts replaced while still serviceable (waste)
- ❌ **Under-maintenance** if failure pattern is irregular
- ❌ **Fixed cost regardless of condition** — money spent even on healthy machines
- ❌ **Increased inspection burden** — consumes labour
- ❌ **Not optimal** for equipment with random failure modes

### C. Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)

**Advantages:**
- ✅ **Maintenance only when actually needed** — eliminates over-maintenance waste
- ✅ **Dramatic reduction in unplanned downtime** (typically **70–90 %**)
- ✅ **Lower lifecycle cost** — parts used to full useful life
- ✅ **Early fault detection** — prevents secondary damage
- ✅ **Improved safety & reliability**
- ✅ **Provides trending data** for future design improvements
- ✅ **Enables prognostics & Remaining Useful Life (RUL) estimation**

**Disadvantages:**
- ❌ **High initial capital investment** — sensors, DAQ systems, software
- ❌ **Requires trained analysts** to interpret vibration spectra, thermograms, etc.
- ❌ **Each technique is fault-specific** — vibration catches bearings but not oil degradation
- ❌ **Not feasible for low-cost equipment** (instrumentation cost > equipment value)
- ❌ **Sensor reliability issues** — sensor failures can give false alarms or miss faults
- ❌ **Data overload** — without AI, manual interpretation is impractical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Question:** "Compare Preventive and Condition Based Maintenance." — Memorise the table above; examiners expect 4–6 paired points.

## 2.4 The P-F Curve — Heart of CBM Concept

This is **THE most-tested diagram** in any maintenance paper.

```
Condition
    │
100%│         ╭───── (New / Healthy)
    │        ╱
    │       ╱
    │      ╱
    │     ╱
    │    ╱   ←─ P (Potential Failure — detectable by sensor)
    │   ╱
    │  ╱         CBM ACTION WINDOW
    │ ╱          (P-F Interval)
    │╱
  0%│─────F────── (Functional Failure — machine cannot perform)
    └────────────────────────────── Time →
```

- **P** = Potential failure occurs. A condition-monitoring sensor (vibration, temperature) can detect it, but the equipment still functions normally.
- **F** = Functional failure. The equipment can no longer perform its intended function.
- **P-F Interval** = The time window during which CBM gives a *warning* before breakdown. **Typical P-F intervals:**
  - Vibration on rolling element bearing: **weeks to months**
  - Oil debris analysis on gearbox: **months**
  - Thermography on electrical connection: **hours to days**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of CBM:** The **shorter** your inspection interval relative to the P-F interval, the **higher** the probability of catching failure before F.

## 2.5 Common CBM Techniques — The "Big Five"

| # | Technique | What it Detects | Typical Sensor / Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vibration Analysis** | Bearing wear, imbalance, misalignment, gear faults, looseness | Accelerometer (FFT spectrum, envelope analysis) |
| 2 | **Thermography (Infrared)** | Overheating electrical joints, insulation breakdown, refractory wear | IR camera |
| 3 | **Oil / Lubricant Analysis** | Wear particles, contamination, additive depletion, viscosity change | Spectrometer, ferrography, viscosity meter |
| 4 | **Ultrasonic Testing** | Cracks, leaks in pressure systems, partial discharge | Ultrasonic transducer |
| 5 | **Acoustic Emission** | Crack initiation & growth in structures | AE sensor (high-frequency) |

Auxiliary techniques: **Motor Current Signature Analysis (MCSA)**, **Performance trending** (flow, pressure, power), **Visual inspection / boroscope**, **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**.

## 2.6 📊 KTU Formula Sheet — Reliability & Maintenance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **must-memorise** formulas. They appear in nearly every KTU numerical / derivation question.

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Probability system is operating when needed | Dimensionless (0–1) or % |
| **Inherent Availability (Aᵢ)** | $A_i = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ (corrective downtime only) | Design availability | % |
| **Achieved Availability (Aₐ)** | $A_a = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MDT}$ where MDT = MTTR + Logistics delay | Operational availability | % |
| **MTBF** | $MTBF = \dfrac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| **MTTR** | $MTTR = \dfrac{Total\ Downtime\ (corrective)}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | Constant failure rate (useful life) | failures/hour |
| **Reliability R(t)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of no failure by time *t* | Dimensionless |
| **Unreliability F(t)** | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | Probability of failure by time *t* | Dimensionless |
| **Probability Density f(t)** | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | Instantaneous failure probability | 1/hour |
| **OEE** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | % |
| **MTTF** (non-repairable) | $MTTF = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time To Failure | hours |
| **Hazard Rate h(t)** | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Conditional failure rate | 1/hour |

> **Constraint for the above table:** λ is constant — applies **only during the useful-life (constant-hazard) region** of the bathtub curve. For Weibull-distributed failures, use $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^\beta}$.

## 2.7 Cost-Effectiveness Logic of CBM

A simple cost model:

$$C_{total} = C_{insp} \cdot N + C_{fail} \cdot E[N_{fail}(N)]$$

Where:
- $C_{insp}$ = cost per inspection (sensors + labour)
- $N$ = number of inspections per unit time
- $C_{fail}$ = cost per functional failure (downtime + repair)
- $E[N_{fail}]$ = expected number of functional failures, which **decreases** as $N$ increases

**CBM logic:** Increase $N$ (inspection frequency) only until the marginal cost of one more inspection equals the marginal savings from one fewer failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Availability Formula

**Statement to prove:**

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

### Step 1 — Define the observation window

Consider a single repairable system observed for a long period $T_{total}$.

$$T_{total} = T_{operating} + T_{down}$$

Where:
- $T_{operating}$ = cumulative time the system was performing its function
- $T_{down}$ = cumulative downtime (failed + under repair)

### Step 2 — Define Availability

Availability is the **fraction of time the system was ready / operational**:

$$A = \frac{T_{operating}}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_{operating}}{T_{operating} + T_{down}}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce MTBF and MTTR

Let $n$ = number of failures observed in $T_{total}$.

By definition:

$$MTBF = \frac{T_{operating}}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{operating} = n \cdot MTBF$$

$$MTTR = \frac{T_{down}}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{down} = n \cdot MTTR$$

### Step 4 — Substitute back

$$A = \frac{n \cdot MTBF}{n \cdot MTBF + n \cdot MTTR}$$

The factor $n$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** If MTBF = 1000 h and MTTR = 10 h, then A = 1000/1010 ≈ **0.9901** = **99.01 %**. Even small MTTR improvements (cutting repair time from 10 h to 5 h) push A from 99.01 % to **99.50 %** — a substantial reliability gain. **This is the single most powerful argument for CBM.**

## 3.2 Derivation of the Exponential Reliability Function

**Statement:** For a constant failure rate λ, the reliability is $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

### Step 1 — Define reliability over an infinitesimal interval

In a small interval $\Delta t$, the probability of survival $R(t+\Delta t)$ depends on having survived up to $t$ and then surviving through $\Delta t$:

$$R(t+\Delta t) = R(t) \cdot [1 - \lambda \Delta t]$$

### Step 2 — Form the difference equation

$$R(t+\Delta t) - R(t) = -\lambda R(t) \Delta t$$

### Step 3 — Take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$

This yields the differential equation:

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = -\lambda R(t)$$

### Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate

$$\int_{R(0)}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln R(t) - \ln R(0) = -\lambda t$$

### Step 5 — Apply initial condition R(0) = 1 (system is new, guaranteed to work at t = 0)

$$\ln R(t) = -\lambda t$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} \quad \blacksquare$$

### Step 6 — Verify MTTF

For a non-repairable component:

$$MTTF = \int_0^\infty R(t)\, dt = \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t}\, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Hence **MTBF = MTTF = 1/λ** when repair effectively restores the system to "as good as new."

## 3.3 Derivation of Optimal Preventive Maintenance Interval

A classic **Ktu 12-mark derivation**. Consider a component that costs:
- $C_p$ per scheduled PM (planned downtime + parts)
- $C_f$ per failure (unplanned downtime + parts + lost production), with $C_f \gg C_p$
- PM is carried out every $T$ hours; component has constant failure rate λ.

### Step 1 — Expected number of failures between PMs

With PM interval $T$ and constant failure rate λ, expected failures in interval $T$:

$$E[N_f(T)] = \lambda T \quad \text{(for small } \lambda T \text{)}$$

More generally for a Weibull distribution:

$$E[N_f(T)] = \int_0^T \lambda(t)\, dt = H(T) \quad \text{(cumulative hazard)}$$

### Step 2 — Average cost per unit time

$$C(T) = \frac{C_p + C_f \cdot \lambda T}{T} = \frac{C_p}{T} + C_f \lambda$$

### Step 3 — Minimise C(T)

Differentiate with respect to $T$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC}{dT} = -\frac{C_p}{T^2} + 0 = 0 \implies \text{No interior minimum!}$$

For constant failure rate, more-frequent PM is *always* cheaper per unit time, but we must add a **rebuild cost** $C_R$ when we actually do a PM. Then:

$$C(T) = \frac{C_p + C_f \cdot \lambda T + C_R}{T} = \frac{C_p + C_R}{T} + C_f \lambda$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dC}{dT} = -\frac{C_p + C_R}{T^2} = 0$$

Again no finite optimum — PM interval becomes zero. This is why **CBM is mathematically superior**: it eliminates the fixed $C_R$ by acting only when the **condition** warrants.

### Step 4 — CBM insight

CBM replaces fixed $T$ with a *condition-dependent trigger*. As long as the actual cost model includes a fixed overhaul cost (which constant-λ PM does not justify), **CBM dominates scheduled PM on a cost-per-unit-time basis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the mathematical reason why modern industries migrate from PM → CBM: under realistic cost structures, CBM is provably optimal.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-style)

**Q:** A pump has MTBF = 2000 h, MTTR = 8 h. An upgrade reduces MTTR to 4 h but increases MTBF to 2400 h. Calculate the % improvement in availability.

### Solution:

**Before:**
$$A_1 = \frac{2000}{2000 + 8} = \frac{2000}{2008} = 0.99602 = 99.60\%$$

**After:**
$$A_2 = \frac{2400}{2400 + 4} = \frac{2400}{2404} = 0.99834 = 99.83\%$$

**Improvement:**
$$\Delta A = 0.99834 - 0.99602 = 0.00232 = 0.232 \text{ percentage points}$$

$$\%\, improvement = \frac{\Delta A}{A_1} \times 100 = \frac{0.00232}{0.99602} \times 100 \approx 0.233\%$$

**Interpretation:** Although numerically small (0.23 %), in a 24/7 plant producing ₹10 lakh/hour, this **0.23 % availability gain ≈ ₹50 lakh/year in avoided lost production**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CBM Implementation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Equipment in Operation] --> B[Condition Monitoring Sensors Installed]
    B --> C[Data Acquisition DAQ System]
    C --> D[Signal Processing FFT Filtering]
    D --> E[Data Storage Trend Database]
    E --> F{Threshold Exceeded?}
    F -->|No| G[Continue Operation Log Data]
    F -->|Yes| H[Alarm Generated to Maintenance Team]
    H --> I[Root Cause Diagnosis]
    I --> J{Fault Confirmed?}
    J -->|No| G
    J -->|Yes| K[Plan Maintenance Schedule]
    K --> L[Execute Repair or Replacement]
    L --> M[Update Equipment History Record]
    M --> G
    G --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The system continuously loops. Condition data is gathered non-invasively while the equipment runs; an intervention (alarm → diagnosis → repair) occurs **only if a threshold is crossed**. This is the essence of CBM.

## 4.2 Maintenance Strategy Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reactive Breakdown] --> P[Preventive Scheduled]
    P --> C[Condition Based CBM]
    C --> PD[Predictive AI ML Based]
    PD --> PR[Proactive Root Cause Elimination]
    PR --> RCM[Reliability Centered Maintenance RCM]
    RCM --> TPM[Total Productive Maintenance TPM]
```

Each arrow represents an **increase in data-driven decision making** and a **decrease in unplanned downtime**.

## 4.3 The P-F Curve Block-Level Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Healthy Zone R near 100 percent] --> P[Point P Potential Failure Detected]
    P --> W[P-F Interval CBM Action Window]
    W --> F[Point F Functional Failure Breakdown]
    F --> D[Downtime and Repair Required]
    D --> R[Restored to Healthy State]
    R --> H
```

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU-typical values (bearing) |
|---|---|---|
| Healthy zone | Equipment operates within design limits | Months–years |
| P-F interval | Window where CBM sensor detects degradation | Weeks–months |
| Functional failure | Equipment can no longer perform required function | Sudden |

## 4.4 The Bathtub Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BathtubCurve[Bathtub Curve h of t vs Time]
        B1[Infant Mortality Region h decreasing] --> B2[Useful Life Region h approximately constant lambda]
        B2 --> B3[Wear Out Region h increasing]
    end
```

- **Infant mortality** → managed by **burn-in / debugging** at factory.
- **Useful life** → managed by **CBM / on-condition tasks**.
- **Wear-out** → managed by **scheduled replacement / overhaul**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** CBM is most valuable in the *useful-life region* of the bathtub curve, where failures are random but precursor signals (vibration, temperature) exist.

## 4.5 CBM Decision Matrix — Technique Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Equipment to be Monitored] --> Q1{Fault Type?}
    Q1 -->|Mechanical Vibration| V[Vibration Analysis Accelerometer FFT]
    Q1 -->|Thermal Hot Spot| T[Infrared Thermography]
    Q1 -->|Wear Particles| O[Oil Analysis Spectrometer]
    Q1 -->|Crack Growth| U[Ultrasonic or Acoustic Emission]
    V --> End[Continuous CM System]
    T --> End
    O --> End
    U --> End
```

# 📘 SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Condition Based Maintenance (CBM). List any four condition monitoring techniques used in CBM.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Condition Based Maintenance is a maintenance strategy in which the actual condition of the equipment is monitored continuously or periodically using appropriate sensors, and maintenance action is initiated **only when one or more condition indicators show that degradation has progressed to a pre-defined unacceptable level.**
>
> **Four condition monitoring techniques:**
> 1. **Vibration analysis** — detects imbalance, misalignment, bearing faults, gear wear.
> 2. **Infrared thermography** — detects overheating in electrical joints, motors, refractory linings.
> 3. **Oil / lubricant analysis** — detects wear particles (Fe, Cu, Cr), contamination, viscosity loss.
> 4. **Ultrasonic / acoustic emission testing** — detects cracks, leaks, partial discharge.

**(3 marks: 1 definition + 4 techniques × ½ mark)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between Preventive Maintenance and Condition Based Maintenance (any three points).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Preventive Maintenance | Condition Based Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Fixed time / usage interval | Condition indicator threshold |
| **Cost efficiency** | Over-maintenance possible (parts replaced early) | Parts used to full life |
| **Skill requirement** | Routine technician skill | Specialist analyst needed |
| **Downtime** | Planned but may be unnecessary | Planned only when required |
| **Data needs** | Calendar / hour meter | Sensors + data acquisition + trending |

**(3 marks: 3 well-explained distinctions × 1 mark each)**

---

## 🅱️ PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the P-F curve concept in Condition Based Maintenance. How does it help in deciding the inspection interval? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **P-F curve** plots the condition (or health) of an equipment against time. It identifies two critical points:

- **Point P (Potential Failure):** The point at which an impending failure becomes **detectable** by a condition-monitoring sensor, but the equipment is still capable of performing its required function. For example, an increase in bearing vibration from 2 mm/s to 7 mm/s RMS while the machine still rotates normally.

- **Point F (Functional Failure):** The point at which the equipment can no longer perform its required function (e.g., bearing seizes, machine trips). Beyond F, the equipment must be repaired before being returned to service.

The horizontal time gap between P and F is called the **P-F Interval**. This interval represents the *window of opportunity* for CBM to act before the functional failure occurs.

**How P-F curve decides inspection interval:**

1. Identify the failure mode and its P-F interval (e.g., 30 days for inner-race bearing fatigue).
2. The **inspection interval** must be **shorter than the P-F interval** to have a high probability of detecting the failure before F.
3. A common rule: inspection interval = **½ to ⅓ of the P-F interval** to allow redundancy against measurement error.
4. If the P-F interval is short (say, hours), continuous online monitoring (sensors + DAQ) is required instead of walk-around inspections.
5. If the P-F interval is long (months), periodic portable measurements suffice — saving cost.

**(7 marks: 2 marks P-F definition + 3 marks inspection-interval logic + 2 marks examples)**

---

**(b) With a neat sketch, explain the bathtub curve. Describe how different maintenance strategies apply in each region. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```
Failure Rate h(t)
    │
    │\
    │ \           /\
    │  \         /  \___
    │   \_______/       \____
    │   Infant  Useful  Wear-out
    │   Mortality Life    Region
    └────────────────────────────── Time →
```

The **bathtub curve** is a qualitative model showing how the *instantaneous failure rate* $h(t)$ of a population of components varies with time. It has three distinct regions:

**Region 1 — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):**
- High but **decreasing** failure rate.
- Caused by manufacturing defects, poor installation, commissioning errors.
- Maintenance strategy: **Debugging / burn-in testing at factory**; replace defective units during warranty period.

**Region 2 — Useful Life (Normal Operating Region):**
- Failure rate is **low and approximately constant** (λ ≈ constant).
- Failures are random (overstress, luck).
- Maintenance strategy: **Condition Based Maintenance (CBM)** is most effective here, as precursors exist and components are too expensive to replace on a schedule.
- Reliability follows $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Region 3 — Wear-out Region:**
- Failure rate **rises sharply** due to aging — fatigue, corrosion, wear.
- Maintenance strategy: **Scheduled replacement / overhaul** before expected wear-out (preventive replacement). This is where *Preventive Maintenance* is genuinely useful.

> **Conclusion:** No single strategy covers all three regions. The optimum programme combines burn-in (factory), CBM (useful life), and scheduled replacement (wear-out) — this is precisely the philosophy of **RCM**.

**(7 marks: 2 marks sketch + 3 marks region explanation + 2 marks strategy mapping)**

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the various advantages and disadvantages of Condition Based Maintenance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages of CBM:**

1. **Reduced unplanned downtime** — faults caught at Point P, before functional failure F. Industry data: 70–90 % reduction in catastrophic breakdowns.
2. **Lower lifecycle cost** — components are replaced only when actually worn out, eliminating over-maintenance waste.
3. **Extended equipment life** — early detection prevents secondary damage (e.g., a worn bearing damaging the shaft).
4. **Improved safety** — catastrophic failures (rotor burst, electrical fire) are prevented.
5. **Better inventory management** — spares ordered based on actual RUL predictions, not guesses.
6. **Prognostic capability** — provides Remaining Useful Life (RUL) enabling planned shutdown windows.
7. **Data for continuous improvement** — trending data feeds back into design improvements.

**Disadvantages of CBM:**

1. **High initial capital** — vibration sensors, IR cameras, spectrometers, DAQ systems, software licences.
2. **Skilled manpower required** — analysts must interpret FFT spectra, ferrograms, thermograms. Training cost is significant.
3. **False alarms & missed faults** — sensor drift, mounting issues, electromagnetic interference cause misdiagnosis.
4. **Limited to specific faults** — vibration catches bearing faults but NOT oil degradation; thermography catches overheating but NOT cracks. Multiple techniques must be deployed.
5. **Not economical for low-cost assets** — instrumenting a ₹5,000 motor with a ₹50,000 vibration system is unjustifiable.
6. **Data management burden** — large sensor streams require historians, databases, often AI/ML tools.

**(7 marks: 4 advantages + 3 disadvantages, each 1 mark)**

---

**(b) A hydraulic press operates 16 hours/day. The MTBF is 1500 hours and MTTR is 10 hours. Calculate (i) Availability, (ii) Reliability after 500 hours, (iii) Expected number of failures per year, (iv) Comment on the suitability of installing a CBM system. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- MTBF = 1500 h
- MTTR = 10 h
- Daily operation = 16 h → Annual operation = 16 × 365 = **5840 h/year**

**(i) Availability:**
$$A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 10} = \frac{1500}{1510} = 0.9934 = \mathbf{99.34\%}$$

**(ii) Reliability after t = 500 hours:**
Failure rate: $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{1500} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ failures/hour

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.0006667 \times 500} = e^{-0.3333} = 0.7165 = \mathbf{71.65\%}$$

**(iii) Expected failures per year:**
$$\text{Expected failures} = \frac{T_{operating}}{MTBF} = \frac{5840}{1500} = \mathbf{3.89 \approx 4\ failures/year}$$

**(iv) CBM suitability comment:**
The press is **mission-critical** (operating 5840 h/year, producing ~4 failures/year). Each unplanned stoppage probably causes significant production loss. Since:
- MTBF is moderate (random failures occur in useful-life region),
- Availability is high but not exceptional (99.34 %),
- A small improvement (e.g., 0.5 %) yields ~30 h/year additional uptime ≈ substantial revenue saved,

**CBM is highly justified.** A vibration monitoring system (cost ~₹2–3 lakh) can be recovered in **one to two avoided failures**. Recommendation: install **online vibration + oil analysis** with alarms set at ½ × P-F interval for all critical bearings and hydraulic valves.

**(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 marks for parts i, ii, iii, iv respectively)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CBM with Predictive Maintenance** — They overlap but CBM is *triggered by a threshold*, Predictive is *triggered by an AI/ML forecast* (RUL). In KTU 2024 answers, both are acceptable synonyms but **distinguish them when the question demands**.
> 2. **Forgetting the P-F interval logic** — Many students draw the P-F curve but **fail to state that the inspection interval must be < P-F interval**. This is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong unit for failure rate λ** — It is **failures per hour**, NOT per year. Convert: $\lambda_{per\ hr} = \lambda_{per\ yr} / 8760$.
> 4. **Availability ≠ Reliability** — Reliability $R(t)$ has *time* as input; Availability $A$ is a *steady-state fraction*. Examiners trap students who use these interchangeably.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in Bathtub Curve question** — A diagram is **mandatory**; a well-labelled 3-region sketch carries 2 marks by itself.
> 6. **Numerical units** — When MTTR is given in *minutes*, convert to hours before applying $A = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR)$. A common 1-mark loss.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid-revision checklist — must be at your fingertips before the exam:**

- ✅ **Maintenance** (BS EN 13306) = *all technical + administrative actions* to retain/restore function.
- ✅ **Reactive** = run-to-failure (cheap upfront, costly long-term).
- ✅ **Preventive (PM)** = calendar / usage-based servicing.
- ✅ **Condition Based (CBM)** = service on sensor-detected condition threshold.
- ✅ **P-F curve** = P (detectable potential failure) → F (functional failure); interval = CBM action window.
- ✅ **Inspection interval < P-F interval** (typically ½ to ⅓ of P-F interval).
- ✅ **CBM techniques (Big 5):** Vibration, Thermography, Oil analysis, Ultrasonic, Acoustic Emission.
- ✅ **Availability:** $A = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- ✅ **Reliability:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for constant λ (useful-life region).
- ✅ **Failure rate:** $\lambda = 1/MTBF$ (during useful life).
- ✅ **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality.
- ✅ **CBM advantages:** reduced downtime, lower lifecycle cost, extended life, safety, prognostic capability.
- ✅ **CBM disadvantages:** high initial cost, skilled manpower, fault-specific techniques, false alarms.
- ✅ **Bathtub curve** has 3 regions: infant mortality, useful life, wear-out — *each needs a different maintenance strategy*.
- ✅ **R(t) and A are different** — R(t) is a time-dependent survival probability; A is a long-run fraction.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Basics, Advantages & Disadvantages, Condition Based Maintenance**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Maintenance Strategies – The Engineering Definition

In the framework of **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme**, **Maintenance Engineering** is defined as the discipline of engineering concerned with the planning, scheduling, execution, and control of activities aimed at preserving and restoring the functional integrity of physical assets, machinery, and industrial systems to their designed operating condition.

A **Maintenance Strategy** is a planned, systematic, and documented approach that defines *what* maintenance activities will be carried out, *when*, *how*, and *by whom*, with the objective of optimizing the **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**, ensuring **safety**, maximizing **availability**, and minimizing the **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** of the asset.

Mathematically, the broad objective of any maintenance strategy is expressed as:

$$
\text{Minimize} \quad C_{\text{Total}} = C_{\text{PM}} + C_{\text{CM}} + C_{\text{ downtime}} + C_{\text{ failure}} + C_{\text{ safety}} + C_{\text{quality}}
$$

Subject to the constraint of achieving:

$$
A_{\text{system}} \geq A_{\text{target}}, \quad R_{\text{system}} \geq R_{\text{target}}, \quad S_{\text{system}} \geq S_{\text{target}}
$$

Where:
- $A_{\text{system}}$ = System **Availability** 
- $R_{\text{system}}$ = System **Reliability**
- $S_{\text{system}}$ = System **Safety performance**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Module 2 mandates a comparative study of multiple maintenance strategies. Students are expected to *tabulate* merits and demerits and identify the *contextual applicability* of each strategy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Human Health Check-up" Model

Imagine you own a car. You have **three approaches** to keep it running:

- **Approach 1 – "Run Until It Breaks" (Breakdown/Corrective Maintenance):** You drive the car until the engine seizes, and only then take it to the garage. The car has been "useful" until catastrophe, but the repair cost is catastrophic and may be fatal to the engine.

- **Approach 2 – "Calendar Servicing" (Preventive/Scheduled Maintenance):** Whether the car needs it or not, you change the engine oil every 5,000 km, regardless of how the car is being driven (highway vs. city), and replace brake pads at fixed intervals. This is **disciplined** but sometimes wasteful — you may throw away parts that still had life in them.

- **Approach 3 – "Doctor's Diagnostic Tests" (Predictive/Condition-Based Maintenance):** You install sensors that monitor engine oil quality, brake pad thickness (indirectly via wear debris), and vibration levels. You service *only when the sensor reading indicates deterioration*. This is the **smartest** but requires investment in diagnostic infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Engineer's Translation:** 
> - Approach 1 = **Reactive Maintenance (RM)**
> - Approach 2 = **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** — *time-based or usage-based*
> - Approach 3 = **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** — *condition-based*

In industrial practice, no single strategy is universally superior. The art of **Maintenance Engineering Management** is to **blend** these strategies into a hybrid program best suited for the asset's criticality and operating environment.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Maintenance Strategies – The Master Framework

The widely accepted **Kardex–Mobley** and **EN 13306** classification organizes maintenance into the following **six canonical strategies**:

| # | Strategy | Core Philosophy | Trigger |
|---|----------|----------------|---------|
| 1 | **Corrective (Breakdown) Maintenance** | Run-to-failure | Failure event |
| 2 | **Preventive (Scheduled) Maintenance** | Service at intervals | Calendar / Usage |
| 3 | **Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance** | Service when condition degrades | Sensor reading |
| 4 | **Opportunity Maintenance** | Service during planned shutdown | Window of opportunity |
| 5 | **Design-Out Maintenance** | Modify design to eliminate failure | Engineering change |
| 6 | **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** | Empower operators, zero losses | Cultural & operational |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When asked to compare strategies in a 14-mark question, always use a **comparison matrix** with columns for *Trigger, Cost Profile, Skill Required, Downtime Impact, and Applicability*. The examiner rewards structured tabular answers over prose paragraphs.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strategy 1 – Corrective Maintenance (CM) / Breakdown Maintenance (BM)

### 2.1.1 Definition & Features
**Corrective Maintenance** is the set of activities performed **after a failure has occurred**, intended to restore a failed asset to a state in which it can perform its required function. It is the *default* strategy when no planning exists.

**Key Features:**
- **Reactive in nature** – actions follow the failure event
- **No pre-failure intervention** – the asset operates until performance drops below acceptable limits
- **Lump-sum cost at failure** – spares, labor, and downtime are incurred simultaneously
- **Emergency-driven planning** – often bypasses standard work-order systems
- **Two sub-forms:** *Immediate CM (palliative)* and *Deferred CM (curative)*

### 2.1.2 Merits
1. **Minimal upfront planning cost** – no scheduled inspections, no recurring service contracts
2. **Optimum utilization of component life** – parts are run until actual end-of-life
3. **Suitable for non-critical, redundant, or low-cost assets** (e.g., standby lighting, fence integrity)
4. **Simple administrative structure** – no CMMS work-order scheduling required
5. **Avoids "maintenance-induced failures"** that sometimes occur during unnecessary disassembly

### 2.1.3 Demerits
1. **Unplanned downtime** is *catastrophic* for production lines (loss of throughput)
2. **Emergency repair costs** are typically 3× to 8× higher than planned repair
3. **Safety risk** to personnel and adjacent equipment (e.g., catastrophic bearing failure)
4. **Secondary damage cascade** – a single bearing seizure can destroy the shaft, housing, and couplings
5. **Spare parts uncertainty** – required spares may not be on-site, lengthening Mean Down Time (MDT)
6. **Insurance and warranty implications** – many OEM warranties are void if scheduled maintenance is not performed

---

## 2.2 Strategy 2 – Preventive Maintenance (PM) / Scheduled Maintenance

### 2.2.1 Definition & Features
**Preventive Maintenance** is the **planned, periodic, and proactive** set of activities carried out at predetermined intervals (calendar, runtime, cycles, or mileage) to *reduce the probability of failure* or *degradation* of an asset.

**Key Features:**
- **Time-based or usage-based** – triggers are *not* the actual condition of the asset
- **Pre-planned work orders** – parts, labor, and downtime are scheduled in advance
- **Two main sub-types:**
  - *Scheduled Overhaul (TBO)* — at fixed hours
  - *On-Condition Servicing* — visual/tactile checks
- **Driven by Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** and **reliability data**
- **Documented in OEM manuals and statutory regulations**

### 2.2.2 Merits
1. **Dramatically reduces unplanned downtime** — failures are intercepted
2. **Improved safety** — worn parts are replaced before catastrophic failure
3. **Better spare parts inventory planning** — predictable consumption
4. **Extended asset life** — wear is reset periodically
5. **Lower long-term cost of ownership** for critical assets
6. **Compliance** with statutory/legal requirements (e.g., pressure vessel inspections, lifting gear)

### 2.2.3 Demerits
1. **Underutilization of part life** — the "**bathtub curve**" shows many parts are still in their useful life zone at PM interval
2. **Higher direct maintenance cost** — labor and parts are spent regardless of need
3. **Possibility of "introduction of failure"** — improper reassembly may induce new defects
4. **Production scheduling pressure** — planned shutdowns may be delayed or rushed
5. **Overconfidence bias** — operators may assume "it was just serviced" and miss early warning signs

---

## 2.3 Strategy 3 – Predictive Maintenance (PdM) / Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM)

### 2.3.1 Definition & Features
**Predictive Maintenance** is the strategy in which maintenance is performed *only when monitored indicators show a measurable degradation* or *impending failure*. It uses **non-destructive testing**, **vibration analysis**, **oil analysis**, **thermography**, and other techniques to assess real-time condition.

**Key Features:**
- **Condition-triggered** — not time-triggered
- **Sensor-driven** — relies on instrumentation
- **Prognostic** — predicts *when* failure will occur, not just *that* it has occurred
- **Highly dependent on data quality and analyst skill**
- **Closes the loop** with **IIoT and Industry 4.0** architectures

### 2.3.2 Merits
1. **Maximum utilization of component life** — change parts *just before* failure
2. **Minimizes unnecessary maintenance** — reduces PM-induced failures
3. **Drastically reduces unplanned downtime** — failures are predicted
4. **Enables root-cause failure analysis (RCFA)** — data captures fault development
5. **Improves product quality** — degraded machines produce defective goods
6. **Safety enhancement** — anomalies are detected before catastrophic event

### 2.3.3 Demerits
1. **High initial investment** — sensors, analyzers, software (CMMS/EAM integration)
2. **Skilled manpower required** — vibration analysts, certified thermographers
3. **False positives and false negatives** — sensor noise, baseline drift
4. **Sensor reliability is itself a failure mode**
5. **Not applicable to all failure types** — random/sudden failures (e.g., brittle fracture) cannot be predicted
6. **Data deluge** — requires robust data management infrastructure

---

## 2.4 Strategies 4, 5, 6 – Opportunity, Design-Out, and TPM

### 2.4.1 Opportunity Maintenance
Performed when the asset is **already stopped** for another reason. Excellent for non-critical sub-components.

### 2.4.2 Design-Out Maintenance
**Eliminate the failure mode** at its source by engineering redesign (e.g., upgrade from bronze bushing to sealed ball bearing, replace V-belt with synchronous timing belt).

### 2.4.3 Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)
Originated in **Nippon Denso (Toyota)**, 1971. TPM is a **holistic, autonomous, and culture-driven** strategy that aims for **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents, and zero waste** through the active participation of *all employees* — from operators to top management. The **Eight Pillars of TPM** are well-documented and are frequent KTU exam topics.

> [!NOTE]
> **TPM Eight Pillars (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> 1. **Jishu Hozen (Autonomous Maintenance)** – operator-driven cleaning, lubrication, tightening
> 2. **Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement)** – small-group, project-based kaizen
> 3. **Planned Maintenance** – schedule-based PM department activities
> 4. **Quality Maintenance** – zero-defect conditions for zero-defect products
> 5. **Education & Training** – multi-skill development
> 6. **Office TPM** – extending TPM to administrative functions
> 7. **Safety, Health & Environment** – zero-accident workplace
> 8. **Early Management / New Product Development** – design for maintainability

---

## 2.5 Comparative Master Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all six strategies** for direct KTU exam use.

| Parameter | Corrective (BM) | Preventive (PM) | Predictive (PdM) | Opportunity | Design-Out | TPM |
|-----------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|-------------|------------|-----|
| **Trigger** | Failure | Calendar/Usage | Condition reading | Plant shutdown | Engineering | Cultural |
| **Cost Profile** | High at failure | Steady recurring | Capex + low Opex | Low (piggyback) | One-time high | Variable |
| **Downtime** | Unplanned (long) | Planned (medium) | Planned (minimal) | None (concurrent) | None post-design | Reduced |
| **Manpower Skill** | Low | Medium | High | Medium | High (R&D) | Multi-skill |
| **Data Dependency** | None | Low | Very high | Low | High | Medium |
| **Best For** | Non-critical, low-cost | Regulated, critical | High-value, critical | Sub-systems of larger plant | Chronic failure modes | Entire plant |
| **Industry 4.0 Fit** | None | Low | Excellent | Low | Medium | Excellent |

---

## 2.6 Condition Monitoring (CM) – The Core Concept

### 2.6.1 Formal Definition
**Condition Monitoring** is the continuous or periodic measurement and interpretation of *characteristic parameters* of a machine (vibration, temperature, acoustic emission, lubricant condition, etc.) to assess its operational state and predict impending failure.

It is the **information-gathering engine** that drives **Predictive Maintenance**.

### 2.6.2 The "Why" Behind Condition Monitoring
The fundamental engineering premise is the **P-F Curve (Potential Failure Curve)**, which describes the time between *first detectable indication of a failure* (P-point) and *functional failure* (F-point).

$$
t_{P \to F} = \text{P-F Interval}
$$

This interval may range from **days** (bearing degradation) to **years** (gear wear). The longer the P-F interval, the more *predictable* the failure becomes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Draw the P-F curve in any PdM-related question. Examiners award marks specifically for this visual aid.

### 2.6.3 Condition Monitoring Objectives
1. Detect incipient faults *before* functional failure
2. Diagnose root cause and severity
3. Forecast remaining useful life (RUL)
4. Verify the effectiveness of repairs
5. Provide data for reliability-centered maintenance (RCM) decisions
6. Benchmark machine performance against design intent

---

## 2.7 Monitoring Systems – Offline vs. Online

### 2.7.1 Offline (Portable) Monitoring

**Offline monitoring** is performed by an engineer or technician who **brings portable instruments** to the machine at scheduled intervals (e.g., monthly, quarterly).

**Architecture:**
- Handheld data collector
- Accelerometer (magnet-mounted) attached to bearing housing
- Data downloaded to software for analysis
- Reports generated by analyst

**Key Features:**
- Human intervention required
- Periodic snapshots (not continuous)
- Lower capital cost
- Suitable for low-to-medium criticality assets
- Analysis is *post-facto* (not real-time)

**Merits:**
- **Lowest capital cost** — no permanent instrumentation
- **Flexibility** — one instrument can be used for hundreds of machines
- **Suitable for route-based inspections** in large plants
- **No need for plant modification** — clip-on sensors

**Demerits:**
- **Time gap between readings** — fast-evolving faults may be missed
- **Inter-operator variability** — placement and force on probe affect data
- **No real-time alerting** — failure between routes is undetected
- **High manpower** — frequent walkarounds
- **Data not available in central control room**

### 2.7.2 Online (Permanent/In-Situ) Monitoring

**Online monitoring** uses **permanently installed sensors** connected continuously to a data acquisition system, often integrated with the plant's **Distributed Control System (DCS)** or **SCADA**.

**Architecture:**
- Permanently mounted accelerometers, RTDs, ultrasonic probes
- Continuous signal conditioning
- Real-time data streaming to server
- Automated alerts via email/SMS/DCS alarms
- Cloud/edge analytics

**Key Features:**
- Always-on, 24/7 surveillance
- Real-time alarm generation
- Suitable for **critical, high-speed, hazardous, or remote** assets
- Foundation of **Industry 4.0** smart factories

**Merits:**
- **Continuous protection** — no time gap
- **Real-time alerts** enable immediate response
- **Trend data** — long-term degradation visible
- **Operator-friendly** — automated diagnostics
- **Enables predictive analytics** with machine learning

**Demerits:**
- **High capital investment** — sensors, cabling, servers, software
- **Sensor reliability** — sensors themselves can fail
- **Complex installation** — often requires plant shutdown
- **Cybersecurity exposure** — networked systems are vulnerable
- **Maintenance of monitoring system** becomes a meta-maintenance problem

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
|-------------------|-----------|-----|
| **Overall Equipment Effectiveness** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Where $A$ = Availability, $P$ = Performance, $Q$ = Quality |
| **Availability** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$ | Fraction of time asset is operational |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\sum t_{\text{uptime}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Reliability metric |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum t_{\text{repair}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Maintainability metric |
| **Reliability Function** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential distribution (constant failure rate) |
| **Hazard Rate** | $\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Instantaneous failure rate |
| **P-F Interval** | $t_{P \to F}$ | Window for predictive intervention |
| **Bathtub Curve Region** | Infant mortality / Useful life / Wear-out | Predicts PM timing |
| **Condition Index** | $\text{CI} = \frac{\text{Threshold} - \text{Current Reading}}{\text{Threshold}}$ | Normalized wear measure |
| **Weibull Shape Parameter** | $\beta > 1$ indicates wear-out | Determines if PM is suitable |
| **Vibration Severity (ISO 10816)** | $V_{\text{RMS}}$ in $\text{mm/s}$ | Bearing/machine health |
| **Cost of Unplanned Downtime** | $C_{\text{UD}} = P_{\text{loss}} \times t_{\text{down}} + C_{\text{rep}}$ | Justifies PdM investment |
| **Life Cycle Cost (LCC)** | $\text{LCC} = C_{\text{acq}} + C_{\text{op}} + C_{\text{maint}} - C_{\text{disposal}}$ | Strategic decision metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Many questions test the student's ability to *select* the appropriate strategy for a *given* scenario. Practice writing 2-3 line justifications linking failure mode → strategy.

---

## 2.9 Techniques and Methods of Condition Monitoring

The KTU syllabus (Module 2) specifically requires coverage of the following techniques. Each is summarized in the matrix below; the next section provides deeper derivation/analysis.

| # | Technique | Parameter Measured | Typical Application | Fault Detected |
|---|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Vibration Analysis** | Acceleration / velocity | Rotating machinery | Imbalance, misalignment, bearing defect, gear fault |
| 2 | **Acoustic Emission (AE)** | High-frequency stress waves | Pressure vessels, pipelines | Crack propagation, leak |
| 3 | **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** | High-frequency sound reflection | Welds, thick sections | Internal cracks, voids |
| 4 | **Infrared Thermography (IRT)** | Surface temperature | Electrical, refractory | Hot spots, insulation failure |
| 5 | **Oil / Lubricant Analysis** | Particle count, viscosity, wear metals | Engines, gearboxes | Wear, contamination, degradation |
| 6 | **Performance Trending** | Pressure, flow, temperature, power | Process equipment | Efficiency loss |
| 7 | **Motor Current Signature Analysis (MCSA)** | Current spectrum | Electric motors | Rotor bar, air-gap, load issues |
| 8 | **Visual / Borescope Inspection** | Optical image | Internal cavities | Corrosion, cracking, deposits |
| 9 | **Eddy Current Testing** | Electromagnetic response | Tubes, conductive surfaces | Surface cracks |
| 10 | **Radiography** | X-ray / gamma attenuation | Welds, castings | Internal defects |

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the P-F Curve Mathematical Model

The P-F (Potential Failure to Functional Failure) curve is the conceptual foundation of condition monitoring. We model the degradation as a function of time.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 – Define the State Variables
Let $C(t)$ = condition parameter (e.g., vibration velocity) at time $t$.
Let $C_{\text{threshold}}$ = alarm threshold (P-point condition).
Let $C_{\text{failure}}$ = functional failure condition (F-point).

### 3.1.2 Step 2 – Define the Failure Boundary
Functional failure occurs when:

$$
C(t) \geq C_{\text{failure}}
$$

Potential failure is detected when:

$$
C(t) \geq C_{\text{threshold}}
$$

The P-F interval is the time between these two events:

$$
t_{P \to F} = t_{F} - t_{P}
$$

where $t_{P}$ and $t_{F}$ are the times at which $C(t)$ crosses the threshold and failure levels, respectively.

### 3.1.3 Step 3 – Model Degradation as a Power Law
For most wear processes (rolling contact fatigue, abrasive wear), degradation follows a power law:

$$
C(t) = C_{0} + K \cdot t^{n}
$$

where:
- $C_{0}$ = initial condition value (background noise floor)
- $K$ = degradation rate constant
- $n$ = time exponent (typically $1 < n < 4$ for fatigue-driven faults)

### 3.1.4 Step 4 – Solve for P-F Interval
Setting $C(t_{P}) = C_{\text{threshold}}$:

$$
C_{\text{threshold}} = C_{0} + K \cdot t_{P}^{n}
$$

$$
t_{P} = \left( \frac{C_{\text{threshold}} - C_{0}}{K} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}}
$$

Similarly, setting $C(t_{F}) = C_{\text{failure}}$:

$$
t_{F} = \left( \frac{C_{\text{failure}} - C_{0}}{K} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 – Subtract to Obtain P-F Interval

$$
\boxed{t_{P \to F} = \left( \frac{C_{\text{failure}} - C_{0}}{K} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - \left( \frac{C_{\text{threshold}} - C_{0}}{K} \right)^{\frac{1}{n}}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step 6 – Engineering Interpretation
- The **larger the gap** between $C_{\text{threshold}}$ and $C_{\text{failure}}$, the **longer the warning time**.
- The **larger the exponent** $n$, the **shorter** the warning time (sudden failure).
- A **low $K$** (slow degradation) gives a long P-F interval → ideal for PdM.
- A **high $K$** (fast degradation) gives a short P-F interval → may force PM intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Implication:** A machine designed with *low $K$* and *high $C_{\text{failure}} - C_{\text{threshold}}$* is "**condition-monitoring-friendly**". This is a design-for-maintainability (DFM) consideration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Availability and OEE

### 3.2.1 Step 1 – Define Downtime Categories
Total calendar time in a period:

$$
T_{\text{calendar}} = T_{\text{uptime}} + T_{\text{downtime}}
$$

Where:

$$
T_{\text{downtime}} = T_{\text{PM}} + T_{\text{CM}} + T_{\text{setup}} + T_{\text{breakdowns}} + T_{\text{changeover}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 – Compute Availability

$$
A = \frac{T_{\text{uptime}}}{T_{\text{calendar}}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 – Performance Efficiency
Actual vs. design cycle time:

$$
P = \frac{T_{\text{theoretical production}}}{T_{\text{uptime}}} = \frac{\text{Actual output} \times t_{\text{cycle}}}{\text{Uptime}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 – Quality Rate

$$
Q = \frac{\text{Good units produced}}{\text{Total units produced}}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 – Overall Equipment Effectiveness

$$
\boxed{\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **World-Class Benchmark:** OEE $\geq 85\%$ is considered *world-class* in discrete manufacturing (85% × 95% × 100%).

### 3.2.6 Worked Example
A pump operates for **7,200 hours** in a quarter, experiences **3 breakdowns** totaling **80 hours** of unplanned downtime, plus **40 hours** of planned PM. Designed capacity is **100 m³/h**, but actual production is **540,000 m³**. Defective output is **5%**.

**Step 1:** Uptime = $7200 - 80 - 40 = 7080$ hours

**Step 2:** Availability $A = \frac{7080}{7200} = 0.9833 = 98.33\%$

**Step 3:** Theoretical production = $7080 \times 100 = 708{,}000$ m³

**Step 4:** Performance $P = \frac{540{,}000}{708{,}000} = 0.7627 = 76.27\%$

**Step 5:** Quality $Q = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 = 95\%$

**Step 6:** 

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.9833 \times 0.7627 \times 0.95 = 0.7125 = 71.25\%
$$

**Interpretation:** This asset is **below world-class** (85%) but **above the typical manufacturing average** (60%). The biggest loss source is **Performance** — investigate speed losses, idling, and minor stops.

---

## 3.3 Reliability Function Derivation (Exponential Model)

### 3.3.1 Step 1 – Start with the Failure Density
Let $f(t)$ be the probability density function of time-to-failure, with cumulative distribution $F(t)$:

$$
F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### 3.3.2 Step 2 – Define Reliability
Reliability is the probability of survival beyond time $t$:

$$
R(t) = 1 - F(t) = \int_{t}^{\infty} f(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 – Apply Constant Hazard Rate (Useful Life Region)
In the useful life of the bathtub curve, hazard rate $\lambda$ is constant. The differential equation for survival is:

$$
\frac{dR(t)}{dt} = -\lambda R(t)
$$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 – Solve the ODE

$$
\int \frac{dR}{R} = -\int \lambda \, dt
$$

$$
\ln R(t) = -\lambda t + C
$$

With boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$
\boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}
$$

### 3.3.5 Step 5 – Mean Time Between Failures

$$
\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### 3.3.6 Step 6 – Hazard Rate Relation

$$
\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

For exponential distribution:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Use:** The exponential model applies only in the **useful-life region** of the bathtub curve. For **infant mortality**, use the Weibull with $\beta < 1$; for **wear-out**, use $\beta > 1$.

---

## 3.4 Condition Monitoring Decision Logic – Analytical Matrix

The choice of condition monitoring technique is governed by a **decision matrix** that links fault type, machine type, and economic feasibility.

| Fault Type | Dominant Frequency Range | Best Technique | Sensor Type | Cost Index |
|------------|--------------------------|----------------|-------------|-----------|
| Imbalance | 1× running speed | Vibration (velocity) | Accelerometer | Low |
| Misalignment | 2× running speed | Vibration (axial) | Accelerometer | Low |
| Bearing Defect (outer race) | BPFO high-freq | Vibration (envelope) | High-freq accelerometer | Medium |
| Bearing Defect (inner race) | BPFI high-freq | Vibration (envelope) | High-freq accelerometer | Medium |
| Gear Tooth Fault | GMF | Vibration (gating) | Accelerometer + tachometer | Medium |
| Cavitation | Broadband 0–20 kHz | Acoustic emission | AE sensor | Medium |
| Lubricant Degradation | Chemical | Oil analysis | Spectrometer, viscometer | High |
| Electrical Hot Spot | 0.1–1 Hz thermal | Thermography | IR camera | Medium |
| Rotor Bar Crack | 2× slip frequency | MCSA | Current clamp | Low |
| Crack Propagation | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | Acoustic emission | AE sensor | High |

### 3.4.1 Frequency Derivation for Bearing Defects
For a rolling element bearing with shaft frequency $f_{s}$, ball diameter $d$, pitch diameter $D$, contact angle $\alpha$, number of rolling elements $N$:

**Ball Pass Frequency Outer Race (BPFO):**

$$
f_{\text{BPFO}} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot f_{s} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{D} \cos\alpha \right)
$$

**Ball Pass Frequency Inner Race (BPFI):**

$$
f_{\text{BPFI}} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot f_{s} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{d}{D} \cos\alpha \right)
$$

**Ball Spin Frequency (BSF):**

$$
f_{\text{BSF}} = \frac{D}{2d} \cdot f_{s} \cdot \left[1 - \left(\frac{d}{D} \cos\alpha \right)^{2} \right]
$$

**Cage / Fundamental Train Frequency (FTF):**

$$
f_{\text{FTF}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot f_{s} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{d}{D} \cos\alpha \right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You are not expected to *derive* these in an exam, but you **must** state them in a question on bearing vibration analysis. Label a spectrum showing BPFO, BPFI, and BSF peaks.

---

## 3.5 Vibration Severity – ISO 10816 Standard

The ISO 10816 standard classifies machine vibration into four zones based on RMS velocity:

| Zone | RMS Velocity Range (mm/s) | Classification | Action |
|------|---------------------------|----------------|--------|
| **A** | $V \leq 1.4$ | Good | Continue normal operation |
| **B** | $1.4 < V \leq 2.8$ | Acceptable | Plan short-term inspection |
| **C** | $2.8 < V \leq 4.5$ | Tolerable short-term | Schedule maintenance |
| **D** | $V > 4.5$ | Unacceptable | Immediate shutdown |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above is for **medium-sized machines** (Class II). For small machines, the limits are 2.25× higher; for large machines, they are 0.75×.

**Worked Example:** A 200 kW motor is measured at $V_{\text{RMS}} = 3.2$ mm/s.
- Zone: **C**
- Action: **Schedule maintenance within weeks**

---

## 3.6 Thermography Analysis – Stefan-Boltzmann Basis

The temperature measured by an IR camera is governed by the Stefan-Boltzmann law:

$$
P_{\text{emitted}} = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}
$$

where:
- $P_{\text{emitted}}$ = radiated power per unit area (W/m²)
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (0 to 1)
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$ (Stefan-Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Engineering Implication:** A 10% misjudgment in $\varepsilon$ causes significant error in absolute temperature. **Always** calibrate using a known reference (e.g., electrical tape with $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture – Maintenance Strategy Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start: Asset Criticality Analysis] --> node2{Criticality Level?}
    node2 -->|High| node3[Apply RCM Analysis]
    node2 -->|Medium| node4[Apply CBM PdM]
    node2 -->|Low| node5[Apply PM Schedule]
    node2 -->|Very Low| node6[Apply Corrective BM]

    node3 --> nodeA{Failure Mode Detectable?}
    nodeA -->|Yes| nodeB[Online Continuous Monitoring]
    nodeA -->|No| nodeC[Scheduled PM with Overhaul]

    node4 --> nodeD{Machine Type?}
    nodeD -->|Rotating| nodeE[Vibration and Oil Analysis]
    nodeD -->|Electrical| nodeF[Thermography and MCSA]
    nodeD -->|Static Pressure| nodeG[UT and AE]

    node5 --> nodeH[Calendar-Based PM]
    node6 --> nodeI[Run to Failure Strategy]
```

## 4.2 Condition Monitoring System Architecture – Online vs Offline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MACHINE[Physical Machine]
        M1[Bearing]
        M2[Gearbox]
        M3[Coupling]
    end

    subgraph OFFLINE[Offline Portable System]
        O1[Portable Accelerometer]
        O2[Handheld Data Collector]
        O3[Laptop Analysis]
        O1 --> O2
        O2 --> O3
    end

    subgraph ONLINE[Online Permanent System]
        P1[Fixed Accelerometers]
        P2[RTD Temperature Sensors]
        P3[AE and UT Probes]
        P4[Signal Conditioner]
        P5[DAS Data Acquisition Server]
        P6[SCADA or DCS]
        P7[Cloud Analytics and ML]
        P1 --> P4
        P2 --> P4
        P3 --> P4
        P4 --> P5
        P5 --> P6
        P5 --> P7
    end

    MACHINE --> OFFLINE
    MACHINE --> ONLINE
```

## 4.3 The P-F Curve – Visual Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Healthy State] -->|P Point| nodeB(Potential Failure Detected)
    nodeB -->|PF Interval| nodeC(F) Functional Failure
    nodeB --> nodeD[Schedule Maintenance]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Restore to Healthy]
    nodeE --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeF[Emergency Repair]
    nodeF --> nodeE
```

## 4.4 The Bathtub Curve – Hazard Rate vs Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time T Zero] --> B[Infant Mortality: Lambda High Decreasing]
    B --> C[Useful Life: Lambda Constant Minimum]
    C --> D[Wear Out: Lambda Increasing]
    D --> E[End of Life Failure]

    subgraph STRATEGY[Recommended Strategy per Region]
        S1[Infant: Burn In and RRT]
        S2[Useful: Corrective Acceptable]
        S3[Wear Out: PM or PdM Scheduled]
    end
```

## 4.5 TPM Eight Pillars – Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TPM[TPM Master Framework]
    TPM --> P1[Autonomous Maintenance Jishu Hozen]
    TPM --> P2[Planned Maintenance]
    TPM --> P3[Quality Maintenance]
    TPM --> P4[Focused Improvement Kobetsu Kaizen]
    TPM --> P5[Early Management]
    TPM --> P6[Education and Training]
    TPM --> P7[Office TPM]
    TPM --> P8[Safety Health and Environment]
    P1 --> ZL[Zero Losses: Breakdown Defect Accident Waste]
    P2 --> ZL
    P3 --> ZL
    P4 --> ZL
    P5 --> ZL
    P6 --> ZL
    P7 --> ZL
    P8 --> ZL
```

## 4.6 Condition Monitoring Decision Tree – By Symptom

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Symptom Observed] --> Q1{What is changing?}
    Q1 -->|Vibration Increases| V1[Use Vibration Spectrum Analysis]
    Q1 -->|Temperature Rises| T1[Use Infrared Thermography]
    Q1 -->|Pressure Drops| P1[Use Ultrasonic Leak Detection]
    Q1 -->|Oil Color Changes| O1[Use Spectrometric Oil Analysis]
    Q1 -->|Noise Emission| N1[Use Acoustic Emission]

    V1 --> V2{Match Peak to Fault Frequency}
    V2 -->|One X Running Speed| V3[Imbalance Fault]
    V2 -->|Two X Running Speed| V4[Misalignment Fault]
    V2 -->|BPFO or BPFI| V5[Bearing Defect]
    V2 -->|GMF| V6[Gear Fault]
```
