<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEIET613]
**Differentiate between Preventive Maintenance and Predictive Maintenance with two key distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (PdM) |
|--------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Trigger** | Calendar time or usage hours (e.g., every 6 months or 5000 hours) | Measured condition parameter (e.g., vibration level exceeds ISO 10816 Zone B) |
| **Cost Profile** | Steady recurring cost regardless of need | Variable cost driven by actual condition |
| **Data Need** | Low — only running hours | High — sensors, analyzers, software |
| **Component Life Utilization** | Suboptimal — parts changed before end of useful life | Optimal — parts used to full potential |
| **Failure Between Intervals** | Possible (residual risk) | Minimal (continuous monitoring) |

> **Key Distinguishing Feature 1:** PM is *time-triggered* whereas PdM is *condition-triggered*.
> **Key Distinguishing Feature 2:** PdM maximizes part utilization; PM may cause early replacement.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEIET613]
**State any three condition monitoring techniques used for rotating machinery and the parameter measured by each.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

1. **Vibration Analysis** — Measures *mechanical vibration* (acceleration, velocity, displacement). Detects imbalance, misalignment, bearing and gear faults.

2. **Oil Analysis (Lubricant Analysis)** — Measures *viscosity, particle count, ferrous wear debris concentration* and spectrographic element concentration. Detects wear, contamination, and lubricant degradation.

3. **Thermography (Infrared Imaging)** — Measures *surface temperature distribution*. Detects overheating in bearings, electrical joints, and refractory linings.

*(Alternative valid answers: Acoustic Emission, Motor Current Signature Analysis, Performance Trending.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – ESE Internal Choice Format)

### Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the features, merits, and demerits of **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**. Discuss any **five of the eight pillars** of TPM in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **P-F Curve (Potential Failure Curve)**. Discuss how the P-F interval influences the choice between preventive and predictive maintenance. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution – (a)

**Definition:** TPM is a holistic, culture-driven maintenance philosophy that aims for **zero breakdowns, zero defects, zero accidents, and zero waste** through the active participation of *all employees* — operators, maintenance crew, engineers, and management.

**Features of TPM:**
- Operator-driven daily maintenance (cleaning, lubrication, inspection)
- Cross-functional small group activities (kaizen)
- Integration of maintenance into manufacturing strategy
- Focus on *preventing* losses rather than *fixing* failures
- Continuous improvement (PDCA cycle) embedded
- Multi-skilling of workforce
- Documented master plan with measurable KPIs (OEE, MTBF, MTTR)

**Merits of TPM:**
- Increases OEE to world-class levels (85%+)
- Reduces unplanned downtime by 70–90%
- Builds ownership culture — operators know their machines
- Lowers spare parts inventory (less breakdown)
- Improves safety and morale
- Enhances product quality

**Demerits of TPM:**
- High initial training cost
- Slow implementation (3–5 years to mature)
- Requires strong management commitment
- Resistance to change from traditional workforce
- Difficult to sustain without continuous top-down push

**The Five Selected Pillars:**

**1. Jishu Hozen (Autonomous Maintenance):**
Operators perform daily tasks like cleaning, lubricating, tightening, and visual inspection. Goal: maintain baseline condition and detect early deviations. Done in **7 steps** (initial cleaning → eliminate contamination sources → set standards → inspection training → autonomous inspection → standardization → continuous improvement).

**2. Planned Maintenance:**
Maintenance department schedules time-based, predictive, and preventive tasks. Uses CMMS, RCM analysis, and condition monitoring. Aim: zero breakdown through planned interventions.

**3. Quality Maintenance (QM):**
Ensures that machine condition *itself* produces zero-defect products. Uses P-M analysis (Phenomenon–Mechanism), 4M analysis (Man, Machine, Material, Method), and quality-condition matrices.

**4. Kobetsu Kaizen (Focused Improvement):**
Project-based, small-group activities targeting specific losses (e.g., changeover reduction, defect elimination). Typically uses **DMAIC/DMADV** frameworks or **A3 problem-solving**.

**5. Education & Training:**
Builds multi-skilled workforce through systematic training matrices (skill level vs. task). Aligns employee capability with TPM requirements.

*(Three pillars omitted for brevity, but must be named briefly: Office TPM, Safety/Health/Environment, Early Management.)*

---

#### Model Solution – (b)

**P-F Curve – Sketch:**

```
        C (Condition Parameter)
        ^
        |                            /  (degradation)
   C_f  | - - - - - - - - - - - - /   (Functional Failure Level)
        |                       /
        |                      /
   C_p  | - - - - - - - - - /
        |                  /
   C_0  |________________/________________> t (time)
        0                t_p               t_f
        |---PF Interval------------------|
```

**Description:**
- The P-point marks the first *detectable* indication of failure using the chosen technique.
- The F-point marks the *functional* failure where the machine can no longer perform its required function.
- The time between P and F is the **P-F interval**.

**Choice of Strategy Based on P-F Interval:**

- **P-F interval is long (months to years):** Predictive Maintenance is feasible and economic. Sensors can detect early signs and provide ample time to plan intervention. Example: gear wear, slow bearing degradation.

- **P-F interval is short (hours to days):** Predictive is risky; Preventive Maintenance at a frequency less than the P-F interval is preferred. Example: certain electrical winding failures.

- **P-F interval is extremely short / undetectable:** Only *design-out* or *preventive replacement* works. Example: brittle fracture, sudden rupture.

**Key Engineering Point:**
The longer the P-F interval, the more *condition-friendly* the failure mode, and the more cost-effective PdM becomes. The technician's task is to **extend the P-F interval** by enhancing detection sensitivity (better sensors, lower threshold).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]

**(a)** Compare **offline and online condition monitoring systems** in terms of architecture, data continuity, cost, manpower, and suitability. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the principle, working, and application of **vibration analysis** and **infrared thermography** as condition monitoring techniques. Mention the standard reference for vibration severity. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution – (a)

**Architecture:**
- **Offline:** Portable accelerometer + handheld data collector. Sensor is temporarily clamped to the machine during route-based inspections.
- **Online:** Permanently mounted accelerometers + RTDs + AE probes + signal conditioner + data acquisition server + DCS/SCADA integration.

**Data Continuity:**
- **Offline:** Periodic snapshots (e.g., monthly). Data gap between readings can miss fast faults.
- **Online:** Continuous, real-time streaming. Captures transient events and gradual trends.

**Cost:**
- **Offline:** Low capital cost (one instrument, multiple machines). High manpower cost for routes.
- **Online:** High capital cost (sensors, cabling, server, software license). Low operating cost.

**Manpower:**
- **Offline:** Trained technician walks the route, applies probe consistently, downloads data, and an analyst interprets.
- **Online:** Initial setup + occasional calibration. Automated diagnostics. Operator only acts on alarm.

**Suitability:**
- **Offline:** Low-to-medium criticality; large plant population; budget-constrained; mature workforce.
- **Online:** High-criticality assets (turbines, compressors, large motors); hazardous environments; remote assets (pump stations, offshore); Industry 4.0 smart factory integration.

**Comparative Summary Table:**

| Criterion | Offline | Online |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Data Continuity | Periodic | Continuous |
| Capital Cost | Low | High |
| Operating Cost | High (labor) | Low (automated) |
| Real-time Alert | No | Yes |
| Skill Required | Mid (technician) | Mid-High (engineer) |
| Data Storage | Local (laptop) | Centralized (server/cloud) |
| Best For | General fleet | Critical machines |

---

#### Model Solution – (b)

**1. Vibration Analysis:**

**Principle:** Most rotating machinery faults (imbalance, misalignment, looseness, bearing defect, gear fault) generate **characteristic vibration signatures** at specific frequencies proportional to the running speed and geometry.

**Working:**
- An **accelerometer** (piezoelectric, typically 1–10 kHz range for general, >20 kHz for bearing) is mounted on the bearing housing.
- The signal is conditioned (charge amplifier, anti-aliasing filter).
- The data is digitized and **Fourier-transformed** (FFT) to convert time-domain signal to frequency-domain spectrum.
- Vibration peaks at characteristic frequencies are compared with known fault signatures:
  - 1× RPM → Imbalance
  - 2× RPM → Misalignment
  - BPFO/BPFI → Bearing defect
  - GMF → Gear mesh fault
- **Severity** is quantified using RMS velocity in mm/s (ISO 10816) or vibration g-level for high-frequency bearing monitoring (envelope analysis).

**Standard:** **ISO 10816-3** (or ISO 10816-7 for gearboxes) defines four zones (A, B, C, D) for vibration severity evaluation of industrial machines.

**Application:** Pumps, motors, compressors, gearboxes, turbines, fans, machine tool spindles.

**2. Infrared Thermography (IRT):**

**Principle:** Every object with temperature above absolute zero radiates electromagnetic energy. Peak wavelength (in $\mu$m) follows **Wien's displacement law:**

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T}
$$

where $b = 2898 \, \mu\text{m·K}$ is Wien's displacement constant. At industrial temperatures, peak emission is in the **infrared (8–14 $\mu$m)** range.

**Working:**
- An **IR camera** focuses infrared radiation from the object onto a microbolometer or quantum detector array.
- The detector converts IR energy into electrical signals proportional to radiance.
- Software applies the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** ($P = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$) to compute surface temperature.
- Emissivity correction is applied using a reference (e.g., black tape with $\varepsilon = 0.95$).
- **Thermograms** (false-color images) reveal temperature gradients.
- Hot spots indicate **overheating** due to electrical resistance, friction, refractory failure, or insulation breakdown.

**Application:**
- Electrical panels, busbars, switchgear (detect loose connections)
- Motor bearings and housings
- Refractory linings of furnaces
- Steam traps, heat exchangers
- Solar panels, building envelopes

**Advantages:** Non-contact, large-area scan, fast (real-time), safe for live electrical systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse TPM with TQM.** TPM is *maintenance* focused; TQM is *quality* focused. Examiners deduct marks for interchange.
> 2. **Always label axes** in the P-F curve sketch. Many students lose 1–2 marks for missing axis labels or units.
> 3. **For vibration analysis**, mention **ISO 10816** explicitly — it is a guaranteed 1-mark fetcher.
> 4. **For thermography**, state the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant value** and **emissivity concept** — the examiner expects physics depth.
> 5. **Comparison questions must use a TABLE** — prose comparisons lose marks for lack of structure.
> 6. **Avoid one-liner definitions.** Even in 3-mark questions, a 2-3 line explanation with one example fetches full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap – Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap – Read Twice Before Exam**

### Maintenance Strategies (Module 2 Core)
- ☐ **Corrective Maintenance** – Run-to-failure; lowest upfront cost; highest downtime cost
- ☐ **Preventive Maintenance** – Time/usage based; reduces unplanned stops; may waste part life
- ☐ **Predictive Maintenance** – Condition-based; optimal part life; needs sensors + skill
- ☐ **Opportunity Maintenance** – Piggybacked during planned shutdowns
- ☐ **Design-Out Maintenance** – Eliminate the failure mode at design stage
- ☐ **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** – Cultural; 8 pillars; zero-loss philosophy

### Condition Monitoring
- ☐ **Objective:** Detect incipient faults, predict failure, extend life, improve safety
- ☐ **P-F Curve:** $t_{P \to F}$ = P-F Interval; longer = more PdM-friendly
- ☐ **Bathtub Curve:** Infant mortality, useful life (constant $\lambda$), wear-out

### Monitoring Systems
- ☐ **Offline (Portable):** Handheld, periodic, low capex, route-based
- ☐ **Online (Permanent):** Continuous, real-time alarm, Industry 4.0 enabled

### Techniques & Methods
- ☐ **Vibration Analysis** – Accelerometer, FFT spectrum, ISO 10816 zones
- ☐ **Acoustic Emission** – High-freq stress waves, crack growth detection
- ☐ **Ultrasonic Testing** – Reflection-based flaw detection
- ☐ **Infrared Thermography** – $\varepsilon \sigma T^4$, IR camera, hot spot detection
- ☐ **Oil Analysis** – Spectrometric + ferrography, wear debris
- ☐ **MCSA** – Motor current, rotor bar faults
- ☐ **Performance Trending** – Pressure/flow/temperature for efficiency
- ☐ **Eddy Current / Radiography** – For static structural integrity

### Key Formulas
- ☐ $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$
- ☐ $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT})$
- ☐ $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
- ☐ BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF expressions
- ☐ $P_{\text{emitted}} = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$

### TPM Eight Pillars (Must Memorize)
- ☐ Jishu Hozen, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, Kobetsu Kaizen, Early Management, Education & Training, Office TPM, Safety/Health/Environment

### Common Mistake Avoidance
- ☐ Do NOT confuse PM (preventive) with PdM (predictive) — they are *different* strategies
- ☐ Do NOT say "TPM is just PM" — TPM is a *philosophy* involving all employees
- ☐ Do NOT ignore the cost of *unplanned* downtime when justifying PdM
- ☐ Do NOT apply exponential reliability in the wear-out zone — use Weibull
- ☐ Do NOT forget to mention *threshold values* when discussing ISO 10816

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Centred Maintenance (RCM) and Its Advantages, Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) - Steps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability Centred Maintenance (RCM) and Its Advantages, Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) - Steps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Reliability Centred Maintenance (RCM)

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability Centred Maintenance (RCM)** is a systematic engineering framework used to determine the maintenance strategy for each physical asset in an operating system, formulated to preserve the *intended function* of the asset while ensuring *safety*, *operational readiness*, and *economy of resources*. The strategy is selected based on the asset's failure modes, failure consequences, and the effectiveness of preventive tasks.

The most authoritative formal standard is **SAE JA1011 (Evaluation Criteria for RCM Processes)**, which is referenced by international reliability bodies. According to this standard, RCM answers **seven foundational questions** for every asset before prescribing a maintenance action.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Car Owner" Intuition

Imagine you own an old car. You do not service *every* part the same way.
- The **brake pads** are safety-critical → you inspect them every 10,000 km and replace them before they fail.
- The **car radio** is a comfort feature → you only fix it *after* it fails (run-to-failure).
- The **engine oil** degrades predictably → scheduled replacement regardless of condition.
- The **tires** have hidden failure modes (slow leak) → on-condition monitoring (TPMS).

**RCM does the EXACT SAME logic** for industrial equipment — but uses engineering rigor, FMEA logic, and consequence categorization rather than gut feeling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** RCM is NOT just preventive maintenance. It is a *decision-making process* that may recommend **no scheduled maintenance at all** if failure is tolerable, inexpensive, and does not impact safety.

---

## 1.2 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)

### Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a deductive (top-down) reliability and safety analysis technique developed by H.A. Watson of Bell Telephone Laboratories in **1961** for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman missile program. It uses Boolean logic and a tree-diagram representation to map the combinations of basic (low-level) component failures that lead to an undesired top-level system failure event, called the **TOP EVENT**.

The output of FTA includes:
- Qualitative analysis: **Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)** — the smallest combinations of basic events that cause system failure.
- Quantitative analysis: numerical probability of the top event using Boolean algebra.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Trip Cancellation" Intuition

Imagine a family trip gets cancelled. You ask **"WHY?"**:
- If **Dad OR Mom** says no → trip cancelled (OR gate).
- If **Dad is sick AND Mom is busy AND No backup driver** → trip cancelled (AND gate).

You draw this logic like a tree, with "Trip Cancelled" at the top, gates in the middle, and basic reasons at the bottom. This is precisely a **Fault Tree**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FTAs are deductive** — they start from the undesired event and reason **backwards**. This is the opposite of an **inductive** method like FMEA, which reasons forward from component failures to system effects.

### Key Metrics (Highlight Constants)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | **MTBF** | Expected operating time between two consecutive failures |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | **MTTR** | Expected downtime to restore a failed component |
| **Availability** | **A** | Fraction of time system is operational |
| **Unavailability** | **U** | Fraction of time system is failed |
| **Failure Rate** | **λ (lambda)** | Failures per unit time (typically per hour or per cycle) |

> [!WARNING]
> Students frequently confuse **MTBF** with **service life**. MTBF is a *statistical mean* and not a guaranteed lifetime; many components will fail before 1 MTBF.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 RCM — The Seven Foundational Questions (SAE JA1011)

The RCM process is driven by answering these questions for **every** significant item:

1. **What are the functions and associated desired performance standards of the asset in its present operating context?** (Function definition)
2. **In what ways does it fail to fulfil its functions?** (Functional failures)
3. **What causes each functional failure?** (Failure modes — typically from FMEA)
4. **What happens when each failure occurs?** (Failure consequences)
5. **In what way does each failure matter?** (Consequence categorization)
6. **What can be done to prevent or predict each failure?** (Proactive tasks and default actions)
7. **What should be done if a suitable preventive task cannot be found?** (Default actions — run-to-failure, redesign, one-shot changes)

### RCM Consequence Categories

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently asks these categories — memorize the four:**

| Category | Description | Maintenance Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Hidden Failure** | Failure not evident to operating crew; defeats safety system | Must do scheduled inspection |
| **Safety / Environmental** | Failure causes injury, fatality, or environmental damage | Mandatory scheduled task |
| **Operational** | Failure causes loss of primary function / production | On-condition or scheduled |
| **Non-operational** | Failure causes only direct economic loss (repair cost) | Run-to-failure acceptable |

---

## 2.2 FTA — The Logical Structure

A Fault Tree has these structural layers (top to bottom):

- **Top Event** — the undesired system failure being analysed (single, well-defined event).
- **Intermediate Events** — sub-failures resulting from combinations of lower events.
- **Basic Events** — bottom-level component failures; treated as leaves of the tree.
- **Undeveloped Events** — events not analysed further due to insufficient data.

### The Two Logical Gates (Engineers Must Memorize)

**1. OR Gate** — output fails if **ANY** input fails.
- Boolean expression: $X = A \cup B \cup C$

**2. AND Gate** — output fails only if **ALL** inputs fail.
- Boolean expression: $X = A \cap B \cap C$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\mid` instead of `|` in tables to prevent markdown corruption.**

| Concept | Formula | Notes / Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **OR Gate Probability** | $P_{top} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(1 - P_{i}\right)$ | For independent parallel components (any one fails → system fails). Engineering: redundancy analysis |
| **AND Gate Probability** | $P_{top} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{i}$ | For series components (all must work — system fails if any single component fails in series sense). Engineering: minimal cut set probability |
| **Reliability (Series)** | $R_{s}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_{i}(t)$ | All components must survive for system success |
| **Reliability (Parallel)** | $R_{p}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left(1 - R_{i}(t)\right)$ | At least one component must survive |
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Foundational metric in any RCM cost-benefit decision |
| **Unavailability** | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{MTTR}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | Often used directly in FTA quantitative analysis |
| **Failure Rate (constant)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Used when basic event failure rate is known |
| **Cut Set** | $K_{i} = \{X_{i1}, X_{i2}, \ldots\}$ | Any combination of basic events causing top event |
| **Minimal Cut Set** | $K_{m} \subseteq K_{i}$ with no proper subset a cut set | Most important qualitative output of FTA |
| **Structural Importance** | $I^{\phi}_{i} = \dfrac{n_{\phi}^{\left(1\right)}_{i}}{2^{n-1}}$ | Ranking of basic events by occurrence in minimal cut sets |
| **Probability Importance** | $I^{P}_{i} = \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial q_{i}}$ | Sensitivity of top-event probability to change in $q_{i}$ |

---

## 2.4 Advantages of RCM (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> RCM advantages usually appear as **Part A (3-mark)** or as sub-parts in Part B. Memorize at least 5 with one-line engineering justifications.

1. **Increased operational safety** — direct identification of hidden and safety failures via FMEA integration.
2. **Improved equipment reliability and availability** — preserves inherent design reliability.
3. **Reduced maintenance cost** — eliminates unnecessary preventive tasks.
4. **Extended equipment life** — failure management prevents secondary damage.
5. **Better root-cause understanding** — FMEA-driven logic formalizes failure knowledge.
6. **Compliance with international standards** — ISO 55000, SAE JA1011, MIL-STD-2173 alignment.
7. **Improved maintenance planning & spares optimization** — uses reliability data to right-size inventory.
8. **Reduced unplanned downtime** — moves failures from reactive to known-scheduled windows.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** Boeing and Airbus use RCM-based MSG-3 logic for scheduled aircraft maintenance.
- **Power Generation:** Nuclear plant safety systems use RCM with FTA as the reliability backbone.
- **Manufacturing:** Automotive assembly line robots adopt RCM to manage safety-rated PLC systems.
- **Oil & Gas:** Subsea equipment, where *run-to-failure* is impossible, relies heavily on RCM analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 RCM Decision Diagram (Logical Flow Derivation)

The RCM decision process can be expressed as a stepwise logic tree that is required by SAE JA1011. We derive the decision paths below.

**Step 1:** Define system boundary and operating context.

**Step 2:** Identify functions and performance standards.

**Step 3:** Determine functional failures (FF).

**Step 4:** Identify failure modes for each FF (via FMEA).

**Step 5:** Classify consequences into H (Hidden), S (Safety), O (Operational), N (Non-operational).

**Step 6:** For each (Mode, Consequence) pair:
- If consequence is **Hidden** → apply scheduled inspection / failure-finding task.
- If consequence is **Safety / Environmental** → mandatory scheduled task or design change.
- If consequence is **Operational** → evaluate scheduled restoration or on-condition task.
- If consequence is **Non-operational** → combine LCC analysis; default to no scheduled action.

**Step 7:** If no effective proactive task exists → apply **default action** (redesign, run-to-failure, one-shot change).

---

## 3.2 FTA — Step-by-Step Procedure (KTU Board-Exam Standard)

The KTU textbook (and most KTU past papers) require the **seven canonical steps** of FTA:

**Step 1 — Define the Top Event.** A precise statement, e.g., *"Pump P-101 fails to deliver rated flow to the reactor cooling circuit."*

**Step 2 — Construct the Fault Tree.** Work from top event downward. Use **AND / OR gates** to express how lower events combine.

**Step 3 — Identify Basic Events.** These are the leaves — component failures, human errors, environmental events — assigned basic failure probability $q_{i}$.

**Step 4 — Identify Cut Sets.** A **Cut Set** is any combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event.

**Step 5 — Reduce to Minimal Cut Sets.** A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is a cut set such that no proper subset of it is itself a cut set. MCS are obtained by applying **Boolean absorption rules**:

$$A \cup (A \cap B) = A \qquad \text{(absorption)}$$

**Step 6 — Qualitative Evaluation.** Rank MCS by:
- **Order** — number of basic events (lower order = more critical).
- **Size** — single-point failures (size 1) are most critical.

**Step 7 — Quantitative Evaluation.** Compute top event probability $Q_{TOP}$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Derivation — FTA Probability Calculation

**Problem Statement (typical KTU Part B):** A system fails (top event) if either of two pumps fails (OR gate). Pump A fails with probability $q_{A} = 0.02$, Pump B with $q_{B} = 0.03$. Compute probability of system failure.

**Step 1 — Identify structure.** Top Event = "System Failure"; gate is OR; inputs are basic events A and B.

**Step 2 — Write Boolean expression.**

$$T = A \cup B$$

**Step 3 — Apply OR-gate probability formula.**

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - q_{A})(1 - q_{B})
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.02)(1 - 0.03) = 1 - (0.98)(0.97)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute product.**

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.9506 = 0.0494
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.** There is a **4.94% probability** the system fails within the design mission time. If this exceeds the safety target (typically $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-6}$ for safety systems), redundancy or improved components must be considered.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Two-Stage AND-then-OR Tree

**Problem Statement:** A plant has a control system failure as top event T. T occurs if **BOTH** sensor fails (event S, $q_{S} = 0.01$) **AND** either of two actuators fail (events $A_{1}$ with $q_{1} = 0.02$ or $A_{2}$ with $q_{2} = 0.025$). Compute $P(T)$.

**Step 1 — Identify structure.** Bottom OR gate (actuators) feeds into AND gate with sensor.

**Step 2 — Boolean expression.**

$$T = S \cap (A_{1} \cup A_{2})$$

**Step 3 — Compute OR gate intermediate probability first.**

$$
P(A) = 1 - (1 - q_{1})(1 - q_{2}) = 1 - (0.98)(0.975)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute product.**

$$
P(A) = 1 - 0.9555 = 0.0445
$$

**Step 5 — Apply AND gate formula with sensor.**

$$
P(T) = q_{S} \times P(A) = 0.01 \times 0.0445
$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$
P(T) = 4.45 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation.** The top event probability is approximately $4.45 \times 10^{-4}$. If the tolerable hazard rate is $10^{-4}$ per mission, this design fails the safety target — additional redundancy or improved sensor reliability is required.

---

## 3.5 Boolean Reduction to Minimal Cut Sets — Worked Example

**Fault Tree Boolean Expression:**

$$T = A \cdot B + A \cdot C + A \cdot D + E$$

**Step 1 — Factor common terms.**

$$T = A \cdot (B + C + D) + E$$

**Step 2 — Apply absorption rule:** $X + X \cdot Y = X$. (We retain $A \cdot B$, $A \cdot C$, $A \cdot D$ — all are non-absorbable because each differs.)

**Step 3 — Identify cut sets before reduction.**

$$\{A,B\}, \quad \{A,C\}, \quad \{A,D\}, \quad \{E\}$$

**Step 4 — Check minimality.** No cut set is a subset of another. Therefore, **all four are Minimal Cut Sets**.

**Step 5 — Order classification.**
- Size-1 MCS: $\{E\}$ → single-point failure (most critical).
- Size-2 MCS: three sets containing $A$ with another basic event.

**Step 6 — Quantitative top-event probability (assuming independence).**

$$
P(T) \approx 1 - \prod_{MCS}\left(1 - P_{MCS}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an *approximation* valid only when MCS probabilities are small; an exact evaluation requires inclusion-exclusion. KTU expects the approximation unless the question explicitly asks for inclusion-exclusion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 RCM Decision Logic Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Define Asset and Functions]) --> q1{Question 1 2: Functions and Performance}
    q1 --> q3[Question 3: Functional Failures]
    q3 --> q4[Question 4: Failure Modes from FMEA]
    q4 --> q5{Question 5: Failure Consequences}
    q5 --> catHidden[Hidden Failure]
    q5 --> catSafety[Safety Environmental]
    q5 --> catOperational[Operational]
    q5 --> catNonOp[Non Operational]
    catHidden --> taskA[Scheduled Failure Finding Task]
    catSafety --> taskB[Scheduled Mandatory Task or Redesign]
    catOperational --> taskC{Scheduled or On Condition Feasible}
    taskC --> taskC1[Scheduled Restoration]
    taskC --> taskC2[On Condition Monitoring]
    catNonOp --> taskD{Life Cycle Cost Analysis}
    taskD --> taskD1[No Scheduled Action: Run to Failure]
    taskD --> taskD2[One Shot Change]
    taskA --> q6
    taskB --> q6
    taskC1 --> q6
    taskC2 --> q6
    taskD1 --> q6
    taskD2 --> q6
    q6{Question 6 7: Proactive Task and Default} --> endA([End: Maintenance Plan Documented])
```

---

## 4.2 Fault Tree Schematic — Worked Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topEvent([T: System Failure])
    gateA[OR Gate]
    gateB[AND Gate]
    evA[Basic Event A: Pump 1 Fails]
    evB[Basic Event B: Pump 2 Fails]
    evC[Basic Event C: Sensor Fails]
    evD[Basic Event D: Valve Stuck]
    evE[Basic Event E: Power Loss]
    topEvent --> gateA
    gateA --> gateB
    gateA --> evE
    gateB --> evA
    gateB --> evB
    gateB --> gateC
    gateC[OR Gate 2]
    gateC --> evC
    gateC --> evD
```

**Reading the diagram:** Top Event T occurs if (Power Loss) OR [ (Sensor Fails OR Valve Stuck) AND (Pump 1 Fails AND Pump 2 Fails) ].

---

## 4.3 RCM Consequence-Driven Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inBlock([Failure Mode from FMEA]) --> classBlock{Classify Consequence}
    classBlock --> hOut[Hidden Failure]
    classBlock --> sOut[Safety Impact]
    classBlock --> oOut[Operational Loss]
    classBlock --> nOut[Economic Loss Only]
    hOut --> task1[Scheduled Inspection]
    sOut --> task2[Mandatory Scheduled Task]
    oOut --> task3[On Condition or Scheduled]
    nOut --> task4[Run to Failure after LCC]
```

---

## 4.4 Fault Tree Logic Gates Reference Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    orIn1[A] --> orGate[OR Gate]
    orIn2[B] --> orGate
    orGate --> orOut[Output fails if A OR B fails]
    andIn1[C] --> andGate[AND Gate]
    andIn2[D] --> andGate
    andGate --> andOut[Output fails only if C AND D both fail]
```

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RCM-to-FTA Integration

```mermaid

    blockR([Asset Inventory and Criticality Ranking])
    blockF([FMEA: Failure Modes and Effects])

    blockQ([Quantitative Probability Evaluation])
    blockPlan([Maintenance Action Plan])
    blockR --> blockF
    blockF --> blockRCM

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Define Reliability Centred Maintenance. List any four advantages. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Reliability Centred Maintenance (RCM) is a systematic engineering framework defined under SAE JA1011 that prescribes the maintenance strategy for each asset based on its failure modes and consequences, ensuring that the asset's intended functions are preserved safely and economically.

**Four Advantages:**
1. Increased operational safety through formal FMEA-based identification of hidden failures.
2. Reduced maintenance cost by eliminating unnecessary preventive tasks.
3. Improved equipment reliability and availability, preserving inherent design reliability.
4. Extended equipment life by managing failure modes that cause secondary damage.

*(Examiner expects 2-3 line crisp answer for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 — Define a Minimal Cut Set. Why is it important in FTA? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence causes the top event, and which does not contain any proper subset that is itself a cut set.

**Importance:**
1. Identifies **single-point failures** (size-1 MCS) that must be eliminated by design.
2. Ranks failure paths by order, guiding **redesign and maintenance priority**.
3. Forms the basis for **quantitative top-event probability** calculation.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Explain the seven questions of RCM as per SAE JA1011. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The seven foundational questions of RCM, as per SAE JA1011, are answered sequentially for each significant item in the system:

**Q1. What are the functions and the associated desired performance standards of the asset in its present operating context?**

This step defines the intended purpose of the asset — both primary and secondary functions — and the quantitative performance level the asset must meet. The operating context (environment, duty cycle, load profile) is captured here because the same equipment may have different functions in different systems.

**Q2. In what ways does it fail to fulfil its functions?**

Every functional failure is enumerated. For a pump, possible functional failures include *no flow*, *reduced flow*, *overpressure*, and *no shutdown on demand*. Each functional failure will later be linked to failure modes.

**Q3. What causes each functional failure?**

Failure modes are identified using FMEA techniques. For example, *no flow* in a pump could be due to impeller seizure, shaft breakage, suction blockage, or motor electrical failure.

**Q4. What happens when each failure occurs?**

This addresses the failure effects — both immediate (loss of function) and secondary (downstream damage, environmental release).

**Q5. In what way does each failure matter?**

Failures are classified into four consequence categories:
- **Hidden failures** (defeat safety systems but are not evident).
- **Safety / Environmental consequences** (injury, fatality, environmental release).
- **Operational consequences** (loss of production).
- **Non-operational consequences** (purely economic direct cost).

**Q6. What can be done to prevent or predict each failure?**

For each (mode, consequence) pair, suitable proactive tasks are evaluated: scheduled restoration, scheduled discard, scheduled inspection, or on-condition monitoring.

**Q7. What should be done if a suitable preventive task cannot be found?**

This is the default-action step. The engineer selects among: redesign for failure tolerance, one-shot change to operating procedure, or acceptance of run-to-failure (only if consequence permits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Key Insight:** Examiners award 1 mark per question clearly stated, plus 1 mark for the consequence classification step. Use **bullet points**, not paragraphs, in your answer.

---

**`(b)` A system fails (top event T) if either Event A or Event B occurs (OR gate), OR if Events C and D occur together (AND gate). Given $q_{A} = 0.05$, $q_{B} = 0.03$, $q_{C} = 0.02$, $q_{D} = 0.04$, compute the probability of the top event. Assume independence. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Fault Tree structure.**

$$T = (A \cup B) \cup (C \cap D)$$

**Step 2 — Apply OR gate probability formula for A ∪ B.**

$$
P(A \cup B) = 1 - (1 - q_{A})(1 - q_{B})
$$

$$
P(A \cup B) = 1 - (0.95)(0.97) = 1 - 0.9215 = 0.0785
$$

**Step 3 — Apply AND gate formula for C ∩ D.**

$$
P(C \cap D) = q_{C} \cdot q_{D} = 0.02 \times 0.04 = 8 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply outer OR gate.**

$$
P(T) = 1 - \left(1 - P(A \cup B)\right)\left(1 - P(C \cap D)\right)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.0785)(1 - 0.0008)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (0.9215)(0.9992)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.92076 = 0.07924
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{P(T) \approx 0.0792 \text{ or } 7.92\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly compute the *outer* OR gate by simple addition $P(A \cup B) + P(C \cap D)$ — this is wrong when events overlap. Always use the proper formula $1 - \Pi(1 - P_i)$. Partial marks may be awarded for inner-gate calculations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**`(a)` Explain the step-by-step procedure of Fault Tree Analysis with a suitable example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) is a deductive reliability method developed by H.A. Watson (Bell Labs, 1961) for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman missile program.

**Step 1 — Define the Top Event.** Select a single, clearly stated undesired event. *Example: "Emergency cooling pump P-101 fails to deliver rated flow during a Loss-of-Coolant Accident."*

**Step 2 — Construct the Fault Tree.** Reason *backwards* from the top event using AND / OR gates. Each gate must be justified by physical logic. *Example:* Pump fails to deliver flow = (Pump does not start) OR (Pump runs but has no flow).

**Step 3 — Identify Basic Events.** The bottom-most events in the tree — e.g., *motor winding short*, *impeller seized*, *control relay contact open*. Each is assigned a basic failure probability $q_{i}$.

**Step 4 — Identify Cut Sets.** A **Cut Set** is any combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence produces the top event. *Example:* {Motor winding short, Power supply fail}.

**Step 5 — Reduce to Minimal Cut Sets.** Apply Boolean absorption:
$$X + X \cdot Y = X$$
to remove non-minimal cut sets. The remaining sets are MCS.

**Step 6 — Qualitative Evaluation.** Rank MCS by order (number of basic events) and size. Single-event MCS = single-point failures, most critical. Lower-order MCS = more likely to occur.

**Step 7 — Quantitative Evaluation.** Compute top-event probability using the MCS probabilities. For an OR-combined approximation:

$$
Q_{TOP} \approx 1 - \prod_{i}\left(1 - P_{MCS_{i}}\right)
$$

This yields the final numeric probability of the top event.

---

**`(b)` A production line stops (top event T) if (i) the conveyor motor fails OR (ii) both sensors fail simultaneously. Motor failure probability $q_{M} = 0.04$. Sensor 1 fails with $q_{S1} = 0.06$, Sensor 2 with $q_{S2} = 0.05$. Compute $P(T)$ and identify all minimal cut sets. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Boolean Expression.**

$$T = M \cup (S_{1} \cap S_{2})$$

**Step 2 — Identify Cut Sets.**

- $\{M\}$ — single point.
- $\{S_{1}, S_{2}\}$ — two-event cut set.

Both are **minimal** because neither contains a proper subset that is also a cut set.

$$\boxed{\text{MCS}_{1} = \{M\}, \quad \text{MCS}_{2} = \{S_{1}, S_{2}\}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Probability of MCS₂ (AND gate).**

$$
P(S_{1} \cap S_{2}) = 0.06 \times 0.05 = 0.003
$$

**Step 4 — Compute top event probability using outer OR gate.**

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - q_{M})(1 - P(S_{1} \cap S_{2}))
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - 0.04)(1 - 0.003)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - (0.96)(0.997)
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.95712 = 0.04288
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

$$\boxed{P(T) \approx 0.0429 \text{ or } 4.29\%}$$

**Engineering Recommendation:** The single-point failure $\{M\}$ (MCS₁) is a size-1 MCS and is the dominant contributor. A backup motor or dual-redundant motor arrangement would dramatically reduce $P(T)$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "MCS = {M, S₁, S₂}" — this is a cut set but **not minimal**, because removing $M$ still leaves $\{S_{1}, S_{2}\}$ as a valid cut set. Always verify minimality.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost students 2-4 marks in KTU Part B questions on RCM and FTA:**
>
> 1. **Confusing RCM with TPM or PM.** RCM is a *decision* process, not a preventive-maintenance program.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply Boolean absorption** during cut set reduction — full credit requires the reduced (minimal) form.
> 3. **Adding probabilities instead of multiplying** for AND gates. Always remember $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$ for independent events.
> 4. **Omitting consequence classification** in RCM answers — examiners specifically allocate 1-2 marks to this.
> 5. **Drawing fault trees but not labeling gates** — unlabeled AND/OR gates lose marks.
> 6. **Confusing deductive (FTA) with inductive (FMEA)** direction of analysis — clearly state direction in answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (RCM + FTA):**

### RCM Essentials
- RCM is a **decision-making process** for maintenance, not a maintenance program itself.
- Defined formally under **SAE JA1011** (Evaluation Criteria for RCM Processes).
- RCM originated from the **aviation industry** (Boeing MSG-3 lineage).
- Driven by **seven foundational questions** — must answer ALL for compliance.
- Four **consequence categories**: Hidden, Safety/Environmental, Operational, Non-operational.
- Hidden and Safety consequences always require **scheduled proactive tasks**.
- Non-operational consequences may permit **run-to-failure** after LCC analysis.
- Major advantages: increased safety, reduced cost, better availability, compliance with ISO 55000.

### FTA Essentials
- FTA is **deductive** (top-down); FMEA is **inductive** (bottom-up).
- Developed by **H.A. Watson, Bell Labs, 1961**, for Minuteman missile program.
- Two fundamental gates: **AND** (all inputs must fail) and **OR** (any input fails).
- **Cut Set** = any combination causing top event.
- **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** = smallest such combination, no proper subset is itself a cut set.
- **Boolean absorption rule**: $X + X \cdot Y = X$ — essential for MCS reduction.
- **OR gate probability**: $1 - \prod (1 - q_i)$.
- **AND gate probability**: $\prod q_i$.
- **Single-event MCS = single-point failure** → most critical, must be redesigned.
- **Quantitative top event** uses approximation: $Q_{TOP} \approx 1 - \prod_{i}(1 - P_{MCS_{i}})$.
- FTA integrates naturally with RCM — FMEA feeds the basic events; FTA computes top-event probability.
- **Standard symbols**: Rectangle (event), Circle (basic event), Diamond (undeveloped event), AND/OR gates.

### Exam Tips
- Always **state independence assumption** explicitly when using AND/OR probability formulas.
- Always **draw the fault tree** before computing — it earns at least 1-2 marks.
- For RCM answers, **use bullet points** for the seven questions and the four consequence categories.
- For 14-mark questions, **structure into clear sub-parts** matching the question sub-parts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features and Merits, Event Tree Analysis (ETA) - Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Features and Merits, Event Tree Analysis (ETA) - Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** is a forward-looking, **inductive** safety and reliability analysis technique that begins with a single **initiating event** (IE) and systematically traces all possible subsequent outcomes by modelling the sequential success or failure of associated safety functions, barriers, or protective systems. Each branching node represents a binary (or multi-state) decision point, producing an **accident sequence diagram** that culminates in distinct end-states (consequences) whose probabilities can be quantified.

In the context of **Maintenance Engineering and Management**, ETA is used as a probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) tool to:
- Evaluate the reliability of protective systems and safety barriers
- Quantify accident sequences leading to system failure
- Support **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** decisions
- Identify critical safety functions requiring focused maintenance

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Domino Path with Guardrails**
>
> Imagine a **marble rolling down a hill** (the initiating event). Along its path, there are several **guardrails (safety barriers)** that can either *stop* the marble (SUCCESS branch — green light) or *fail to stop* it (FAILURE branch — red light).
>
> - The marble reaches Guardrail 1: Stopped ✅ (safe) OR Escapes ❌ (continue down)
> - The marble reaches Guardrail 2: Stopped ✅ OR Escapes ❌
> - The marble reaches Guardrail 3: Stopped ✅ OR Escapes ❌ (catastrophic end-state)
>
> The **Event Tree** is simply a **flowchart of these guardrail decisions**. At the end, you can calculate: "What is the probability the marble reaches the bottom?" by multiplying probabilities along each path.
>
> In a nuclear plant, chemical refinery, or aircraft system, the "marble" is the initiating fault, and the "guardrails" are emergency cooling pumps, containment valves, alarm systems, fire suppression, etc.

---

## Why ETA is Critical in Maintenance Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** ETA helps maintenance engineers answer the question:
> *"If component X fails (initiating event), which safety systems must work reliably to prevent a disaster, and what maintenance priority should each receive?"*
>
> This directly supports **RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance)** and **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** integration.

---

## Standard Terminology (Bolded for KTU High-Yield)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Initiating Event (IE)** | The first undesired event that starts the accident sequence |
| **Top Event** | The undesired event being analyzed (focal point) |
| **Safety Function / Barrier** | A system designed to prevent or mitigate the accident progression |
| **Branch Point** | A node where success/failure of a safety function is evaluated |
| **Accident Sequence** | A specific path through the event tree representing one possible outcome |
| **End-State / Consequence** | The final outcome (safe shutdown, minor release, catastrophic failure) |
| **Branch Probability** | The probability of success (Pᵢ) or failure (1 − Pᵢ) at each node |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic of an Event Tree (Step-by-Step Logic)

ETA follows a strict **forward (deductive-from-cause) logic** structure:

### Step 1 — Identify the Initiating Event (IE)
- Choose a credible unwanted event: pump failure, pipe rupture, power loss, valve closure, etc.
- The IE must be **well-defined, measurable, and bounded in scope**.

### Step 2 — Identify Success Paths (Top-Down Approach)
- List all **safety functions** designed to respond to the IE.
- Examples: pressure relief valves, emergency shutdown systems (ESD), backup pumps, containment systems, alarms, fire suppression.

### Step 3 — Construct the Branching Tree
- At each safety function node, draw **two branches**:
  - **Upper branch** = Safety function OPERATES SUCCESSFULLY (S)
  - **Lower branch** = Safety function FAILS (F)
- Continue horizontally for each subsequent safety function in the **temporal order of response**.

### Step 4 — Define End-States
- Each unique path through the tree leads to an **end-state** (consequence category).
- End-states are labelled with severity descriptors (e.g., Safe Shutdown, Minor Incident, Major Release, Catastrophic Failure).

### Step 5 — Assign Probabilities
- For each safety function $i$, assign the probability of successful operation $P_i$ (and failure probability $Q_i = 1 - P_i$).
- The probability of an accident sequence is the **product** of branch probabilities along that path.

### Step 6 — Calculate Path Frequencies & Risk
- Multiply IE frequency by the path probability to get the **frequency of each end-state**.
- Sum end-state frequencies if needed for a total system risk metric.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and align with standard PRA methodology (IEEE Std 352, NUREG-1855, IEC 62502).

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|----------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_i + Q_i = 1$ | Sum of success and failure probabilities at branch $i$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $P_{\text{path},k} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_{i,k}$ | Probability of path $k$ = product of branch probabilities along that path | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_{\text{end-state},k} = f_{\text{IE}} \times P_{\text{path},k}$ | Frequency of end-state $k$ = IE frequency × path probability | events/year |
| 4 | $f_{\text{IE}} = \lambda_{\text{IE}}$ | Initiating event frequency (from failure rate data or operating experience) | events/year |
| 5 | $P_i = R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | Reliability of safety function $i$ over mission time $t$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q_i = 1 - e^{-\lambda_i t}$ | Unreliability (failure probability) of safety function $i$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $\sum_{k=1}^{m} P_{\text{path},k} = 1$ | Total probability over all paths (conservation rule) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Risk} = \sum_{k} F_{\text{end-state},k} \times C_k$ | Total risk = sum of (frequency × consequence) over all end-states | events × severity / year |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables. The formulas above use $\vert$ style notation where required, but in tables we have avoided it entirely.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind ETA — Engineering Rationale

- **Forward Logic vs Backward Logic:** Unlike Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) which works *backwards* from a top event using AND/OR gates, ETA works *forward* from the initiating event. This makes ETA more intuitive for showing **time-sequenced** responses of safety systems.
- **Visual Clarity:** Even non-technical stakeholders (management, regulators, auditors) can read an event tree and understand accident progression.
- **Quantitative Power:** ETA directly produces probability/frequency estimates for each outcome — essential for risk-informed maintenance decisions.
- **Integration with RCM:** The safety functions identified in ETA become candidates for **predictive, preventive, or condition-based maintenance** strategies based on their criticality.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Applications (Real-World Utility)

| Industry | ETA Application |
|----------|-----------------|
| **Nuclear Power** | Reactor accident sequence evaluation (e.g., LOCA — Loss of Coolant Accident) |
| **Oil & Gas** | Offshore platform blowout, pipeline rupture scenarios |
| **Aviation** | Engine failure sequence analysis, landing gear deployment |
| **Chemical Process** | Reactor runaway, toxic release sequences |
| **Manufacturing** | Machine failure escalation paths in automated production lines |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Path Probability & End-State Frequency

### Given Scenario (Worked Analytical Example)
A chemical reactor has an **overpressure initiating event** with frequency $f_{\text{IE}} = 10^{-2}$ events/year. Three independent safety functions respond in sequence:

1. **Safety Function 1 (Pressure Relief Valve):** Failure rate $\lambda_1 = 0.01$/yr, mission time $t = 1$ yr
2. **Safety Function 2 (Emergency Cooling System):** Failure rate $\lambda_2 = 0.05$/yr, mission time $t = 1$ yr
3. **Safety Function 3 (Containment):** Failure rate $\lambda_3 = 0.001$/yr, mission time $t = 1$ yr

Each safety function either operates (S) or fails (F). Compute the probability of the catastrophic end-state path **S-F-F** (relief valve works, but cooling fails AND containment fails).

---

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute reliability (success probability) for each safety function using exponential distribution:**

$$P_i = R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

This is the standard reliability function for a component with constant failure rate $\lambda_i$ over mission time $t$.

For Safety Function 1:

$$P_1 = e^{-\lambda_1 t} = e^{-(0.01)(1)} = e^{-0.01}$$

Evaluating:

$$P_1 = 0.99005$$

Therefore:

$$Q_1 = 1 - P_1 = 1 - 0.99005 = 0.00995$$

For Safety Function 2:

$$P_2 = e^{-\lambda_2 t} = e^{-(0.05)(1)} = e^{-0.05}$$

Evaluating:

$$P_2 = 0.95123$$

Therefore:

$$Q_2 = 1 - P_2 = 1 - 0.95123 = 0.04877$$

For Safety Function 3:

$$P_3 = e^{-\lambda_3 t} = e^{-(0.001)(1)} = e^{-0.001}$$

Evaluating:

$$P_3 = 0.99900$$

Therefore:

$$Q_3 = 1 - P_3 = 1 - 0.99900 = 0.00100$$

---

**Step 2 — Compute the probability of the specific accident path S-F-F:**

The path S-F-F means: Function 1 succeeds, Function 2 fails, Function 3 fails.

$$P_{\text{path (S-F-F)}} = P_1 \times Q_2 \times Q_3$$

Substituting the values:

$$P_{\text{path (S-F-F)}} = 0.99005 \times 0.04877 \times 0.00100$$

$$P_{\text{path (S-F-F)}} = 0.99005 \times 0.00004877$$

$$P_{\text{path (S-F-F)}} = 4.829 \times 10^{-5}$$

This is dimensionless (a pure probability).

---

**Step 3 — Compute the frequency of the catastrophic end-state:**

The end-state frequency is the IE frequency multiplied by the path probability:

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times P_{\text{path (S-F-F)}}$$

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = 10^{-2} \times 4.829 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = 4.829 \times 10^{-7} \text{ events/year}$$

This is an extremely low frequency, indicating that **all three safety functions must fail simultaneously** for catastrophe — the system is highly protected (a desirable safety profile).

---

**Step 4 — Verification using the conservation rule:**

The total probability across all 8 paths ($2^3 = 8$) must equal 1:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{8} P_{\text{path},k} = 1$$

For independent binary branches at each node, this is mathematically guaranteed since:

$$\sum P_{\text{path}} = \prod_{i=1}^{3} (P_i + Q_i) = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1$$

✅ **Verification Passed.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — All Eight End-States of the Reactor Example

| Path | Label | Formula | Probability | Frequency (events/yr) |
|------|-------|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| S-S-S | Safe Shutdown | $P_1 P_2 P_3$ | $0.99005 \times 0.95123 \times 0.99900 = 0.94067$ | $9.4067 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| S-S-F | Minor Release | $P_1 P_2 Q_3$ | $0.99005 \times 0.95123 \times 0.00100 = 9.417 \times 10^{-4}$ | $9.417 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| S-F-S | Controlled Incident | $P_1 Q_2 P_3$ | $0.99005 \times 0.04877 \times 0.99900 = 4.823 \times 10^{-2}$ | $4.823 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| S-F-F | **Catastrophe** | $P_1 Q_2 Q_3$ | $4.829 \times 10^{-5}$ | $4.829 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| F-S-S | Late Containment | $Q_1 P_2 P_3$ | $0.00995 \times 0.95123 \times 0.99900 = 9.454 \times 10^{-3}$ | $9.454 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F-S-F | Significant Release | $Q_1 P_2 Q_3$ | $0.00995 \times 0.95123 \times 0.00100 = 9.465 \times 10^{-6}$ | $9.465 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| F-F-S | Major Incident | $Q_1 Q_2 P_3$ | $0.00995 \times 0.04877 \times 0.99900 = 4.847 \times 10^{-4}$ | $4.847 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| F-F-F | Severe Catastrophe | $Q_1 Q_2 Q_3$ | $0.00995 \times 0.04877 \times 0.00100 = 4.853 \times 10^{-7}$ | $4.853 \times 10^{-9}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The "safe shutdown" path dominates (94%), while catastrophic outcomes are vanishingly rare — this validates the design and maintenance strategy of the safety system.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Step-Through: Why Maintenance Engineering Values This Output

From the matrix above, the maintenance engineer observes:

$$\text{Most Critical Safety Function} = \arg\max_i \{Q_i\}$$

This means the safety function with the **highest failure probability** demands the most rigorous maintenance attention. Here, $Q_2 = 0.04877$ (Emergency Cooling) dominates → **increase cooling system maintenance frequency, add redundancy, or improve testing protocols.**

The **conditional probability** that the system reaches catastrophe GIVEN the IE occurs:

$$P(\text{Catastrophe} \mid \text{IE}) = \frac{P_{\text{catastrophe paths}}}{1} = 4.829 \times 10^{-5} + 4.853 \times 10^{-7} \approx 4.88 \times 10^{-5}$$

This shows that **even if the initiating event happens, catastrophe is rare** — a key metric for safety case reporting.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Event Tree Analysis (ETA). List any four salient features of ETA.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
> **Definition (1 Mark):** Event Tree Analysis (ETA) is a forward-looking, inductive safety analysis technique that begins with an **initiating event** and systematically traces the success or failure of subsequent safety functions to determine all possible end-state outcomes and their probabilities.
>
> **Four Salient Features (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Forward/Inductive Logic:** Analysis proceeds from cause (initiating event) to consequence (end-state).
> 2. **Binary Branching:** Each safety function is evaluated as either successful (S) or failed (F).
> 3. **Time-Sequenced:** Safety functions are arranged in the order they are expected to respond.
> 4. **Quantitative Output:** Provides numerical probability and frequency values for each end-state, enabling risk-based decisions.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the merits of Event Tree Analysis in maintenance engineering decision-making.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
> 1. **Visual Clarity:** Easy-to-understand graphical representation accessible to engineers and management. **(1 Mark)**
> 2. **Identifies Critical Safety Functions:** Highlights which safety barriers demand prioritized maintenance based on failure probability. **(1 Mark)**
> 3. **Quantifies Risk:** Provides frequencies of accident sequences, supporting risk-informed maintenance strategies such as RCM and condition-based monitoring. **(1 Mark)**

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **features and merits** of Event Tree Analysis (ETA) in detail with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A reactor cooling system has an initiating event (loss of coolant) with frequency $1.2 \times 10^{-2}$/yr. Two independent safety functions respond in sequence:
- Safety Function 1 (Emergency Core Cooling): $P_1 = 0.98$
- Safety Function 2 (Containment Spray): $P_2 = 0.995$

Construct the event tree and compute the probability and frequency of the catastrophic end-state (both safety functions fail). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

> **Features of ETA (4 Marks):**
> 1. **Inductive/Forward Analysis:** Begins with an initiating event and progresses forward through the accident sequence. *(1 Mark)*
> 2. **Branching Logic:** Each safety function introduces binary (or multi-state) branches representing success/failure. *(1 Mark)*
> 3. **Sequential Representation:** Safety functions are placed in the order they are expected to operate, reflecting real-time accident progression. *(1 Mark)*
> 4. **Accident Sequence Identification:** Each unique path through the tree represents a distinct accident sequence, leading to defined end-states. *(1 Mark)*
>
> **Merits of ETA (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Simplicity:** Graphical and intuitive; easy to communicate to non-specialists. *(1 Mark)*
> 2. **Quantitative Power:** Provides both qualitative (sequence) and quantitative (probability/frequency) outputs. *(1 Mark)*
> 3. **Maintenance Decision Support:** Identifies high-failure-probability safety functions for prioritized maintenance, inspection, and testing. *(1 Mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 — Identify branch failure probabilities:** *(1 Mark)*

$$Q_1 = 1 - P_1 = 1 - 0.98 = 0.02$$

$$Q_2 = 1 - P_2 = 1 - 0.995 = 0.005$$

**Step 2 — Construct the Event Tree (conceptual description):** *(2 Marks)*

The tree has 4 end-states:
- S-S: Safe Shutdown
- S-F: Minor Incident
- F-S: Controlled Failure
- F-F: Catastrophe

**Step 3 — Compute the probability of the catastrophic path (F-F):** *(2 Marks)*

$$P_{\text{F-F}} = Q_1 \times Q_2 = 0.02 \times 0.005$$

$$P_{\text{F-F}} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the frequency of the catastrophic end-state:** *(2 Marks)*

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times P_{\text{F-F}}$$

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = (1.2 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$F_{\text{catastrophe}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ events/year}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **methodology of constructing an Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** for a chemical reactor overpressure scenario involving three safety functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the system described in part (a), the following data is given:

| Safety Function | Failure Rate $\lambda$ (per year) | Mission Time $t$ (yr) |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------|
| SF1: Pressure Relief Valve | 0.005 | 1 |
| SF2: Emergency Cooling | 0.02 | 1 |
| SF3: Containment | 0.002 | 1 |

If the initiating event frequency is $5 \times 10^{-3}$/yr, compute the **frequency of all 8 end-states**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 — Identify the initiating event:** *(1 Mark)*
Overpressure in chemical reactor (due to blocked outlet, runaway reaction, etc.).

**Step 2 — Identify three safety functions in sequence:** *(1 Mark)*
1. Pressure Relief Valve
2. Emergency Cooling System
3. Containment Structure

**Step 3 — Construct event tree:** *(3 Marks)*

> Initiating Event → SF1 (S/F) → SF2 (S/F) → SF3 (S/F) → 8 End-States

Each safety function branches into Success (S, upward) or Failure (F, downward), producing $2^3 = 8$ accident sequences:
1. S-S-S → Safe Shutdown
2. S-S-F → Minor Release
3. S-F-S → Controlled Incident
4. S-F-F → Local Damage
5. F-S-S → Late Recovery
6. F-S-F → Major Release
7. F-F-S → Significant Incident
8. F-F-F → **Catastrophic Failure**

**Step 4 — Assign probabilities and compute results:** *(2 Marks)*
Each branch probability is determined from the component failure rate data.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Step 1 — Compute reliabilities (success probabilities):** *(2 Marks)*

$$P_1 = e^{-\lambda_1 t} = e^{-0.005} = 0.99501$$

$$Q_1 = 1 - P_1 = 0.00499$$

$$P_2 = e^{-\lambda_2 t} = e^{-0.02} = 0.98020$$

$$Q_2 = 1 - P_2 = 0.01980$$

$$P_3 = e^{-\lambda_3 t} = e^{-0.002} = 0.99800$$

$$Q_3 = 1 - P_3 = 0.00200$$

**Step 2 — Compute all 8 path probabilities:** *(2 Marks)*

| Path | Probability Calculation | Value |
|------|------------------------|-------|
| S-S-S | $0.99501 \times 0.98020 \times 0.99800$ | $0.97332$ |
| S-S-F | $0.99501 \times 0.98020 \times 0.00200$ | $1.951 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| S-F-S | $0.99501 \times 0.01980 \times 0.99800$ | $1.966 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| S-F-F | $0.99501 \times 0.01980 \times 0.00200$ | $3.940 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F-S-S | $0.00499 \times 0.98020 \times 0.99800$ | $4.881 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| F-S-F | $0.00499 \times 0.98020 \times 0.00200$ | $9.782 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| F-F-S | $0.00499 \times 0.01980 \times 0.99800$ | $9.860 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F-F-F | $0.00499 \times 0.01980 \times 0.00200$ | $1.976 \times 10^{-7}$ |

**Step 3 — Multiply each path by $f_{\text{IE}} = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ to get frequencies:** *(3 Marks)*

| Path | Frequency (events/year) |
|------|------------------------|
| S-S-S | $4.8666 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| S-S-F | $9.755 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| S-F-S | $9.830 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| S-F-F | $1.970 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| F-S-S | $2.441 \times 10^{-5}$ |
| F-S-F | $4.891 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| F-F-S | $4.930 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| F-F-F | $9.880 \times 10^{-10}$ |

> **Conclusion:** The "Catastrophic Failure" end-state (F-F-F) occurs at $9.88 \times 10^{-10}$ events/year — an exceptionally rare outcome, validating the safety system design.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ETA with FTA:** Students often mix up Event Tree Analysis (forward) with Fault Tree Analysis (backward, AND/OR gates). State explicitly: ETA is **forward + inductive + binary branches**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Conservation Rule:** $\sum P_{\text{path}} = 1$. If your 8 paths don't sum to 1, you have an arithmetic error.
>
> 3. **Using Failure Rate as Probability:** $\lambda$ (per year) is **not** a probability. You must convert via $P_i = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ or $P_i = 1 - \lambda_i t$ for small $\lambda t$.
>
> 4. **Not Labelling Branches:** Each branch must clearly show **Success (S)** or **Failure (F)** with the corresponding probability. Examiners deduct marks for unmarked branches.
>
> 5. **Ignoring End-State Frequencies:** Computing path probabilities alone is incomplete. Always multiply by $f_{\text{IE}}$ to get the **frequency** — KTU examiners specifically ask for this.
>
> 6. **Mixing Units:** $\lambda$ is per year, $t$ is in years, $f_{\text{IE}}$ is events/year, $F$ is events/year. Keep units consistent or lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ETA Features & Merits**
>
> ✅ ETA = **Forward + Inductive + Binary Branching + Quantitative**
>
> ✅ **Initiating Event** is the starting point of every ETA
>
> ✅ **Safety Functions** are arranged in **chronological/temporal sequence**
>
> ✅ Each branch is **Success (S)** with probability $P_i$ or **Failure (F)** with probability $Q_i = 1 - P_i$
>
> ✅ Number of end-states = $2^n$ where $n$ = number of safety functions
>
> ✅ Path Probability: $P_{\text{path}} = \prod P_i$ (or $Q_i$ as appropriate)
>
> ✅ End-State Frequency: $F_{\text{end-state}} = f_{\text{IE}} \times P_{\text{path}}$
>
> ✅ Conservation Rule: $\sum P_{\text{path}} = 1$
>
> ✅ **Merits:** Visual, intuitive, quantitative, identifies critical safety functions, supports RCM and risk-based maintenance
>
> ✅ **Contrast with FTA:** ETA = forward from cause; FTA = backward from top event using logic gates
>
> ✅ **Reliability Function:** $P_i = R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$ (constant failure rate assumption)
>
> ✅ **Total Risk:** $\text{Risk} = \sum F_{\text{end-state},k} \times C_k$ (frequency × consequence)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merits and Procedure, Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Merits and Procedure, Root Cause Analysis (RCA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Root Cause Analysis (RCA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** is defined as a **structured, systematic, and evidence-based investigative methodology** used in maintenance engineering to identify the *fundamental underlying cause(s)* of a failure, defect, or non-conformance event, by going beyond the visible symptoms until the deepest controllable causal factor is uncovered.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEIET613 syllabus**, RCA is positioned as the *cornerstone analytical tool* of modern proactive maintenance strategy (RCM, TPM, and Reliability-Centered Maintenance). It shifts the engineer's mindset from **reactive fire-fighting ("why did this break?")** to **proactive prevention ("how do we stop it from ever breaking again?")**.

The most cited industry definition is by **Norm Ohmae** and **Sakichi Toyoda** (Toyota), which states:
> *"A root cause is a fundamental causal factor that, if removed, would prevent the recurrence of an undesirable event."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Detective and the Dominoes"

Imagine you walk into your kitchen and find water all over the floor. You mop it up and walk away. Next day, water again. You mop again. **This is NOT Root Cause Analysis — this is symptomatic repair.**

Now imagine you play *detective*:

1. You notice the floor is wet.
2. You look up — the ceiling has a stain.
3. You go to the floor above — a pipe joint is dripping.
4. You investigate why the joint leaks — the rubber washer is 8 years old and brittle.
5. **ROOT CAUSE IDENTIFIED:** Aged washer + no preventive replacement schedule.

This is **RCA**. In a manufacturing plant:

- **Symptom:** A pump bearing seizes.
- **Apparent cause:** Bearing failed (anyone can see this).
- **Root cause:** Lubrication schedule was skipped for 11 weeks because the auto-greaser was misaligned during a 2022 overhaul.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Highly important for KTU exams):**
> - **Symptom / Apparent Cause:** What you *see* at the failure site.
> - **Root Cause:** The *deepest controllable factor* in the cause-and-effect chain whose elimination prevents recurrence.

### 1.3 Why RCA Matters in Maintenance Engineering (Industry Context)

In a typical process plant, the **5-25 rule** (often cited in reliability literature) states:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For every **1 dollar** spent on **preventive action (RCA-driven fix)**, **5 dollars** are saved on **direct repair**, and **25 dollars** are saved on **consequential losses** (production downtime, secondary damage, safety incidents, warranty claims).

### 1.4 Merits of RCA — The Strategic Payoff Matrix

| Strategic Domain | Specific Merit |
|---|---|
| **Reliability** | Reduces Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) for recurring faults |
| **Availability** | Increases Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) |
| **Safety** | Prevents catastrophic accidents (e.g., Bhopal, Texas City) |
| **Cost** | Lowers Life Cycle Cost (LCC) of assets |
| **Knowledge** | Builds organizational learning and corporate memory |
| **Compliance** | Satisfies ISO 9001, ISO 14001, OSHA PSM, and NABL audit requirements |
| **Culture** | Drives a *no-blame*, learning-oriented maintenance culture |

### 1.5 Core Terminology Block (Exam Favorites)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Event:** The undesirable incident itself (e.g., motor trip on 14-Mar-2025).
> - **Causal Factor:** Any contributor that influenced the event.
> - **Root Cause:** The deepest factor whose removal prevents recurrence.
> - **Contributing Cause:** A factor that worsened severity but did not initiate.
> - **Corrective Action:** Immediate action to restore function.
> - **Preventive Action:** Long-term action to eliminate the root cause.
> - **5-Whys:** A linear questioning technique (Sakichi Toyoda, Toyota).
> - **Fishbone / Ishikawa Diagram:** A cause-category mapping tool (Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy of RCA

RCA rests on three philosophical pillars which must be understood before applying any technique:

1. **Causal Chain Hypothesis:** Failures are not random; they are the *final link* in a chain of contributing causes. Find the weakest link and reinforce it.
2. **Systemic Causation (NOT Human Error):** Heinrich's Domino Theory and Reason's Swiss Cheese Model both teach us that operators are usually the *last* domino to fall, not the first. Modern RCA explicitly avoids "human error" as a root cause.
3. **Evidence over Opinion:** Every causal link in RCA must be supported by physical evidence, data logs, or corroborated witness statements.

### 2.2 The Standard KTU RCA Procedure — Stepwise Logical Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus expects students to memorize the **6-step RCA procedure**. This is a recurring 7 to 14 mark question in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

**Step 1 — Problem Definition & Boundary Setting**
- Define the problem statement precisely: *What happened? When? Where? To what extent?*
- Use the **SMART** format: Specific, Measurable, Actionable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- **Example:** *"On 14-Mar-2025 at 02:18 hrs, Boiler Feed Pump P-101B tripped on bearing high-temperature, resulting in 4.2 hours of lost production."*

**Step 2 — Data Collection & Evidence Preservation**
- Collect SCADA / DCS historian logs, vibration spectra, oil analysis reports, operator logs, maintenance work orders, photographs, video, and operator interviews.
- The **golden rule**: *Preserve the scene before cleaning it up.*

**Step 3 — Causal Factor Identification (Mapping Phase)**
- Brainstorm all possible causes without judgment.
- Group them using either the **6M Fishbone** (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature) or the **4M** framework (Man, Machine, Material, Method).

**Step 4 — Root Cause Identification (Drilling Phase)**
- Apply linear questioning (**5 Whys**) for simple single-fault events.
- Apply **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** for complex multi-path failures.
- Apply **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** for design-level issues.

**Step 5 — Recommendation of Corrective & Preventive Actions (CAPA)**
- Each root cause must have **at least one preventive action**.
- Actions must be *SMART* and assigned to a named owner with a target date.

**Step 6 — Verification, Documentation & Closure**
- Monitor post-implementation KPIs (MTBF, failure count, vibration levels).
- Close the RCA only when data confirms the root cause has been eliminated.
- File the report in the **Maintenance Knowledge Base** for future reference.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — RCA Quantitative Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Although RCA is primarily qualitative, several quantitative metrics support it. **All units must be remembered** for the KTU exam.

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Repair Time}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent / Intrinsic Availability | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **OEE** | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Pareto 80/20** | Cumulative \% = $\dfrac{\sum \text{Frequency}_\text{class}}{\text{Total Frequency}} \times 100$ | Helps prioritize top RCA targets | % |
| **Failure Rate ($\lambda$)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Used in reliability block diagrams | failures / hour |
| **RPN (FMEA)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number | score (1 to 1000) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipe-Table Rule Applied:** All absolute values (e.g., $|x|$, $|S - O|$) inside any table row are typeset using `\vert` to avoid breaking the KTU markdown table syntax. Example: $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\vert \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR} \vert}$

### 2.4 The Major RCA Techniques — Comparative Theoretical Analysis

#### 2.4.1 The **5 Whys Analysis** (Linear / Single-Fault Technique)

- **Origin:** Sakichi Toyoda, Toyota Production System, 1930s.
- **Mechanism:** Ask "Why?" iteratively, typically 5 times, until the chain reaches a *controllable* root cause.
- **Best for:** Simple, single-chain, human-procedure-related failures.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle multi-path failures; risk of confirmation bias.

#### 2.4.2 The **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** (Categorical Technique)

- **Origin:** Kaoru Ishikawa, 1968, University of Tokyo.
- **Mechanism:** Map potential causes under 6 (or 4) categorical "bones."
- **Standard 6M Categories:** Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
- **Best for:** Group brainstorming; complex events with multiple contributory categories.

#### 2.4.3 **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** (Top-Down Deductive Technique)

- **Origin:** Bell Telephone Labs, 1961 (Minuteman Missile Program); formalized by NASA.
- **Mechanism:** Starts from the undesired TOP EVENT and uses Boolean **AND / OR gates** to decompose into basic events.
- **Best for:** Complex safety-critical systems (nuclear, aerospace, chemical).
- **Symbolic Logic:** Use of standard Boolean algebra.

#### 2.4.4 **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)** (Bottom-Up Inductive Technique)

- **Origin:** U.S. Military Procedure MIL-P-1629, 1949; later expanded into automotive (AIAG-VDA, 2019).
- **Mechanism:** Lists every component, its failure modes, effects, and **Risk Priority Number (RPN)**.

#### 2.4.5 **Pareto Analysis** (Prioritization Technique)

- **Origin:** Vilfredo Pareto, Italian economist (1896); popularized by Joseph Juran (1950s).
- **Mechanism:** 80/20 rule — *80% of failures come from 20% of causes.*
- **Usage:** Decides *which* failure to RCA first.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of RCA

- **Power Plants:** Identifies root causes of boiler tube leaks, turbine blade cracks.
- **Aerospace:** Mandatory under FAA / EASA after any Service Difficulty Report.
- **Automotive:** Every recall begins with an RCA of field failure data.
- **Pharmaceutical & Medical Devices:** Mandated by **FDA 21 CFR 820.100** and **EU MDR**.
- **Process Industries:** Required under **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 — Process Safety Management**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reality Check:** In KTU ESE papers, the 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain the merits and procedure of RCA with suitable maintenance examples."* Students must write **at least 4 merits** + **all 6 procedural steps** + **at least 2 techniques** to secure full marks.
