# MODULE 3 | KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Lean Maintenance. List any four wastes relevant to a maintenance function. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Lean Maintenance** is the application of Toyota Production System principles to maintenance, aiming to **maximise equipment effectiveness (OEE)** by eliminating **Muda (waste), Mura (unevenness) and Muri (overburden)**.

**Four wastes relevant to maintenance:**

1. **Waiting** – Machine idle awaiting spare or technician.
2. **Inventory** – Excess slow-moving spares tying up working capital.
3. **Motion** – Technician walking unnecessarily to fetch tools.
4. **Defects** – Rework caused by poor repair quality.

*(1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the four wastes.)*

---

### Question 2: What is meant by the "Zero Maintenance Concept"? State any two design strategies that help achieve it. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Zero Maintenance** is a design philosophy whereby machinery is engineered to operate **its entire economic life without planned human intervention**, achieved by building-in reliability and maintainability.

**Two design strategies:**

1. **Use of sealed-for-life components** (e.g., greased-for-life bearings, hermetically sealed motors).
2. **Built-in redundancy and derating** — operating components well below their rated capacity to extend life.
3. *(any other valid: self-lubricating materials, condition-monitoring sensors, oil-mist lubrication, modular quick-change subassemblies.)*

*(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Students frequently list **tools (5S, Kaizen)** without explaining **how each tool applies to maintenance**. Marks are awarded for the **maintenance-specific linkage**, not just the definition. Always close each tool with a maintenance example.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> On the Zero Maintenance question, students often confuse "zero maintenance" with "no maintenance" — losing a full mark. Always clarify that **Zero Maintenance requires the *highest* degree of design, monitoring, and operator care — not neglect.**

---

### Question A — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Lean Maintenance concept** in detail. Discuss its **key features** and **benefits** with reference to OEE. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **seven (+1) wastes** of lean as applied to a maintenance department of a manufacturing plant, with one maintenance-specific example for each. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Lean Maintenance Concept, Features & Benefits (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Lean Maintenance is the application of lean manufacturing principles (derived from Toyota Production System) to maintenance activities, with the objective of **maximising value-added maintenance work while eliminating waste (Muda), unevenness (Mura) and overburden (Muri)**.

**Key Features (3 marks):**

1. **Operator-driven autonomous maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators own daily cleaning, lubrication, inspection and tightening of their machines, freeing craftsmen for high-skill work.
2. **Pull-based spares replenishment (Kanban):** Spares are replenished only when a bin is empty, reducing inventory and obsolescence.
3. **Visual management and 5S:** Tools, parts and procedures are visible, standardised and located at point-of-use — eliminating motion and search waste.
4. **Continuous improvement (Kaizen):** Small, daily, low-cost improvements driven by front-line staff.
5. **Data-driven decision-making:** CMMS / EAM systems feed dashboards tracking OEE, MTBF, MTTR, %M of RAV.

**Benefits (3 marks):**

1. **Higher OEE** — typical uplift from 60% to 85%+, directly translating to **production capacity without new capex**.
2. **Lower MTTR and higher MTBF** — downtime drops, planning improves.
3. **Reduced maintenance cost as % of RAV** — from 6–8% (uncontrolled) down to 2–4%.
4. **Improved safety** — 5S and Poka-Yoke reduce accidents.
5. **Morale & skill uplift** — multi-skilled workforce, ownership culture.
6. **Lower inventory carrying cost** — working capital freed.

#### (b) Seven (+1) Wastes in Maintenance (7 Marks)

| # | Waste | Maintenance Example (each 1 mark × 7 = 7) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Defects** | A replaced bearing is fitted with wrong torque → repeat failure within a week → rework cost. |
| 2 | **Over-production** | Greasing a sealed-for-life bearing every month — wastes labour and contaminates the seal. |
| 3 | **Waiting** | A production line halted for 5 hours because a ₹200 fuse was not on shelf. |
| 4 | **Non-utilised talent** | A diploma electrical engineer assigned to floor-sweeping instead of root-cause analysis. |
| 5 | **Transportation** | Forklift covering 800 m per shift to fetch bolts from a centralised spares store. |
| 6 | **Inventory** | ₹50 lakh of bearings lying unused for 8 years — working capital blocked and obsolescence risk. |
| 7 | **Motion** | Technician walks 50 m to find a torque wrench because tools are not at point-of-use. |
| 8 | **Extra-processing** | Filling a 12-page work permit for a 10-min lamp replacement — bureaucratic over-documentation. |

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **Zero Maintenance Concept** in detail. Discuss the design strategies and enabling technologies that make it achievable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Lean Maintenance** and **Traditional Maintenance** on eight parameters. Discuss why Lean Maintenance is preferred in modern Industry 4.0 environments. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Zero Maintenance Concept — Detailed (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Zero Maintenance is an ideal-state maintenance philosophy in which the **frequency of both preventive and corrective interventions approaches zero** during the useful economic life of an asset, achieved through **designing-in reliability and maintainability**.

**Layers / Continuum (2 marks):**
The path from Reactive → Preventive → Predictive → Proactive → Zero Maintenance is a **maturation sequence**, each step adding capability.

**Design Strategies for Zero Maintenance (3 marks):**

1. **High-reliability components** — derated bearings (operating at <50% of rated load), upgraded metallurgy, ceramic coatings.
2. **Sealed / lifetime-lubricated assemblies** — permanently greased bearings, oil-mist centralised lubrication.
3. **Built-in redundancy** — dual pumps, parallel fans, N+1 configurations so single failures do not stop production.
4. **Self-monitoring & auto-diagnosis** — embedded vibration, temperature and current sensors triggering local shut-down (Jidoka).
5. **Modular design & quick-change subassemblies** — even when failure occurs, swap-in is so fast that production loss is negligible.

**Enabling Technologies (1 mark):**
IIoT sensors, edge analytics, AI-driven prescriptive maintenance, digital twins, additive-manufactured spare parts.

#### (b) Lean vs Traditional — Eight Parameters (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Traditional | Lean Maintenance |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Maintenance philosophy | "Fix when it breaks" | Prevent + predict + improve continuously |
| 2. Ownership | Maintenance dept. only | Shared (operator + maintenance) |
| 3. Spare parts | High buffer (push) | Kanban (pull) |
| 4. KPI focus | MTTR, downtime hours | OEE, MTBF, %M of RAV, Kaizen savings |
| 5. Improvement style | Big projects / CAPEX | Daily Kaizen, PDCA |
| 6. Documentation | Thick manuals | One-page SOPs, visual controls |
| 7. Skills | Single-skilled trade | Multi-skilled, cross-trained |
| 8. Cultural tone | Blame on operator | Blame on process; learning org |

**Why Lean fits Industry 4.0 (1 bonus mark for closing synthesis):**
Lean provides the **disciplined baseline** (clean, standardised, data-rich processes) upon which **Industry 4.0 technologies** (IIoT, AI, digital twin, predictive analytics) can deliver true value. Without lean discipline, sensors only generate noise.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Sheet — Module 3, Lean Maintenance & Zero Maintenance**

- **Lean Maintenance (LM)** = TPS + TPM applied to maintenance — maximises value, eliminates waste.
- **Three Evils:** **Muda** (waste), **Mura** (unevenness), **Muri** (overburden).
- **DOWNTIME mnemonic** for the 8 wastes: **D**efects, **O**ver-production, **W**aiting, **N**on-utilised talent, **T**ransportation, **I**nventory, **M**otion, **E**xtra-processing.
- **Four Pillars of LM:** (1) Eliminate waste, (2) Kaizen / continuous improvement, (3) Autonomous maintenance, (4) Planned maintenance system.
- **Key Lean Tools:** 5S, Kaizen, Kanban, Poka-Yoke, Jidoka, SMED, Heijunka, TPM, OEE.
- **OEE Formula (always testable):**
  $$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$
- **World-class OEE benchmark:** **≥ 85%** (typical Indian SME: 60–75%).
- **MTBF and MTTR trade-off:**
  $$A_{\text{inherent}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$
- **Zero Maintenance is achieved through DESIGN, not neglect.** It is the highest layer of the maintenance maturity pyramid (Reactive → Preventive → Predictive → Proactive → Zero Maintenance).
- **Design strategies for Zero Maintenance:** sealed-for-life components, derating, redundancy, self-lubrication, modular quick-change assemblies, embedded sensors, Jidoka.
- **Industry 4.0 synergy:** Lean = disciplined baseline; IIoT/AI = intelligence layer; both **must coexist** for world-class maintenance.
- **Common exam errors to avoid:**
  - Do not equate Zero Maintenance with "no maintenance."
  - Do not list lean tools without **maintenance-specific examples**.
  - Do not forget **OEE units / benchmarks** when discussing benefits.
  - Do not confuse **Kanban (pull)** with **MRP (push)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 5-S Maintenance Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** 5-S Maintenance Concept # 📘 MODULE 3 — 5-S MAINTENANCE CONCEPT ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 🔹 Formal Academic Definition The **5-S Methodology** is a structured, five-step Japanese workplace organization and standardization technique that forms the foundational pillar of **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** and **Lean Manufacturing**. Originally derived from the Toyota Production System (TPS), 5-S establishes a disciplined, visually controlled, and waste-free work environment. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of Maintenance Engineering, 5-S is treated as a **proactive housekeeping and management philosophy** that eliminates the seven wastes (*muda*), reduces equipment downtime, and ensures operator-level ownership of machine health. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact):** > *5-S is a systematic approach to workplace organization, standardization, and self-discipline that creates an efficient, safe, and productive maintenance environment, forming the basis for autonomous maintenance in TPM.* ### 🔹 The Five Pillars — Original Japanese & English Equivalents | Japanese Term | Romanji | English Meaning | Core Focus | |---|---|---|---| | 整理 | **Seiri** | **Sort** | Eliminate unnecessary items | | 整頓 | **Seiton** | **Set in Order** | Arrange items for easy access | | 清掃 | **Seiso** | **Shine / Sweep** | Clean the workplace & equipment | | 標準化 | **Seiketsu** | **Standardize** | Standardize processes & visuals | | 躾 | **Shitsuke** | **Sustain / Self-Discipline** | Maintain discipline & habit | ### 🔹 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **🏠 Think of 5-S like organizing your own kitchen before cooking a complex dish:** > > - **Sort (Seiri):** Throw away expired spices and broken utensils you never use. > - **Set in Order (Seiton):** Place frequently-used knives on a magnetic strip within arm's reach; store rarely-used items in top cabinets. > - **Shine (Seiso):** Wipe the counter and stove after every meal — you immediately notice if a burner is clogged. > - **Standardize (Seiketsu):** Label every shelf, use color-coded chopping boards, and follow the same recipe card format. > - **Sustain (Shitsuke):** Make this your daily habit — never let dirty dishes pile up overnight. > > In a factory, this translates to: mechanics find tools instantly, oil leaks are spotted in seconds, breakdowns are prevented, and audits become effortless. ### 🔹 Relevance to Maintenance Engineering In KTU's Maintenance Engineering framework, **5-S is the gateway to Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)** — the 7th pillar of TPM. Without 5-S, operators cannot clean, inspect, or lubricate machines effectively because the workplace is chaotic. **Standard Maintenance Metrics Highlighted:** - **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** ↑ improves - **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** ↓ reduces - **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** ↑ improves - **First-Pass Yield** ↑ improves - **Safety Incident Rate** ↓ reduces --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🔍 Step-by-Step Breakdown of Each "S" ### 1️⃣ SEIRI — SORT (The Foundation) **Operational Principle:** *Separate "needed" from "not needed." Discard the latter without hesitation.* **Step-by-Step Logic:** - Conduct a **Red-Tag Campaign**: Items not used in the last **30 days** are tagged red. - Move red-tagged items to a quarantine area. - Items unused for **> 90 days** are disposed, recycled, or stored remotely. - Maintain a "Need vs. Unneeded" inventory register. **Why it matters for Maintenance:** - Reduces tool-search time (a major hidden downtime contributor). - Frees up valuable floor space near critical machines. - Eliminates obsolete spare parts clogging stores. > [!TIP] > **Red-Tagging Decision Rule:** Apply the *"Last Used — Frequency — Quantity"* test. If an item has not been used in 30+ days, it is a candidate for removal. --- ### 2️⃣ SEITON — SET IN ORDER (A Place for Everything) **Operational Principle:** *After sorting, arrange remaining items so they can be located in < 30 seconds.* **Step-by-Step Logic:** - Apply the **"30-Second Rule"**: any tool, gauge, or part must be reachable within 30 seconds. - Use **Shadow Boards (Hensetsu Boards)**: tools are outlined in paint so missing items are immediately visible. - Implement **Floor Markings**: yellow lines for walkways, white for work zones, red for danger zones, green for safety equipment (as per **OSHA / IS 2379 color codes**). - Use **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** racks for consumables and lubricants. **Why it matters for Maintenance:** - Reduces MTTR dramatically — mechanics waste zero time searching for spanners. - Prevents cross-contamination of lubricants. - Enables visual management at a glance. --- ### 3️⃣ SEISO — SHINE (Clean to Inspect) **Operational Principle:** *Cleaning IS inspection. A clean machine cannot hide leaks, cracks, or loose bolts.* **Step-by-Step Logic:** - Assign **Cleaning Zones**: every operator owns a specific machine area. - Clean during **machine warm-up** or **idle** cycles — never interfere with production. - Use the **"Photo-Before-and-After"** technique to track standards. - Identify the **3-K's**: Kaizen (Improvement), Kikai (Machines), Kiken (Hazards) during cleaning. **Why it matters for Maintenance:** - Oil leaks, vibration, and overheating are detected **before** catastrophic failure. - Cleaning reveals loose fasteners, cracked welds, and worn belts. - It builds operator pride and ownership. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often describe Seiso as "just cleaning." In board answers, **emphasize that Seiso is an inspection activity disguised as cleaning** — this distinction fetches full marks. --- ### 4️⃣ SEIKETSU — STANDARDIZE (Make it Visual & Repeatable) **Operational Principle:** *Convert the first three S's into repeatable, documented, and visually standardized routines.* **Step-by-Step Logic:** - Develop **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** for sorting, arranging, and cleaning. - Use **Visual Controls**: labels, color codes, signage, photographs, checklists. - Implement **5-Minute 5-S Audits** at shift start. - Create **Standardized Cleaning Checklists** laminated and posted on each machine. **Why it matters for Maintenance:** - Ensures consistency across shifts, operators, and departments. - Enables new workers to adapt quickly. - Forms the audit baseline for management reviews. --- ### 5️⃣ SHITSUKE — SUSTAIN (Discipline is Everything) **Operational Principle:** *Without discipline, 5-S collapses within weeks. Build the habit.* **Step-by-Step Logic:** - Conduct **daily 5-min huddles** at shift start. - Implement **5-S Audit Scorecards** (typically scored out of 100). - Celebrate 5-S Champions publicly. - Integrate 5-S into **KPI dashboards** and **annual appraisals**. **Why it matters for Maintenance:** - Sustains the gains from steps 1–4 indefinitely. - Cultivates a culture of continuous improvement (*Kaizen*). --- ## 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** 5-S is primarily a qualitative methodology. The following **derived metrics** are used in KTU 2024 Scheme questions to quantify 5-S effectiveness. | # | Metric | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **5-S Audit Score** | $S_{5S} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i} \times 100$ | Weighted score across the 5 pillars | % | | 2 | **Tool Search Time Reduction** | $\Delta T_s = T_{before} - T_{after}$ | Time saved per tool-retrieval event | minutes | | 3 | **Workspace Utilization Ratio** | $U_r = \frac{A_{useful}}{A_{total}} \times 100$ | Useful floor area vs. total floor area | % | | 4 | **Defect Reduction Rate** | $D_r = \frac{D_{before} - D_{after}}{D_{before}} \times 100$ | % drop in defects post-5S | % | | 5 | **Downtime Reduction** | $\eta_{dt} = \frac{DT_{before} - DT_{after}}{DT_{before}} \times 100$ | % downtime reduction | % | | 6 | **OEE Improvement** | $\Delta OEE = OEE_{after} - OEE_{before}$ | Gain in Overall Equipment Effectiveness | % | | 7 | **5-S Compliance Index** | $C_{5S} = \frac{N_{compliant\_items}}{N_{total\_items}} \times 100$ | % items meeting 5-S standards | % | | 8 | **Kaizen Suggestion Index** | $KSI = \frac{N_{implemented}}{N_{submitted}} \times 100$ | Implementation rate of improvement ideas | % | **Where:** - $w_i$ = weight assigned to the $i^{th}$ S pillar (e.g., Sort=20%, Set in Order=20%, Shine=20%, Standardize=20%, Sustain=20%) - $s_i$ = score (out of 100) for the $i^{th}$ S pillar during audit - $A_{useful}$ = floor area used productively, $A_{total}$ = total available floor area - $D$ = number of defects, $DT$ = downtime hours, $N$ = number of items/suggestions ### 🎯 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Sector (Toyota, Maruti Suzuki):** 5-S reduced shop floor accidents by **40%** and tool search time by **70%**. - **Aerospace MRO (Maintenance, Repair & Overhaul):** 5-S enabled Airbus to reduce FOD (Foreign Object Debris) incidents. - **Power Plants:** 5-S-led cleaning helped NTPC detect micro-leaks in boiler tubes before catastrophic rupture. - **Hospitals:** Toyota's 5-S principles reduced surgical instrument retrieval time by **35%**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🧮 Derivation: 5-S Audit Score with Weighted Pillars The 5-S audit is graded by an experienced auditor who scores each of the 5 pillars on a scale of **0 to 100**. Since some pillars (like *Sustain*) are harder to achieve, weights are applied. ### 📘 Step-by-Step Derivation Let: - $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4, s_5$ be the auditor scores for Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, and Sustain respectively. - $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4, w_5$ be the corresponding importance weights. **Step 1: Compute the weighted sum of all pillar scores.** $$ W_{sum} = w_1 \cdot s_1 + w_2 \cdot s_2 + w_3 \cdot s_3 + w_4 \cdot s_4 + w_5 \cdot s_5 $$ **Step 2: Compute the total possible weight (denominator).** $$ W_{total} = w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 + w_5 $$ **Step 3: Compute the weighted average score (out of 100).** $$ S_{5S} = \frac{W_{sum}}{W_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i} $$ **Step 4: Express the final audit score as a percentage.** $$ S_{5S\, \%} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i} \times 100 $$ **Interpretation Bands (KTU Standard):** | Score Range | Rating | Action Required | |---|---|---| | 90 – 100 % | **World Class** | Benchmark & replicate in other units | | 75 – 89 % | **Good** | Minor improvements | | 60 – 74 % | **Acceptable** | Structured improvement plan needed | | < 60 % | **Poor** | Major overhaul & re-training required | --- ### 🧮 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A maintenance workshop is audited for 5-S compliance. The auditor scores and weights are: | Pillar | Score ($s_i$) | Weight ($w_i$) | |---|---|---| | Sort | 80 | 0.20 | | Set in Order | 75 | 0.20 | | Shine | 70 | 0.25 | | Standardize | 65 | 0.15 | | Sustain | 60 | 0.20 | **Find the overall 5-S Audit Score.** **Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate weighted contributions.** $$ \begin{aligned} w_1 s_1 &= 0.20 \times 80 = 16.00 \\ w_2 s_2 &= 0.20 \times 75 = 15.00 \\ w_3 s_3 &= 0.25 \times 70 = 17.50 \\ w_4 s_4 &= 0.15 \times 65 = 9.75 \\ w_5 s_5 &= 0.20 \times 60 = 12.00 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Sum the weighted contributions.** $$ W_{sum} = 16.00 + 15.00 + 17.50 + 9.75 + 12.00 = 70.25 $$ **Step 3: Sum the weights.** $$ W_{total} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.20 = 1.00 $$ **Step 4: Compute the percentage score.** $$ S_{5S} = \frac{70.25}{1.00} \times 100 = 70.25\% $$ **Conclusion:** The workshop scores **70.25 %** — falls in the **Acceptable** band. Shine and Sustain pillars need focused improvement. --- ## 📋 Analytical Matrix: 5-S vs. Other Maintenance Philosophies | Feature | **5-S** | **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** | **TQM (Total Quality Management)** | **Lean Manufacturing** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Origin** | Toyota, Japan (1980s) | Nippondenso, Japan (1971) | Deming, Juran (USA) | Toyota Production System | | **Primary Focus** | Workplace organization | Equipment effectiveness | Product/service quality | Waste elimination | | **Scope** | Floor-level housekeeping | 8 pillars (incl. Autonomous Maint.) | Entire organization | Entire value stream | | **5-S Relationship** | Foundation of TPM | Includes 5-S as prerequisite | 5-S supports clean quality | 5-S is Step 1 of Lean | | **Maintenance Role** | Indirect (enabler) | Direct (core of TPM) | Indirect (defect prevention) | Indirect (Muda removal) | | **Time Horizon** | Daily habit | 3–5 year transformation | Continuous | Continuous | | **Key Metric** | 5-S Audit Score | OEE | First-Pass Yield | Lead Time | --- ## 📊 Before–After Impact Matrix (Industry Data) | Parameter | **Before 5-S** | **After 5-S** | % Improvement | |---|---|---|---| | Tool Search Time (avg.) | 12 min | 3 min | 75 % ↓ | | Unplanned Downtime (hrs/month) | 45 | 18 | 60 % ↓ | | Workplace Accidents | 8/year | 2/year | 75 % ↓ | | Defect Rate (PPM) | 3500 | 1100 | 68.6 % ↓ | | Floor Space Utilization | 55 % | 82 % | 49 % ↑ | | Operator Engagement Score | 4/10 | 8/10 | 100 % ↑ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🔄 Diagram 1: 5-S Cyclical Implementation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START 5-S Initiative] --> B["S1 SEIRI SORT
Identify and remove
unnecessary items"] B --> C["S2 SEITON SET IN ORDER
Arrange needed items
30 second rule"] C --> D["S3 SEISO SHINE
Clean workplace
Clean to inspect"] D --> E["S4 SEIKETSU STANDARDIZE
Document SOPs
Visual controls"] E --> F["S5 SHITSUKE SUSTAIN
Build discipline
Daily audits"] F --> G{Audit Score
Above 80 percent?} G -->|No| B G -->|Yes| H["CELEBRATE AND
BENCHMARK"] H --> I[Kaizen Continuous
Improvement Loop] I --> B ``` --- ## 🏭 Diagram 2: 5-S as the Foundation of TPM (8 Pillars) ```mermaid flowchart TD TPM[TPM TOTAL PRODUCTIVE MAINTENANCE] --> P0["PILLAR 0
5-S FOUNDATION
Housekeeping and Discipline"] TPM --> P1["PILLAR 1
Autonomous Maintenance Jishu Hozen"] TPM --> P2["PILLAR 2
Planned Maintenance"] TPM --> P3["PILLAR 3
Quality Maintenance"] TPM --> P4["PILLAR 4
Training"] TPM --> P5["PILLAR 5
Early Equipment Management"] TPM --> P6["PILLAR 6
Safety Health Environment"] TPM --> P7["PILLAR 7
Office TPM Administration"] P0 --> P1 P0 --> P2 P0 --> P3 P0 --> P4 P0 --> P5 P0 --> P6 P0 --> P7 P1 --> OE["OEE BREAKTHROUGH
Greater than 85 percent"] P2 --> OE P3 --> OE P4 --> OE P5 --> OE P6 --> OE P7 --> OE ``` --- ## 🛠️ Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of 5-S Maintenance System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"] I1[Raw Materials] I2[Tools and Spare Parts] I3[Operators and Technicians] I4[Machine Specifications] end subgraph S1LAYER["SEIRI SORT MODULE"] S1A[Red Tagging] S1B[Quarantine Zone] S1C[Disposal Approval] end subgraph S2LAYER["SEITON SET IN ORDER MODULE"] S2A[Shadow Boards] S2B[Floor Markings] S2C[Location Labels] end subgraph S3LAYER["SEISO SHINE MODULE"] S3A[Cleaning Schedule] S3B[Inspection Log] S3C[Abnormality Report] end subgraph S4LAYER["SEIKETSU STANDARDIZE MODULE"] S4A[SOP Library] S4B[Visual Standards] S4C[Checklists] end subgraph S5LAYER["SHITSUKE SUSTAIN MODULE"] S5A[Daily Huddles] S5B[Audit Scorecard] S5C[Reward System] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"] O1[Reduced MTTR] O2[Increased MTBF] O3[Higher OEE] O4[Zero Accident Goal] O5[Engaged Workforce] end I1 --> S1LAYER I2 --> S1LAYER I3 --> S1LAYER I4 --> S1LAYER S1LAYER --> S2LAYER S2LAYER --> S3LAYER S3LAYER --> S4LAYER S4LAYER --> S5LAYER S5LAYER --> OUTPUT ``` --- ## 🎯 Diagram 4: 5-S Audit Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is the workplace
free of unnecessary

CHAOS

SORTING BEGINS

KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the 5-S concept. List and briefly explain each 'S' in the context of maintenance engineering.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **5-S concept** is a Japanese workplace organization methodology that creates an efficient, safe, and disciplined maintenance environment. It is the foundation of **Autonomous Maintenance** in TPM. The five S's are: 1. **Seiri (Sort):** Eliminate unnecessary items from the workplace using a Red-Tagging system. Items not used in the last 30 days are tagged and removed. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Seiton (Set in Order):** Arrange needed items so they can be retrieved within **30 seconds** using shadow boards, labels, and floor markings. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Seiso (Shine):** Clean the workplace regularly. Cleaning acts as inspection, helping detect oil leaks, loose bolts, and abnormal vibrations early. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Seiketsu (Standardize):** Convert the first three S's into repeatable SOPs, visual controls, and checklists. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Shitsuke (Sustain):** Build self-discipline through daily audits, training, and reward systems so 5-S becomes a habit. *(0.5 mark)* *5-S reduces MTTR, improves MTBF, and is a prerequisite for TPM.* *(0.5 mark)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Explain how 5-S acts as the foundation pillar of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM).** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 5-S is considered the **0th Pillar (Foundation Pillar)** of TPM because: - **Without Sort:** Operators cannot identify which machine parts need attention because the area is cluttered. *(0.75 mark)* - **Without Set in Order:** Tools and gauges are lost, increasing MTTR and frustrating mechanics. *(0.75 mark)* - **Without Shine:** Oil leaks, cracks, and overheating go undetected until catastrophic failure. *(0.75 mark)* - **Without Standardize:** Best practices are not documented, so improvements vanish when shift changes. *(0.75 mark)* Therefore, the **7 TPM pillars (Autonomous Maintenance, Planned Maintenance, Quality Maintenance, etc.) can only succeed after 5-S is fully implemented.** *(0.75 mark — partial)* --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the five pillars of the 5-S methodology. For each pillar, provide **one maintenance-specific example** from a manufacturing plant. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A maintenance workshop scored the following in a 5-S audit: | Pillar | Score (out of 100) | Weight | |---|---|---| | Sort | 90 | 0.15 | | Set in Order | 85 | 0.20 | | Shine | 70 | 0.25 | | Standardize | 80 | 0.20 | | Sustain | 60 | 0.20 | **Calculate the weighted 5-S Audit Score and interpret the result using the KTU grading bands. Suggest two corrective actions for the lowest-scoring pillar.** **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question A #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **1. Seiri (Sort):** Removing unneeded items. *(1 mark)* *Maintenance Example:* In a CNC machining cell, all jigs and fixtures not used in the last 30 days are moved to a central store. This frees 25% of the cell floor and allows quicker access to active tools. *(0.5 mark)* **2. Seiton (Set in Order):** Arrange items for fast retrieval. *(1 mark)* *Maintenance Example:* A shadow board is installed next to each CNC machine, with outlines of every required wrench and gauge. Missing tools are detected instantly at shift start. *(0.5 mark)* **3. Seiso (Shine):** Clean to inspect. *(1 mark)* *Maintenance Example:* During daily cleaning of a hydraulic press, an operator notices a hairline oil leak from a cylinder seal — repaired during the next planned stop, preventing a 6-hour breakdown. *(0.5 mark)* **4. Seiketsu (Standardize):** Standardize via SOPs and visuals. *(1 mark)* *Maintenance Example:* A laminated checklist with photographs is posted on each machine, listing daily cleaning steps, lubrication points, and inspection items. *(0.5 mark)* **5. Shitsuke (Sustain):** Discipline and audits. *(1 mark)* *Maintenance Example:* A 5-S audit is conducted weekly by a cross-functional team; results are displayed on a scoreboard and the winning cell gets the "5-S Champion" trophy. *(0.5 mark)* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Compute weighted score for each pillar.** $$ \begin{aligned} w_1 s_1 &= 0.15 \times 90 = 13.50 \\ w_2 s_2 &= 0.20 \times 85 = 17.00 \\ w_3 s_3 &= 0.25 \times 70 = 17.50 \\ w_4 s_4 &= 0.20 \times 80 = 16.00 \\ w_5 s_5 &= 0.20 \times 60 = 12.00 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Sum of weighted contributions.** $$ W_{sum} = 13.50 + 17.00 + 17.50 + 16.00 + 12.00 = 76.00 $$ **Step 3: Sum of weights.** $$ W_{total} = 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 1.00 $$ **Step 4: Compute percentage score.** $$ S_{5S} = \frac{76.00}{1.00} \times 100 = 76.00\% $$ **Step 5: Interpretation (KTU Band).** *(1 mark)* The score of **76%** falls in the **"Good" band (75 – 89%)** as per KTU grading — the workshop is above acceptable but needs structured improvements, particularly in **Sustain (60/100)**. **Step 6: Two Corrective Actions for Sustain (Lowest Pillar).** *(2 marks)* 1. **Daily 5-Minute Huddles:** Begin every shift with a 5-minute stand-up meeting where operators review 5-S compliance and raise yesterday's deviations. 2. **Visible Audit Scoreboard:** Install a real-time 5-S scoreboard in the workshop, with weekly photographs and public recognition for high-scoring teams. *(Additional optional actions: integrate 5-S into operator KPIs, conduct monthly Kaizen workshops.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how **5-S acts as the foundation of Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)** and discuss its relationship with **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen)**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A manufacturing unit implements 5-S. The data before and after 3 months of implementation is: | Parameter | Before 5-S | After 5-S | |---|---|---| | Tool search time (minutes/event) | 12 | 3 | | Unplanned downtime (hours/month) | 50 | 20 | | Defects (PPM) | 4000 | 1200 | | Floor space utilization (%) | 55 | 80 | **Calculate the % improvement in each parameter and comment on which area shows the greatest gain.** **(7 Marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution — Question B #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Block Diagram Explanation (Use Mermaid-equivalent sketch):** *(3 marks)* 5-S → Enables → Autonomous Maintenance → Enables → Planned Maintenance → Achieves → OEE Breakthrough. - **5-S (Foundation):** Establishes a clean, organized workplace. - **Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** Operators clean, inspect, lubricate, and tighten their own machines — possible ONLY if 5-S is in place. - **Planned Maintenance:** Mechanics execute scheduled tasks with confidence because tools and spares are findable. - **Quality Maintenance:** Zero-defect culture builds on standardized processes (Seiketsu). - **OEE Outcome:** Availability × Performance × Quality increases. **Relationship with Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen):** *(4 marks)* 1. **Sort** ensures only essential equipment is in the operator's zone. *(1 mark)* 2. **Set in Order** provides easy access to cleaning tools, gauges, and lube points. *(1 mark)* 3. **Shine** IS the inspection step of Autonomous Maintenance. *(1 mark)* 4. **Standardize** documents the AM activities into SOPs and checklists. *(0.5 mark)* 5. **Sustain** ensures operators follow AM routines daily. *(0.5 mark)* *Conclusion:* 5-S is the prerequisite for Jishu Hozen — without it, operators cannot own their machines. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **1. Tool Search Time:** *(1.5 marks)* $$ \Delta T_{s\,\%} = \frac{12 - 3}{12} \times 100 = \frac{9}{12} \times 100 = 75.00\% $$ **2. Unplanned Downtime:** *(1.5 marks)* $$ \eta_{dt} = \frac{50 - 20}{50} \times 100 = \frac{30}{50} \times 100 = 60.00\% $$ **3. Defect Rate:** *(1.5 marks)* $$ D_r = \frac{4000 - 1200}{4000} \times 100 = \frac{2800}{4000} \times 100 = 70.00\% $$ **4. Floor Space Utilization:** *(1.5 marks)* $$ \Delta U_r = \frac{80 - 55}{55} \times 100 = \frac{25}{55} \times 100 = 45.45\% $$ **Summary Table:** *(0.5 mark)* | Parameter | % Improvement | |---|---| | Tool Search Time | **75.00 %** ← Greatest | | Unplanned Downtime | 60.00 % | | Defects | 70.00 % | | Floor Space | 45.45 % | **Comment:** *(0.5 mark)* The **greatest gain (75%)** is in **tool search time** — directly attributable to **Seiton (Set in Order)** through shadow boards and visual management. This proves that 5-S delivers its highest immediate ROI by eliminating workplace waste (*muda*). --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks in 5-S Questions:** > > 1. **Mere listing of the 5 S's without maintenance context.** Examiners expect each pillar explained with a *machine or shop-floor example*. (–2 marks) > 2. **Treating Seiso as "just cleaning."** Seiso = Cleaning **+ Inspection**. Always state the inspection angle. (–1 mark) > 3. **Skipping the numerical step-by-step in calculation questions.** Always show intermediate weighted products like $w_1 s_1 = 0.15 \times 90 = 13.50$. (–2 marks) > 4. **Forgetting to interpret the audit score** against the KTU band. Examiners expect rating + corrective action. (–1 mark) > 5. **Confusing 5-S weights** — total weight must equal **1.00**. Cross-check before final answer. > 6. **Writing "5-S is a cleaning method" in the introduction** — instant 1-mark penalty. Use the correct term: **"workplace organization and standardization methodology."** --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **5-S = Seiri + Seiton + Seiso + Seiketsu + Shitsuke** - ✅ **Japanese Origin:** Toyota Production System; transliterated into English as **Sort, Set in Order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain**. - ✅ **5-S is Pillar 0 of TPM** — the foundation for Autonomous Maintenance (Jishu Hozen). - ✅ **Red-Tag System:** Used in Seiri for items unused > 30 days. - ✅ **30-Second Rule:** Core principle of Seiton. - ✅ **Shine = Cleaning + Inspection** (always state both). - ✅ **5-S Audit Score formula:** $S_{5S} = \frac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i} \times 100$. - ✅ **Weights must sum to 1.00** (or 100%). - ✅ **KTU Bands:** ≥90% World Class | 75–89% Good | 60–74% Acceptable | <60% Poor. - ✅ **Visual Management Tools:** Shadow boards, floor markings, color codes, checklists, scoreboards. - ✅ **Improvements observed:** Tool search time ↓, Downtime ↓, Defects ↓, Space utilization ↑, OEE ↑. - ✅ **Common Exam Pitfall:** Always pair the 5-S concept with a maintenance-engineering example — generic answers fetch partial marks only. - ✅ **Sustain (Shitsuke)** is universally the lowest-scoring pillar — examiners love testing corrective actions for it. - ✅ **Linkages to remember:** 5-S → TPM → Lean → Kaizen → OEE. - ✅ **5-S Maturity Curve:** Chaos → Sorting → Organized → Standardized → Self-Sustaining (typically 12+ months journey). ---

topics.title: Maintenance Effectiveness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Effectiveness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Maintenance Effectiveness

**Maintenance Effectiveness** is a multidimensional engineering metric that quantitatively measures the degree to which a maintenance program successfully achieves its intended objectives of preserving, restoring, or improving the functional capability of physical assets, systems, and processes, while simultaneously optimizing lifecycle cost, safety, and operational availability.

Mathematically, it is expressed as a composite function of three cardinal engineering attributes:

$$\text{Maintenance Effectiveness} = f(\text{Availability}, \text{Reliability}, \text{Maintainability})$$

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, Maintenance Effectiveness is treated as a **Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** that allows plant managers, reliability engineers, and maintenance planners to objectively evaluate the ROI (Return on Investment) of any maintenance strategy — be it Reactive (Breakdown), Preventive, Predictive, or Proactive (RCM/TPM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> Maintenance Effectiveness is NOT the same as Maintenance Efficiency. Effectiveness measures *whether the right maintenance is being done*, while efficiency measures *whether the maintenance is being done right*. A maintenance team can be 100% efficient (low cost, fast repairs) but entirely ineffective if they are repairing the wrong components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Governing Standard:** The most widely accepted industrial framework for quantifying Maintenance Effectiveness globally is the **SEMI E10** standard and the **ISO 14224** standard for reliability data collection. In modern Industry 4.0 contexts, it is measured via the **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** index, pioneered by Seiichi Nakajima under the **TPM (Total Productive Maintenance)** philosophy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a hospital emergency room (ER). The hospital's administration wants to know if their **maintenance** (doctors, nurses, equipment servicing) is effective.

- If an X-ray machine breaks down twice a week, it doesn't matter how cheaply it was repaired (efficiency) — the ER is ineffective at delivering service.
- Maintenance effectiveness is like asking: *"Are we keeping the right machines running, for the right amount of time, at the right quality, and at the right cost?"*

Another powerful analogy is **car ownership**:
- An **effective** car owner performs timely oil changes, rotates tires, and replaces brake pads *before* a highway breakdown occurs.
- An **ineffective** owner waits for the car to fail, pays for costly towing, and misses critical meetings.

In a factory, **Maintenance Effectiveness** answers: *"For every hour of scheduled production time, how much truly value-adding output did the equipment deliver?"* The gap between the ideal and actual performance is the **Six Big Losses** that effectiveness metrics aim to close.

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Maintenance Effectiveness

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, Maintenance Effectiveness is built upon **five interconnected pillars**:

1. **Equipment Availability** — The fraction of time the asset is ready for production.
2. **Performance Efficiency** — The actual operating speed vs. designed speed.
3. **Quality Rate** — The fraction of good units produced vs. total units produced.
4. **Reliability** — The probability that the asset performs its intended function under stated conditions.
5. **Cost Optimization** — The ratio of maintenance spend to value preserved or recovered.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question asks "Define Maintenance Effectiveness," always mention that it is a *composite* metric — never give a single-number answer. The examiner looks for the holistic view covering availability, reliability, maintainability, and cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) Framework

The single most important quantitative tool for measuring Maintenance Effectiveness is **OEE**, introduced by Seiichi Nakajima (1988). OEE is the gold standard of TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) and is directly examined in KTU Module 3.

OEE is the product of three independent sub-factors:

$$\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$$

Each factor is expressed as a percentage (0–100%), and world-class manufacturing benchmarks are well established.

### 2.1.1 Availability Factor (A)

Availability measures the **uptime** of the equipment, penalizing any unplanned stoppages (breakdowns) and setup/changeover losses.

$$A = \frac{\text{Planned Production Time} - \text{Downtime}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100$$

Where **Downtime** includes all stoppages due to equipment failure, setup, adjustment, and idling.

### 2.1.2 Performance Factor (P)

Performance measures **speed losses** — running the machine at reduced speeds or producing below its designed cycle time.

$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100$$

The denominator uses **Operating Time** (i.e., Planned Production Time minus Downtime).

### 2.1.3 Quality Factor (Q)

Quality measures the **yield** — the fraction of good pieces produced versus total pieces produced.

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces Produced}} \times 100$$

## 2.2 The "Six Big Losses" Model

The TPM framework attributes OEE losses to six discrete categories. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** structure.

| **Loss Category** | **Type** | **Affects OEE Factor** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Equipment Failure (Breakdowns) | Downtime Loss | Availability |
| 2. Setup and Adjustment Losses | Downtime Loss | Availability |
| 3. Idling and Minor Stoppages | Speed Loss | Performance |
| 4. Reduced Speed Losses | Speed Loss | Performance |
| 5. Defect Losses (Process) | Quality Loss | Quality |
| 6. Defect Losses (Startup/Yield) | Quality Loss | Quality |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** Maintenance Effectiveness strategies are explicitly designed to attack these six losses. For example, **Predictive Maintenance (Condition Monitoring)** primarily addresses Losses 1, 3, and 4; **SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die)** addresses Loss 2; **Poka-Yoke** addresses Losses 5 and 6.

## 2.3 Reliability, Maintainability & Availability (RAM) Metrics

These three parameters form the **engineering backbone** of Maintenance Effectiveness and are frequently asked in KTU examinations.

### 2.3.1 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

MTBF is the average elapsed time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. It is a pure **reliability** metric.

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

**Units:** Hours (or km, cycles, etc.). For non-repairable items, the equivalent term is **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**.

### 2.3.2 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)

MTTR is the average time required to restore a failed system to operational status. It is a pure **maintainability** metric.

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime (Repair Time)}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

MTTR is composed of:

$$\text{MTTR} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{admin}} + T_{\text{logistics}} + T_{\text{repair}} + T_{\text{checkout}}$$

### 2.3.3 Inherent Availability (Ai)

The fundamental Maintenance Effectiveness equation linking RAM parameters:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Inherent Availability** — it includes only operating time and corrective maintenance time. **Achieved Availability** additionally includes preventive maintenance downtime and supply delays, while **Operational Availability** further includes administrative and logistics delays.

### 2.3.4 Reliability Function (R(t))

For a constant failure rate $\lambda$ (typical of the useful life in the "bathtub curve"):

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

Where the failure rate is the reciprocal of MTBF:

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$$

## 2.4 Cost-Effectiveness of Maintenance

Maintenance effectiveness is incomplete without economic justification. The **Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER)** is widely used:

$$\text{CER} = \frac{\text{Benefit Gained (Cost Avoided or Revenue Preserved)}}{\text{Total Maintenance Cost Incurred}}$$

A **CER > 1** indicates effective maintenance (benefits exceed costs). A **CER < 1** indicates the maintenance strategy is consuming more value than it preserves.

A related metric is the **Maintenance Cost as a Percentage of Replacement Asset Value (RAV)**:

$$\text{Maintenance Cost \%} = \frac{\text{Annual Maintenance Expenditure}}{\text{Replacement Value of Asset}} \times 100$$

**Industry Benchmark:** Best-in-class plants spend **3%–5%** of RAV on maintenance. Spending less than 2% often indicates deferred maintenance (which becomes a hidden liability); spending more than 8% indicates inefficient or excessive maintenance.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Table for KTU 2024 Exam Preparation**

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Benchmarks (World-Class)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Availability (A) | (Run Time $\div$ Planned Time) $\times 100$ | % | $\geq 90\%$ |
| Performance (P) | (Ideal Cycle $\times$ Pieces) $\div$ Run Time $\times 100$ | % | $\geq 95\%$ |
| Quality (Q) | Good Pieces $\div$ Total Pieces $\times 100$ | % | $\geq 99\%$ |
| OEE | $A \times P \times Q \div 10000$ | % | $\geq 85\%$ |
| MTBF | Total Uptime $\div$ Number of Failures | Hours | Asset-dependent |
| MTTR | Total Downtime $\div$ Number of Failures | Hours | $<$ 1 hour (critical equip.) |
| Inherent Availability $A_i$ | MTBF $\div$ (MTBF + MTTR) | Dimensionless | $\geq 0.99$ |
| Failure Rate $\lambda$ | $1 \div \text{MTBF}$ | Failures/hour | Asset-dependent |
| Reliability $R(t)$ | $e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Mission-specific |
| Maintenance Cost Intensity | (Annual Maint. Cost $\div$ RAV) $\times 100$ | % | $3\%-5\%$ |
| Cost-Effectiveness Ratio | Benefit $\div$ Cost | Dimensionless | $>$ 1 |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Maintenance Effectiveness Metrics

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** OEE is used to comply with **FDA 21 CFR Part 11** and validate equipment performance for batch production records.
- **Aerospace:** MTBF and MTTR are used to certify aircraft components under **DO-178C** and **DO-254** standards.
- **Power Generation:** Operational Availability dictates grid dispatch decisions and capacity payments.
- **Automotive (OEM):** OEE is a Tier-1 supplier KPI; failing to maintain OEE $\geq 85\%$ can lead to contract termination.
- **Oil & Gas:** Maintenance Effectiveness directly impacts **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** ratings of safety-instrumented systems (per IEC 61511).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: OEE from First Principles

We will rigorously derive the OEE formula from its constituent factors, explicitly showing every algebraic transition.

### Step 1 — Define Planned Production Time (PPT)

Let the scheduled calendar time of a shift be $T_{\text{shift}}$. The Planned Production Time is the time the asset was *expected* to produce, which is shift time minus any planned non-production activities (e.g., planned maintenance, breaks already accounted for):

$$T_{\text{PPT}} = T_{\text{shift}} - T_{\text{planned stops}}$$

### Step 2 — Define Downtime and Operating Time

Let $T_{\text{DT}}$ be the total **unplanned downtime** during the shift (breakdowns, setup adjustments, idling). Then the actual **Operating Time** is:

$$T_{\text{OT}} = T_{\text{PPT}} - T_{\text{DT}}$$

### Step 3 — Availability (A)

By definition, availability is the ratio of uptime to the time the asset *should* have been running:

$$A = \frac{T_{\text{OT}}}{T_{\text{PPT}}}$$

### Step 4 — Performance (P)

Let $N$ be the total pieces actually produced (counting good + defect). Let $T_{\text{c,ideal}}$ be the **Ideal Cycle Time** (theoretical minimum time per piece, as per design). The theoretical time required to make $N$ pieces at perfect speed is:

$$T_{\text{theoretical}} = N \times T_{\text{c,ideal}}$$

Performance compares the theoretical time to actual operating time:

$$P = \frac{T_{\text{theoretical}}}{T_{\text{OT}}} = \frac{N \times T_{\text{c,ideal}}}{T_{\text{OT}}}$$

### Step 5 — Quality (Q)

Let $N_{\text{good}}$ be the number of conforming pieces. Then:

$$Q = \frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N}$$

### Step 6 — Combine into OEE

OEE is defined as the product of the three factors:

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

Substituting the expressions derived above:

$$\text{OEE} = \left(\frac{T_{\text{OT}}}{T_{\text{PPT}}}\right) \times \left(\frac{N \times T_{\text{c,ideal}}}{T_{\text{OT}}}\right) \times \left(\frac{N_{\text{good}}}{N}\right)$$

### Step 7 — Algebraic Simplification

Notice that $T_{\text{OT}}$ cancels in the numerator of the second factor with the denominator of the first factor, and $N$ cancels in the third factor. The result is a beautifully simple expression:

$$\text{OEE} = \frac{N_{\text{good}} \times T_{\text{c,ideal}}}{T_{\text{PPT}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** OEE reduces to *"the time it would have taken to make all the good pieces at ideal speed, divided by the time we actually had to make them."* This is the single most intuitive interpretation of OEE and is frequently asked in KTU viva voce.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Inherent Availability from MTBF and MTTR

Consider a **single repairable asset** that alternates between two states:
- **UP** state: Duration MTBF (mean).
- **DOWN** state: Duration MTTR (mean).

### Step 1 — Define a Renewal Cycle

A complete cycle is one UP period followed by one DOWN period:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Availability

Availability is the fraction of total cycle time spent in the UP state:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{Time Up}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### Step 3 — Express in Terms of Failure Rate $\lambda$

Substituting $\lambda = 1 / \text{MTBF}$ and $\mu = 1 / \text{MTTR}$ (repair rate):

$$A_i = \frac{1 / \lambda}{1 / \lambda + 1 / \mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

This is the well-known **Markov two-state availability** formula used in reliability block diagrams.

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: OEE Calculation (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A CNC machining centre has the following operational data for one 8-hour shift:
- Scheduled shift time: **8 hours**
- Planned breaks: **30 minutes**
- Unplanned downtime (breakdowns + setup): **45 minutes**
- Total pieces produced: **280 pieces**
- Good pieces: **261 pieces**
- Design cycle time: **1.5 minutes per piece**

**Find: (a) Availability, (b) Performance, (c) Quality, (d) OEE.**

### Solution:

**Step (a) — Availability:**

$$T_{\text{PPT}} = 8 \times 60 - 30 = 450 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{\text{DT}} = 45 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{\text{OT}} = 450 - 45 = 405 \text{ minutes}$$

$$A = \frac{405}{450} \times 100 = 90.00\%$$

**Step (b) — Performance:**

$$T_{\text{theoretical}} = 280 \times 1.5 = 420 \text{ minutes}$$

$$P = \frac{420}{405} \times 100 = 103.70\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Performance can exceed 100% if the operator ran the machine *faster* than the ideal cycle. Per the strict OEE definition, Performance is **capped at 100%** in most software implementations (e.g., SEMI E10), because "over-speed" usually indicates an unsafe or non-sustainable condition. In academic KTU problems, however, students should report the calculated value and note the cap convention.

**Step (c) — Quality:**

$$Q = \frac{261}{280} \times 100 = 93.21\%$$

**Step (d) — OEE:**

$$\text{OEE} = \frac{90.00 \times 103.70 \times 93.21}{10000} = 86.99\%$$

This result places the CNC machine in the **"world-class"** band ($\geq 85\%$) per TPM benchmarks.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: MTBF, MTTR, and Availability

**Problem:** A pump has operated for **8760 hours** (1 year) and experienced **4 failures**. The total downtime for these 4 failures (from detection to restart) was **12 hours**.

**Find: (a) MTBF, (b) MTTR, (c) Inherent Availability.**

### Solution:

**Step (a) — MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{8760}{4} = 2190 \text{ hours per failure}$$

**Step (b) — MTTR:**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ hours per failure}$$

**Step (c) — Inherent Availability:**

$$A_i = \frac{2190}{2190 + 3} = \frac{2190}{2193} = 0.99863 = 99.86\%$$

This indicates highly effective maintenance of the pump.

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Reliability Function

**Problem:** A sensor has MTBF = 5000 hours. The maintenance engineer wants a **95% reliability** over a critical 100-hour mission. Is the sensor acceptable?

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute Failure Rate:**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.0002 \text{ failures/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Reliability Function:**

$$R(100) = e^{-0.0002 \times 100} = e^{-0.02} = 0.9802 = 98.02\%$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

Since $R(100) = 98.02\% \geq 95\%$, the sensor **meets** the mission requirement. It is acceptable for deployment.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Linking Maintenance Strategies to Effectiveness Gains

This matrix shows how specific maintenance interventions improve each effectiveness metric. This is **high-yield KTU content** for design-type questions.

| **Maintenance Strategy** | **Primary Effectiveness Gain** | **OEE Factor Improved** | **MTBF Effect** | **MTTR Effect** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Reduces unexpected failures | Availability | Increases | Decreases |
| Predictive Maintenance (PdM / CBM) | Eliminates unnecessary disassembly | Availability, Performance | Increases | Decreases |
| Condition Monitoring (Vibration, IR, Ultrasound) | Detects incipient failures | Availability, Performance | Increases | Decreases |
| RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance) | Optimizes PM intervals | All three (A, P, Q) | Increases | Decreases |
| TPM (Total Productive Maintenance) | Operator-driven early detection | Availability, Performance | Increases | Decreases |
| Spare Parts Management (OEM) | Reduces logistics delay | Availability | No effect | Decreases |
| Operator Training | Reduces human-error downtime | Availability, Quality | Increases | Decreases |
| Root Cause Analysis (RCA) | Eliminates recurring failures | Availability | Increases | Decreases |
| Design-Out Maintenance (Proactive) | Removes failure modes | All three | Increases | Decreases |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The OEE Tree — Six Big Losses and Their Effectiveness Levers

This block-level functional architecture maps the three OEE pillars to the six big losses and the maintenance strategies that address each.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["OEE Overall Equipment Effectiveness"] --> node2["Availability A"]
    node1 --> node3["Performance P"]
    node1 --> node4["Quality Q"]

    node2 --> node5["Downtime Loss 1: Equipment Failure"]
    node2 --> node6["Downtime Loss 2: Setup and Adjustment"]

    node3 --> node7["Speed Loss 3: Idling and Minor Stops"]
    node3 --> node8["Speed Loss 4: Reduced Speed"]

    node4 --> node9["Quality Loss 5: Process Defects"]
    node4 --> node10["Quality Loss 6: Startup Yield Loss"]

    node5 --> node11["Counter Strategy: PdM and PM"]
    node6 --> node12["Counter Strategy: SMED Techniques"]
    node7 --> node13["Counter Strategy: TPM Autonomy"]
    node8 --> node14["Counter Strategy: Root Cause Analysis"]
    node9 --> node15["Counter Strategy: Poka Yoke and SPC"]
    node10 --> node16["Counter Strategy: First Time Right Programs"]

    node11 --> node17["Maintenance Effectiveness Output"]
    node12 --> node17
    node13 --> node17
    node14 --> node17
    node15 --> node17
    node16 --> node17

    node17 --> node18["Higher OEE greater than 85 percent"]
    node17 --> node19["Lower Lifecycle Cost per Unit"]
    node17 --> node20["Improved Safety and Compliance"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: The RAM (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability) Triangle

This diagram illustrates how MTBF and MTTR are the engineering levers that determine Inherent Availability, which in turn drives Maintenance Effectiveness.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Reliability Domain"] --> nodeB["MTBF Mean Time Between Failures"]
    nodeC["Maintainability Domain"] --> nodeD["MTTR Mean Time To Repair"]

    nodeB --> nodeE["Inherent Availability Ai"]
    nodeD --> nodeE

    nodeE --> nodeF["Maintenance Effectiveness KPI"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Decisions on PM Intervals"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["Spare Parts Stocking Policy"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Outsourcing vs In house Repair"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Design for Maintainability DfM"]

    nodeB --> nodeK["Improved by RCM and PdM"]
    nodeD --> nodeL["Improved by Training and Modularity"]
    nodeK --> nodeE
    nodeL --> nodeE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: The Maintenance Effectiveness Hierarchy

This shows how strategic maintenance objectives cascade down to operational metrics.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Strategic Goal: World Class Manufacturing"] --> node2["Tactical Objective: Zero Breakdowns"]
    node1 --> node3["Tactical Objective: Zero Defects"]
    node1 --> node4["Tactical Objective: Zero Accidents"]

    node2 --> node5["Operational KPI: OEE greater than 85 percent"]
    node3 --> node6["Operational KPI: First Pass Yield greater than 99 percent"]
    node4 --> node7["Operational KPI: TRIR less than 0.5"]

    node5 --> node8["Leading Indicator: MTBF trending up"]
    node5 --> node9["Leading Indicator: MTTR trending down"]
    node5 --> node10["Leading Indicator: PM Compliance greater than 95 percent"]
    node5 --> node11["Leading Indicator: Maintenance Cost less than 5 percent of RAV"]

    node8 --> node12["Maintenance Effectiveness Score"]
    node9 --> node12
    node10 --> node12
    node11 --> node12
```

## 4.4 Interpretation of the Diagrams

- **Diagram 1 (OEE Tree):** Demonstrates that the three OEE factors each have two specific loss categories, and each loss has a targeted counter-strategy. This is the *diagnostic* side of Maintenance Effectiveness.
- **Diagram 2 (RAM Triangle):** Shows that Maintenance Effectiveness is *engineered* through deliberate manipulation of MTBF (reliability engineering) and MTTR (maintainability engineering). It links engineering decisions to operational outcomes.
- **Diagram 3 (Hierarchy):** Provides the *governance* view — strategic goals must be translated into tactical objectives, which must be measured by operational KPIs, which are driven by leading indicators. Maintenance Effectiveness is a **leading indicator**, not a lagging one.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Maintenance Effectiveness and distinguish it from Maintenance Efficiency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Maintenance Effectiveness** is the degree to which a maintenance program achieves its intended objectives of preserving asset function, availability, and reliability at optimal lifecycle cost. It is a *composite* metric encompassing availability, reliability, maintainability, and quality.

**Maintenance Efficiency**, in contrast, is a *resource-utilization* metric that measures how well maintenance resources (labour, time, spare parts) are consumed — e.g., jobs completed per man-hour, or cost per work order.

**Key Distinction:** Efficiency asks *"Are we doing the maintenance right?"* (minimum waste of resources). Effectiveness asks *"Are we doing the right maintenance?"* (maximum value preservation). A team can be highly efficient (low cost) but ineffective (e.g., doing PM on a non-critical asset while ignoring a critical one). The two must be balanced — and the KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes that **Effectiveness is the higher-order goal**; efficiency must serve effectiveness.

*(Award 1 mark for definition of each; 1 mark for distinction.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is OEE? List the three factors and write one formula for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** is a TPM metric that quantifies the fraction of scheduled production time that is *truly productive* — i.e., the time spent making good pieces at ideal speed.

The three factors are:

1. **Availability ($A$)** — The fraction of scheduled time the equipment is actually running.

$$A = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} \times 100$$

2. **Performance ($P$)** — The ratio of actual production rate to the design rate.

$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Pieces}}{\text{Operating Time}} \times 100$$

3. **Quality ($Q$)** — The fraction of conforming units.

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good Pieces}}{\text{Total Pieces}} \times 100$$

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q / 10000$$

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing factors, 1 mark for formulas.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Six Big Losses** model in TPM. How are these losses related to the three factors of OEE? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A packaging line operates for **2 shifts of 8 hours each** (16 hours total). Planned breaks total **1 hour per shift**. Unplanned downtime (breakdowns) is **2 hours per shift**. The line produced **6000 units per shift** at an ideal cycle time of **4 seconds per unit**. Defective units were **150 per shift**.

Compute: (i) Availability, (ii) Performance, (iii) Quality, (iv) OEE. Identify the dominant loss category and recommend one maintenance strategy to improve it. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a):

The Six Big Losses is a diagnostic taxonomy developed by Seiichi Nakajima (1988) within the TPM framework. It groups all equipment-related losses into six categories, each linked to a specific OEE factor:

| **Loss** | **Category** | **OEE Factor Affected** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Equipment Failure (Unplanned Breakdowns) | Downtime | Availability |
| 2. Setup and Adjustment Losses | Downtime | Availability |
| 3. Idling and Minor Stoppages | Speed | Performance |
| 4. Reduced Speed Losses | Speed | Performance |
| 5. Defect Losses (During Production) | Quality | Quality |
| 6. Startup / Reduced Yield Losses | Quality | Quality |

*(3 marks for the table/diagram.)*

The relationship is direct: **Losses 1 and 2 reduce Availability**, **Losses 3 and 4 reduce Performance**, and **Losses 5 and 6 reduce Quality**. OEE drops by the cumulative impact of all six losses, making this taxonomy a structured framework for root-cause investigation.

A neat block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) maps the OEE tree to the six losses. The maintenance strategy is selected based on which loss is dominant — for example, PdM addresses Loss 1; SMED addresses Loss 2; operator skill development addresses Losses 3 and 4; Poka-Yoke addresses Losses 5 and 6.

*(2 marks for the linkage explanation; 2 marks for the diagnostic-to-strategy mapping.)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b):

**Step 1 — Per-Shift Calculations:**

$$T_{\text{PPT per shift}} = 8 \times 60 - 60 = 420 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{\text{DT per shift}} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ minutes}$$

$$T_{\text{OT per shift}} = 420 - 120 = 300 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Availability:**

$$A = \frac{300}{420} \times 100 = 71.43\%$$

**Step 3 — Performance:**

$$T_{\text{theoretical}} = 6000 \times 4 \text{ s} = 24000 \text{ s} = 400 \text{ minutes}$$

$$P = \frac{400}{300} \times 100 = 133.33\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Performance > 100% Note:** Performance exceeds 100% in this case because the actual rate (6000 pieces / 300 min = 20 pieces/min) is faster than the ideal rate (1 piece / 4 s = 15 pieces/min). For KTU exam purposes, **report the calculated value (133.33%)** and add a remark that this is often capped at 100% in industry software. This shows the examiner that you understand the convention. *(1 mark penalty risk if the student simply writes "100%" without explanation.)*

**Step 4 — Quality:**

$$Q = \frac{6000 - 150}{6000} \times 100 = 97.50\%$$

**Step 5 — OEE:**

$$\text{OEE} = \frac{71.43 \times 133.33 \times 97.50}{10000} = 92.85\%$$

*(1 mark each for A, P, Q, and OEE — total 4 marks.)*

**Step 6 — Identify Dominant Loss:**

The **low Availability (71.43%)** is the bottleneck. The dominant loss is **Loss 1: Equipment Failure / Unplanned Breakdowns** (2 hours of unplanned downtime per shift is excessive).

**Step 7 — Recommended Maintenance Strategy:**

**Predictive Maintenance (Condition Monitoring)** — Install vibration sensors, thermography, and oil analysis on critical bearings and drives of the packaging line. This detects incipient failure before it becomes a breakdown, reducing unplanned downtime and improving Availability. Alternatively, **RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance)** can be deployed to optimize PM intervals based on actual failure modes.

*(3 marks for identifying dominant loss and recommending justified strategy.)*

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice): [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** Define and derive the **Inherent Availability** of a repairable system from first principles. How is it related to MTBF and MTTR? What are its limitations? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A critical compressor has been in service for **18 months (540 days, 24-hour operation)**. During this period, it failed **6 times** with the following downtime durations (in hours) per failure: **4, 6, 8, 3, 5, 10**. Compute the MTBF, MTTR, and Inherent Availability. If the target availability is **99.5%**, determine whether the current maintenance regime is effective, and recommend a corrective action. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a):

**Definition (2 marks):** Inherent Availability ($A_i$) of a repairable system is the fraction of *total time* (operating + corrective repair) during which the system is *expected* to be operational. It considers only hardware uptime and corrective maintenance downtime — *excluding* preventive maintenance, supply delays, and administrative/logistics time.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a single repairable asset alternating between UP and DOWN states in renewal cycles. Let:

- $\text{MTBF}$ = mean duration of the UP state
- $\text{MTTR}$ = mean duration of the DOWN state

A complete renewal cycle has duration:

$$T_{\text{cycle}} = \text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$$

By definition, availability is the ratio of UP time to total cycle time:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{UP time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

In terms of failure rate $\lambda$ and repair rate $\mu$ (where $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF}$ and $\mu = 1/\text{MTTR}$):

$$A_i = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Assumes constant failure rate (memoryless process), valid only in the useful life of the "bathtub curve."
- Excludes PM time, logistics delays, and administrative downtime — so it gives a *best-case* estimate.
- Not applicable to non-repairable items (use MTTF instead of MTBF).
- Ignores the stochastic nature of failures; actual availability has a distribution.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Total Operating Time and Downtime:**

Total operating time = 540 days × 24 hours = **12960 hours**

Total downtime = $4 + 6 + 8 + 3 + 5 + 10 = \mathbf{36 \text{ hours}}$

Number of failures = **6**

**Step 2 — Compute MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{12960}{6} = \mathbf{2160 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MTTR:**

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{36}{6} = \mathbf{6 \text{ hours}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Inherent Availability:**

$$A_i = \frac{2160}{2160 + 6} = \frac{2160}{2166} = \mathbf{0.99723 = 99.72\%}$$

*(1 mark each for MTBF, MTTR, and $A_i$ — total 3 marks.)*

**Step 5 — Compare with Target (1 mark):**

Target $A_i \geq 99.5\%$. Achieved $A_i = 99.72\%$. **The current maintenance regime is technically MEETING the target**, but only marginally (margin of 0.22 percentage points). The trend is fragile.

**Step 6 — Corrective Action (3 marks):**

The **MTTR of 6 hours** is the lever to improve. Note that one failure took **10 hours** to repair (an outlier dragging the average up). Recommended actions:

1. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** on the 10-hour failure — investigate why repair time was so long. Was it a spare parts shortage? Skill gap? Severe failure mode?
2. **Condition Monitoring (Vibration + Thermography)** on the compressor bearings and seals to detect the failure mode earlier, allowing planned repairs during scheduled downtime.
3. **Critical Spare Parts Stocking** for high-MTTR items (impellers, seals, bearings) to eliminate logistics-induced repair delays.
4. **Cross-Skilling of Maintenance Crew** to reduce MTTR.

These actions would reduce MTTR toward 2–3 hours, pushing $A_i$ above 99.9% — a robustly effective maintenance regime.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing OEE with "Efficiency":** OEE is *productivity of good output vs. scheduled time*. Do NOT use OEE as a synonym for general efficiency. Many students write "OEE = good output / total output" — this is **Quality**, not OEE. Full marks lost.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the /10000 in OEE:** When $A$, $P$, $Q$ are all in percent (e.g., 90, 95, 99), the product is $90 \times 95 \times 99 = 846450$, which represents *percent-squared-cubed*. Divide by $10000$ to get percent. Students who forget this get an OEE of "846450%" — automatic zero.
>
> 3. **Reporting Performance > 100% as 100% silently:** In KTU problems, always *show the calculation* and add a sentence: "Note: Performance > 100% in practice is often capped at 100% per SEMI E10 convention." This earns full credit and demonstrates depth.
>
> 4. **Using MTBF for non-repairable items:** The correct term for non-repairable items is **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)**, not MTBF. Examiners check this distinction.
>
> 5. **Mixing Inherent, Achieved, and Operational Availability:** Students who write "Availability = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR)" and call it "operational availability" lose 1 mark. **Inherent** Availability uses only corrective maintenance time; **Achieved** includes PM; **Operational** includes supply/admin delays.
>
> 6. **Skipping Units in the Final Answer:** Every numerical answer must have units (hours, %, etc.). "MTBF = 2160" is incomplete; "MTBF = 2160 hours" is correct.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [Maintenance Effectiveness is a **composite metric** — never a single number. It blends availability, performance, quality, reliability, and cost.]
- [The most widely used operational index is **OEE = A × P × Q**, with world-class benchmark ≥ 85%.]
- [**Inherent Availability** is given by $A_i = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — this is the most-tested formula in KTU.]
- [MTBF measures **reliability**; MTTR measures **maintainability**; both are levers of effectiveness.]
- [The **Six Big Losses** of TPM provide a diagnostic taxonomy mapping to A, P, Q — favourite 14-mark question structure.]
- [Maintenance Cost Intensity should be **3%–5% of RAV** for best-in-class plants.]
- [Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER) = Benefit ÷ Cost should be **> 1** for effective maintenance.]
- [Distinguish **Effectiveness** (doing the right maintenance) from **Efficiency** (doing maintenance right).]
- [Performance factor in OEE is **capped at 100%** in industry per SEMI E10; in KTU calculations, show the value and note the cap.]
- [Reliability function $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ assumes constant failure rate — valid in the useful-life region of the bathtub curve.]
- [For non-repairable items, use **MTTF** in place of MTBF.]
- [Predictive Maintenance improves **Availability** and **Performance**; TPM and SMED target the Six Big Losses directly.]
- [RCA (Root Cause Analysis) and RCM (Reliability-Centered Maintenance) are the *highest-leverage* interventions for improving Maintenance Effectiveness in the long term.]
- [Always state **units** in numerical answers and **cite standards** (SEMI E10, ISO 14224) where applicable to earn full marks in KTU 2024 ESE.]
