
### 4.4 Comparative Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["CLASSIFICATION OF MAINTENANCE ORGANISATION"]
    stepB["LINE ORGANISATION"]
    stepC["STAFF ORGANISATION"]
    stepD["LINE AND STAFF ORGANISATION"]
    stepE["DIRECT COMMAND VERTICAL"]
    stepF["ADVISORY HORIZONTAL"]
    stepG["HYBRID COMMAND PLUS ADVICE"]
    stepH["SMALL PLANTS REPETITIVE TASKS"]
    stepI["R AND D CONSULTANCY"]
    stepJ["LARGE MEDIUM INDUSTRIAL PLANTS"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepA --> stepC
    stepA --> stepD
    stepB --> stepE
    stepC --> stepF
    stepD --> stepG
    stepE --> stepH
    stepF --> stepI
    stepG --> stepJ
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Line Organisation and Staff Organisation in maintenance management.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Line Organisation:** Authority flows vertically in a direct command chain (Manager → Supervisor → Worker). Decisions are fast, discipline is strong, but there is no expert advice available. Best suited for small plants.
- **Staff Organisation:** Specialists provide expert advice horizontally without direct command authority. Decisions are technically superior but slower, and conflict between line and staff can arise. Best suited for R&D or highly specialised units.
- **Key Difference:** Line gives *orders*; Staff gives *advice*. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. What is meant by "Functional Authority" in a Line-and-Staff Maintenance Organisation? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Functional Authority** is the limited right granted to a staff specialist to issue mandatory instructions within their specialised domain, even to personnel outside their direct command chain.
- **Example:** A Safety Officer in a chemical plant can order a line production supervisor to halt operations if an unsafe condition is detected — overriding the line authority in this specific domain. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q(a) [7 Marks]: Explain in detail the features, advantages, and disadvantages of a Line Organisation in maintenance management. State two situations where it is most suitable.**

**Model Solution:**

**Features:**
- Oldest and simplest structure.
- Clear vertical chain of command (Scalar Chain — Fayol's Principle).
- Direct flow of authority from top to bottom.
- Unity of command is strictly maintained.
- No specialist staff positions exist.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, clear, and easy to operate.
- Fast decision-making due to direct authority.
- Strong discipline and accountability.
- Low administrative cost — no extra staff specialists needed.
- Quick communication along the vertical chain.

**Disadvantages:**
- Top manager becomes overloaded with decisions.
- Lack of expert/specialist advice.
- Limited scope for innovation or research-based maintenance (like RCM).
- Promotions limited within one department only.
- Not suitable for large plants with complex machinery.

**Suitable Situations:**
- Small-scale manufacturing units with fewer than 100 workers.
- Plants with repetitive, standardised maintenance tasks (e.g., small packaging units).
- Situations requiring very fast decision-making (e.g., emergency breakdown response).

**(7 marks — 1 mark each for features, 3 marks for advantages/disadvantages, 2 marks for situations)**

---

**Q(b) [7 Marks]: Compare Line Organisation and Staff Organisation in maintenance management using any five parameters in a tabular form.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Line Organisation | Staff Organisation |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority Flow** | Vertical, direct command | Horizontal, advisory only |
| **Decision Speed** | Very fast | Slow (consultations required) |
| **Specialisation** | Low / General | Very high / Expert |
| **Cost** | Low | High (multiple specialists) |
| **Suitability** | Small plants, repetitive tasks | R&D, consultancy, complex systems |
| **Unity of Command** | Strictly maintained | Not applicable |
| **Innovation** | Limited | Encouraged |

**Conclusion:** The choice between the two depends on plant size, complexity, and the need for specialisation. **(7 marks — 5 marks for table, 2 marks for conclusion)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q(a) [7 Marks]: Describe the Line-and-Staff Organisation in maintenance management with the help of a block diagram. List four advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Solution:**

**Description:**
The Line-and-Staff Organisation is a hybrid structure that combines the direct command chain of the line system with the expert advisory support of staff specialists. The line authority flows vertically (Manager → Supervisor → Worker), while staff officers (Reliability Engineer, Safety Officer, Planning Engineer) provide horizontal advice. In some cases, staff officers exercise **functional authority** in their specialised domains.

**Block Diagram:**

```
          PLANT MANAGER
                |
        MAINTENANCE MANAGER
        /        |        \
  Reliability   Safety    Planning
   Engineer    Officer    Engineer
   (Staff)     (Staff)    (Staff)
       \        |        /
    MECHANICAL / ELECTRICAL SUPERVISOR (LINE)
                |
        MAINTENANCE FOREMAN (LINE)
                |
        SKILLED TECHNICIANS (LINE)
```

**Advantages:**
- Expert advice available without diluting line authority.
- Better quality of maintenance decisions.
- Promotes both discipline (line) and innovation (staff).
- Suitable for large and complex industrial plants.
- Effective planning via maintenance planning engineers.

**Disadvantages:**
- Conflict between line and staff if roles are unclear.
- Higher administrative cost.

**(7 marks — 3 for description + diagram, 3 for advantages, 1 for disadvantages)**

---

**Q(b) [7 Marks]: "Modern industrial plants prefer Line-and-Staff Organisation for maintenance." Justify this statement with four reasons and explain the role of a Maintenance Planning Engineer as a staff officer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Justification (4 Reasons):**
- **Reason 1 — Combines Speed and Expertise:** Line gives quick decisions; Staff provides expert advice. This hybrid balances both.
- **Reason 2 — Suitable for Large Plants:** Modern plants are large with thousands of workers; pure line overloads managers, so staff support is essential.
- **Reason 3 — Handles Complex Equipment:** Modern machinery (CNC, robotics, PLC systems) needs specialist reliability engineers, not just foremen.
- **Reason 4 — Supports Modern Maintenance Philosophies:** TPM, RCM, and Predictive Maintenance require dedicated planners and analysts — pure line cannot support these.

**Role of Maintenance Planning Engineer (Staff Officer):**
- Prepares preventive maintenance schedules.
- Estimates downtime, spares, and manpower requirements.
- Coordinates between production and maintenance teams.
- Analyses breakdown history and suggests improvements.
- Advises line supervisors on job prioritisation without issuing direct orders.

**(7 marks — 4 for justification, 3 for staff role explanation)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic:**
> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing "Line Organisation" with "Production Organisation" — students often describe production hierarchy instead of *maintenance* hierarchy.
> - **Mistake 2:** Failing to draw a proper **block diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners award 2 marks specifically for diagrams; skipping them costs marks.
> - **Mistake 3:** Writing "Staff has no authority" — **incorrect**. Staff has advisory authority; in some cases, functional authority. Mention this distinction.
> - **Mistake 4:** Not mentioning **Fayol's principles** (Unity of Command, Scalar Chain) — these are high-weight keywords that examiners look for.
> - **Mistake 5:** Generic advantages/disadvantages copied from textbooks — examiners expect plant-specific examples (e.g., "cement plant", "refinery") for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - The three classifications are **Line**, **Staff**, and **Line-and-Staff**.
> - **Line** = Direct vertical command; fast decisions; low cost; small plants.
> - **Staff** = Horizontal advisory; expert guidance; high cost; R&D / complex units.
> - **Line-and-Staff** = Hybrid; combines command speed with expert advice; most widely used in modern industries.
> - **Functional Authority** = Limited authority of staff within their specialised domain (e.g., Safety Officer stopping unsafe work).
> - **Unity of Command** = Each worker reports to **only one superior** (Fayol's Principle).
> - **Scalar Chain** = Unbroken vertical line of authority.
> - **Span of Control** = Typically 5–8 subordinates per supervisor for optimal efficiency.
> - Plant size, process complexity, equipment criticality, and budget decide the organisation type.
> - Always include a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it carries 2 marks alone.
> - Mention **real industrial examples** (cement plant, refinery, power plant) to score higher.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Functional # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Functional Analysis (in Maintenance Engineering)** is a systematic, structured methodology used within Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) to identify the **intended purpose (functions)** of a physical asset, determine the **performance standards** expected of it, and pinpoint the precise conditions under which it is considered to have **lost its function (functional failures)**. In the framework of **SAE JA1011 / SAE JA1012 (RCM Standards)** adopted globally and reflected in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, functional analysis forms the **second of the seven classical RCM steps**, immediately after system selection and boundary definition. A **Function** is defined as the specific action or output that an asset is expected to perform in its operational context. Functions are broadly classified into: | Function Type | Operational Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | **Primary Function** | The main reason the asset was installed | Pump: deliver 100 m³/hr of water at 5 bar pressure | | **Secondary Functions** | Supporting roles such as containment, safety, comfort, control, aesthetics, or protection | Pump: contain water without leakage, operate without excessive noise (<85 dB) | | **Hidden Functions** | Functions whose failure would not be immediately obvious to operations staff but has significant consequences | Pump: standby pump must start on demand (failure not seen until primary fails) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Family Car** > > Imagine you own a car. Before you can decide *how* to maintain it, you must first clearly answer: **"What is this car supposed to DO?"** > > - **Primary Function:** Take my family from home to office safely at a reasonable speed (e.g., 0–100 km/h in <12 s). > - **Secondary Function:** Keep passengers dry during rain, keep the cabin cool in summer, protect occupants in a crash. > - **Functional Failure:** The car *cannot* take the family to office — not because every part is broken, but because it no longer meets the **performance standard** (e.g., brakes work, but stopping distance is now 60 m instead of the required 25 m → it has *functionally failed* even though no single component is "broken"). > > **Functional Analysis** is exactly this thought process — applied rigorously to industrial assets like pumps, motors, turbines, transformers, and CNC machines. The critical insight is that a **functional failure is NOT necessarily a physical failure**. An asset can be 100% physically intact (all original parts present, no cracks, no wear) and still be functionally failed because its performance has degraded below acceptable limits. ## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics and Standards > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Four Quantification Levels (per RCM Standard SAE JA1011):** > > Every function must be quantified using **observable, measurable performance standards**. KTU examiners expect students to explicitly mention: > > 1. **Desired Performance (DP):** The output level the user is *paying for* (e.g., 100 m³/hr flow). > 2. **Acceptable Performance Range:** A small tolerance window around DP (e.g., 100 ± 5 m³/hr). > 3. **Degraded Performance:** Output below the acceptable range but still partially useful. > 4. **Failed State:** Output below degraded performance; asset is functionally failed. > > Without quantification, functional analysis cannot proceed to failure mode analysis. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Functional Analysis Functional Analysis is **NOT** an equipment list or a parts inventory. It is a **top-down deductive reasoning process** that answers four sequential questions: > [!NOTE] > **The Four Sequential Questions of Functional Analysis:** > > **Q1. What are the operating contexts (modes) of the asset?** > → E.g., a pump operates in *normal mode*, *standby mode*, and *emergency fire-water mode*. > > **Q2. What does the asset do in each context?** > → List every Primary, Secondary, and Hidden function for each mode. > > **Q3. What is the expected performance standard for each function?** > → Quantify with units, tolerances, and reference conditions. > > **Q4. In what way can each function fail (functional failures)?** > → State the failure as the *loss or degradation of the named function*, NEVER as a failure of a part. ## 2.2 Functional Failures — The RCM Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Standard Wording):** > > *"A Functional Failure is a state of the asset in which it is no longer able to fulfil one or more of its functions to a standard of performance deemed acceptable by the user or regulatory body."* Functional Failures are categorised as follows: | Category | Definition | Consequence Type | |---|---|---| | **Total Functional Failure (TFF)** | Complete loss of the named function | Immediate, usually obvious | | **Partial Functional Failure (PFF)** | Function still available but below acceptable performance | Often hidden, insidious | | **On-Demand Failure (ODF)** | Function fails only when demanded (typical of standby/protective equipment) | Hidden until emergency | ## 2.3 Functional Block Diagram (FBD) — The Visual Logic Tool The **Functional Block Diagram** is a top-down hierarchical representation that shows: - **Inputs** (energy, material, information) entering the system - **System** (the asset itself) as a single bounded block - **Subsystems** (sub-blocks performing partial functions) - **Outputs** (desired product/service) It is *function-oriented*, NOT *component-oriented*. An FBD precedes the system block diagram and forms the bridge between operational requirements and failure analysis. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Equations and Metrics for Functional Analysis** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Performance Ratio} \;\; \eta &= \frac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Desired Output}} \times 100\% \\[6pt] \text{Functional Failure Index} \;\; FFI &= \frac{\text{No. of Functional Failures Observed}}{\text{Total Operating Hours}} \;\; [\text{failures/hr}] \\[6pt] \text{Function Availability} \;\; A_f &= \frac{\text{Hrs Asset Meets Functional Standard}}{\text{Total Calendar Hours}} \times 100\% \\[6pt] \text{On-Demand Reliability} \;\; R_{od} &= \frac{\text{Successful Demands}}{\text{Total Demands}} \\[6pt] \text{Mean Time Between Functional Failures} \;\; MTBFF &= \frac{\sum \text{Operating Time}}{\text{No. of Functional Failures}} \;\; [\text{hrs}] \end{aligned} $$ **Critical Boundary Conditions (often asked in KTU Part A):** | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | KTU Pitfall | |---|---|---|---| | $\eta$ | Performance ratio | % | Often confused with *efficiency* | | $A_f$ | Function availability | % | NOT MTBF — calendar-based, not operating-time-based | | $R_{od}$ | On-demand reliability | decimal | Used ONLY for standby/protective equipment | | $MTBFF$ | Mean time between functional failures | hrs | Functional, not physical metric | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where Functional Analysis is Used in Industry:** > > - **Aerospace:** FAA mandates functional analysis before any MSG-3 maintenance program. > - **Power Generation:** Identifying hidden failures in protection relays, fire-water pumps, and emergency diesel generators. > - **Oil & Gas (ONGC, BPCL, IOCL):** Mandatory per API RP 580/581 risk-based inspection methodology. > - **Manufacturing (KSIDC Kerala industries):** TPM pillar of *Planned Maintenance* uses functional analysis to define OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) loss categories. > - **KTU Relevance:** Functional analysis is the *gateway* to FMEA, RCM decision logic, and condition-monitoring task selection. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Functional Availability from First Principles > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example — Functional Availability Derivation** Consider an industrial centrifugal pump rated for **100 m³/hr flow at 5 bar discharge pressure**. Plant management has set the **acceptable performance window** as: $$ Q_{acceptable} = 100 \pm 5 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}, \quad P_{acceptable} = 5 \pm 0.25 \;\; \text{bar} $$ During a 720-hour month, the pump logged the following performance data: | Operating Block | Duration (hr) | Flow (m³/hr) | Pressure (bar) | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | Block 1 | 480 | 102 | 5.1 | Acceptable | | Block 2 | 120 | 96 | 4.8 | Acceptable | | Block 3 | 80 | 89 | 4.0 | Functional Failure (degraded) | | Block 4 | 40 | 0 | 0 | Functional Failure (total) | **Step 1: Identify blocks where function is met.** Block 1 and Block 2 fall within the acceptable window: $$ T_{acceptable} = 480 + 120 = 600 \;\; \text{hrs} $$ **Step 2: Identify functional failure blocks.** $$ T_{failed} = 80 + 40 = 120 \;\; \text{hrs} $$ **Step 3: Apply the Function Availability formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_f &= \frac{T_{acceptable}}{\text{Total Calendar Hours}} \times 100\% \\[6pt] &= \frac{600}{720} \times 100\% \\[6pt] &= 83.33\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation for KTU Board Answers:** > > The pump is **physically running** for 100% of the time (720/720 hrs), but **functionally available only 83.33%** of the time. This is precisely why KTU examiners emphasize that **functional availability is lower than operational availability** when degradation is present. ## 3.2 Derivation: On-Demand Reliability for Standby Equipment > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example — Emergency Fire Pump (Hidden Function)** A diesel-driven fire pump is tested monthly by manually starting it. Over **36 months**, the following test results were recorded: - Number of test demands: $n = 36$ - Number of successful starts: $k = 33$ - Number of failed starts: $3$ **Step 1: Apply the On-Demand Reliability formula.** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{od} &= \frac{k}{n} = \frac{33}{36} \\[6pt] &= 0.9167 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 91.67\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Comment on hidden failure implication.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** The fire pump is a *protective* asset with a **hidden function**. Its *physical* failure (won't start) was invisible for 33 months until the manual test caught it. This justifies periodic function-testing tasks in any RCM-derived maintenance plan. ## 3.3 Derivation: Functional Failure Index (FFI) Using the same 720-hr data: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{No. of Functional Failures} &= 2 \;\; (\text{Block 3 and Block 4}) \\[6pt] \text{Total Operating Hours} &= 720 \;\; \text{hrs} \\[6pt] FFI &= \frac{2}{720} = 0.00278 \;\; \text{failures/hr} \end{aligned} $$ This converts into a Mean Time Between Functional Failures: $$ \begin{aligned} MTBFF &= \frac{1}{FFI} = \frac{720}{2} = 360 \;\; \text{hrs} \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix for KTU Board Answers | Question Asked in Exam | Conceptual Hook | Key Equation to Cite | |---|---|---| | Define functional failure | SAE JA1011 standard wording | Loss of function to acceptable standard | | Differentiate functional vs physical failure | Example: brake pads worn vs stopping distance > spec | Performance ratio $\eta$ | | Calculate function availability | Time-based, calendar hours | $A_f = T_{acceptable}/T_{calendar}$ | | Identify hidden functions | Standby/protective equipment | On-demand reliability $R_{od}$ | | Explain FBD vs SBD | Function-first vs part-first | Hierarchical top-down logic | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Industrial Pumping System > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (Mermaid-safe alphanumeric node IDs, no markdown formatting inside labels): ```mermaid graph TD INPUT_A["FLUID INPUT
Raw Water from Reservoir"] INPUT_B["ELECTRICAL INPUT
3 Phase 415V 50Hz"] INPUT_C["CONTROL SIGNAL
SCADA Start Stop"] SYSMAIN["PUMP SYSTEM ASSET
Boundary Defined Block"] SUB_FUNCPUMP["FUNCTION PUMP UNIT
Primary Function Deliver Fluid"] SUB_FUNCMOTOR["FUNCTION MOTOR DRIVE
Convert Electrical to Mechanical"] SUB_FUNCSEAL["FUNCTION SEAL ASSEMBLY
Contain Fluid No Leakage"] SUB_FUNCCOOL["FUNCTION COOLING FAN
Maintain Winding Temperature"] OUT_FLOW["OUTPUT DESIRED
100 m3/hr at 5 bar"] OUT_SAFE["OUTPUT SAFETY
Leak Free Operation"] OUT_TEMP["OUTPUT THERMAL
Winding below 80 degree C"] FF1["FUNCTIONAL FAILURE 1
Insufficient Flow Output"] FF2["FUNCTIONAL FAILURE 2
Loss of Containment Leak"] FF3["FUNCTIONAL FAILURE 3
Motor Overheat Trip"] INPUT_A --> SYSMAIN INPUT_B --> SYSMAIN INPUT_C --> SYSMAIN SYSMAIN --> SUB_FUNCPUMP SYSMAIN --> SUB_FUNCMOTOR SYSMAIN --> SUB_FUNCSEAL SYSMAIN --> SUB_FUNCCOOL SUB_FUNCPUMP --> OUT_FLOW SUB_FUNCSEAL --> OUT_SAFE SUB_FUNCMOTOR --> OUT_TEMP SUB_FUNCCOOL --> OUT_TEMP SUB_FUNCPUMP -.Failure State.-> FF1 SUB_FUNCSEAL -.Failure State.-> FF2 SUB_FUNCMOTOR -.Failure State.-> FF3 ``` ## 4.2 Hierarchical RCM Process Showing Where Functional Analysis Fits ```mermaid graph TD STAGE_A["RCM STAGE 1
System Selection and Boundary Definition"] STAGE_B["RCM STAGE 2
Functional Analysis FUNCTIONS and STANDARDS"] STAGE_C["RCM STAGE 3
Failure Modes and Effects Analysis FMEA"] STAGE_D["RCM STAGE 4
Consequence Categorisation"] STAGE_E["RCM STAGE 5
RCM Decision Logic Task Selection"] STAGE_F["RCM STAGE 6
Implementation of PM Plan"] STAGE_G["RCM STAGE 7
Continuous Improvement and Review"] GATE1["GATE: Are Functions Clearly Defined Quantified"] GATE2["GATE: Are Failure Modes Associated with Each Functional Failure"] STAGE_A --> STAGE_B --> GATE1 GATE1 -- Yes --> STAGE_C GATE1 -- No --> STAGE_B STAGE_C --> GATE2 GATE2 -- Yes --> STAGE_D GATE2 -- No --> STAGE_C STAGE_D --> STAGE_E --> STAGE_F --> STAGE_G ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why This Diagram Matters for KTU Answers:** > > When the KTU question asks *"Discuss the role of functional analysis in RCM"*, the examiner expects a process diagram showing Functional Analysis as **Stage 2**, immediately after system definition and before FMEA. Missing this sequence will cost 2–3 marks. ## 4.3 Functional Failure Identification Matrix (Mermaid View) ```mermaid graph LR F1["FUNCTION 1
Primary Deliver Fluid"] FF1["F FAILURE
Reduced Flow"] FF2["F FAILURE
No Flow Total"] FM1["FAILURE MODE 1
Impeller Worn"] FM2["FAILURE MODE 2
Suction Line Blocked"] FM3["FAILURE MODE 3
Shaft Broken"] CAUSE1["ROOT CAUSE
Cavitation"] CAUSE2["ROOT CAUSE
Foreign Object"] CAUSE3["ROOT CAUSE
Fatigue"] F1 --> FF1 F1 --> FF2 FF1 --> FM1 FF1 --> FM2 FF2 --> FM3 FM1 --> CAUSE1 FM2 --> CAUSE2 FM3 --> CAUSE3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** The diagram above explicitly shows the **logical chain**: Function → Functional Failure → Failure Mode → Root Cause. This is the exact sequence required by SAE JA1011. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Modeled on KTU University Exam pattern (Dec 2023 / June 2024 style).** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *"Define the term 'Functional Failure' as applied in Reliability-Centered Maintenance. Differentiate it from a physical failure with one industrial example."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Functional Failure, as defined under **SAE JA1011 RCM Standard**, is a state in which an asset is no longer able to fulfil one or more of its intended functions to a standard of performance deemed acceptable by the user or regulatory body. > > **Difference from Physical Failure:** > > - *Physical Failure:* A component is broken, cracked, worn-out, or missing. > - *Functional Failure:* The asset fails to meet its performance standard — even though all components may be physically intact. > > **Industrial Example:** A centrifugal pump delivers 70 m³/hr against a contractual requirement of 100 m³/hr due to internal wear. All components are physically present and not broken, yet the pump is **functionally failed** because it does not meet the performance standard. ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *"List and briefly explain the three categories of functions identified during Functional Analysis."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** > 1. **Primary Function:** The main reason the asset exists in the system. *Example:* A compressor's primary function is to deliver compressed air at 7 bar pressure. > 2. **Secondary Function:** Supporting functions such as containment, safety, control, comfort, aesthetics, or protection. *Example:* The compressor must contain refrigerant without leakage and operate below 85 dB noise. > 3. **Hidden Function:** A function whose failure is not immediately apparent to operations staff and is revealed only when the function is demanded or inspected. *Example:* An emergency fire pump must start on demand — its failure remains hidden until the fire event occurs. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > > Common pitfalls that cost marks in Functional Analysis questions: > 1. Writing "component failure" instead of "functional failure" — examiners deduct 1–2 marks. > 2. Forgetting to **quantify** performance standards with units. > 3. Ignoring **hidden functions** — this is a KTU favourite for 2-mark sub-parts. > 4. Confusing *operational availability* with *functional availability*. > 5. Failing to cite the RCM standard (SAE JA1011) — usually costs 1 mark in definitions. ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 4 Choice 1]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **Functional Analysis** as applied in Reliability-Centered Maintenance. With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Functional Block Diagram (FBD)** of an industrial cooling water pump system and identify **three functional failures** associated with it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A boiler feed pump is rated to deliver **120 m³/hr at 12 bar**. During a 30-day month (720 hrs), the operating log shows: | Block | Duration (hr) | Flow (m³/hr) | Pressure (bar) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 400 | 121 | 12.1 | | 2 | 200 | 118 | 11.8 | | 3 | 80 | 95 | 9.0 | | 4 | 40 | 0 | 0 | Calculate the **Functional Availability** and the **Mean Time Between Functional Failures (MTBFF)**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** > **Definition (2 marks):** Functional Analysis is the second stage of RCM (per SAE JA1011) in which the **intended functions** of an asset are systematically identified, **performance standards quantified**, and the conditions under which each function is considered *failed* are explicitly stated. > > **Three Function Types (1 mark):** Primary, Secondary, Hidden. > > **Functional Block Diagram of Cooling Water Pump (2 marks):** Top-level block receives fluid input, electrical input, and control signal. Sub-blocks perform functions of pumping, sealing, cooling, and driving. Outputs are delivered flowrate, leak-free containment, and acceptable noise. > > **Three Functional Failures (2 marks):** > > - **FF1:** Pump delivers less than rated flow (e.g., 80 m³/hr vs required 100 m³/hr) → *partial functional failure*. > - **FF2:** Pump fails to deliver any flow on start command → *total functional failure*. > - **FF3:** Pump leaks process fluid externally → *secondary functional failure (containment loss)*. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **Step 1: Identify acceptable performance window.** $$ Q_{acceptable} = 120 \pm 6 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr}, \quad P_{acceptable} = 12 \pm 0.6 \;\; \text{bar} $$ **Step 2: Identify acceptable blocks.** Block 1: 121 m³/hr, 12.1 bar → **Acceptable (within tolerance)** Block 2: 118 m³/hr, 11.8 bar → **Acceptable (within tolerance)** Block 3: 95 m³/hr, 9.0 bar → **FUNCTIONAL FAILURE** Block 4: 0 m³/hr, 0 bar → **FUNCTIONAL FAILURE** $$ T_{acceptable} = 400 + 200 = 600 \;\; \text{hrs} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Functional Availability.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_f &= \frac{600}{720} \times 100\% \\[6pt] &= 83.33\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Calculate MTBFF.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{No. of functional failures} &= 2 \\[6pt] \text{Operating hours} &= 720 \;\; \text{hrs} \\[6pt] MTBFF &= \frac{720}{2} = 360 \;\; \text{hrs} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Comment (1 mark).** > The pump operates physically throughout the month but its **functional availability is only 83.33%**. The 16.67% functional downtime was caused by partial and total performance failures. This indicates that **condition-monitoring tasks** (vibration analysis of bearings, flow-vs-head trending) should be introduced to detect the gradual performance degradation evident in Block 3 before it escalates to total failure. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module 4 Choice 2]** **(a)** Discuss the **four sequential questions** that govern Functional Analysis in RCM. Explain the difference between **Operational Availability, Functional Availability, and On-Demand Reliability** with one industrial example for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An emergency diesel generator must start within **15 seconds** of a power-fail signal (hidden function). Over **48 test demands**, it started successfully in **45** cases and failed to start in **3** cases. The generator operates an average of **2 hrs per month** in scheduled tests. Calculate: (i) On-Demand Reliability (ii) Mean Time Between Functional Failures (assuming the 3 failures occurred during the 48 demands spanning 48 months of testing) Comment on the maintenance implications. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** **Four Sequential Questions (4 marks):** 1. *What are the operating contexts of the asset?* — Identify modes: normal, standby, emergency, start-up, shut-down. 2. *What does the asset do in each context?* — Enumerate Primary, Secondary, Hidden functions for each mode. 3. *What is the expected performance standard for each function?* — Quantify with units, tolerances, and reference conditions. 4. *In what way can each function fail?* — State functional failures as **loss or degradation** of the named function. **Differences between Availability Metrics (3 marks):** | Metric | Definition | Industrial Example | |---|---|---| | **Operational Availability $A_o$** | Fraction of time the asset is *physically operating* (regardless of whether it meets performance) | A pump runs for 700/720 hrs = 97.22% — but at degraded flow | | **Functional Availability $A_f$** | Fraction of time the asset *meets its performance standard* | Same pump meets 100 m³/hr spec for 600/720 hrs = 83.33% | | **On-Demand Reliability $R_{od}$** | Probability that a standby/emergency asset performs its function when demanded | Fire pump starts successfully 33/36 times = 91.67% | --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **(i) On-Demand Reliability:** $$ \begin{aligned} R_{od} &= \frac{k}{n} = \frac{45}{48} \\[6pt] &= 0.9375 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 93.75\% \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) MTBFF:** The 3 functional failures occurred during 48 demand events spanning 48 months of testing. The total operating time during this period (assuming 2 hrs per demand test): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{total} &= 48 \times 2 = 96 \;\; \text{hrs} \\[6pt] MTBFF &= \frac{96}{3} = 32 \;\; \text{hrs} \end{aligned} $$ **Maintenance Implications (1 mark):** > The diesel generator has a high on-demand reliability of 93.75% but the 3 failures are **hidden** — they would not be detected without scheduled test demands. KTU examiner note: This justifies the inclusion of **periodic function-testing tasks** (e.g., monthly no-load start test) in the preventive maintenance schedule. Additionally, root-cause analysis of the 3 start failures (battery voltage drop, fuel line air-locking, glow-plug failure) should drive a **predictive task** (monthly battery health check) into the maintenance plan. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:** > > ✅ **Functional Analysis = Stage 2 of RCM** (per SAE JA1011) — comes *after* system definition, *before* FMEA. > > ✅ **Always quantify functions** with units and tolerance windows — KTU examiners deduct marks if functions are stated qualitatively only. > > ✅ **Three function types:** Primary (main purpose), Secondary (containment/safety/comfort), Hidden (failure not obvious — standby equipment). > > ✅ **Functional Failure ≠ Physical Failure.** Functional failure is *loss of function to acceptable standard*; physical failure is *component breakage*. They are NOT the same. > > ✅ **Functional Availability $A_f$** is calendar-time-based, NOT operating-time-based. Always divide by total calendar hours. > > ✅ **On-Demand Reliability $R_{od}$** applies *only* to protective/standby assets where failure is hidden until demand. > > ✅ **MTBFF = Total Operating Hours / No. of Functional Failures** — uses operating time, not calendar time. > > ✅ **Functional Block Diagram (FBD)** precedes System Block Diagram (SBD) and is function-oriented, not part-oriented. > > ✅ **Logical Chain to Memorise:** Function → Functional Failure → Failure Mode → Root Cause → Effects → Consequences. > > ✅ **Hidden function detection** is worth 2 marks in any KTU Part B question — always mention standby fire pumps, emergency generators, relief valves, and protection relays as examples. > > ✅ **Industrial Examples to Quote in Answers:** Centrifugal pump, boiler feed pump, cooling tower fan, CNC spindle, diesel generator, fire pump, hydraulic press. > > ✅ **Standard to Cite:** Always write *"as per SAE JA1011 / SAE JA1012 RCM Standard"* at the start of any functional analysis definition for full marks.

topics.title: Centralized and Decentralized Maintenance Organisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET613 - MAINTENANCE ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Centralized and Decentralized Maintenance Organisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Maintenance Organisation – Formal Definition

A **Maintenance Organisation** is the formal structural framework of personnel, departments, hierarchies, communication channels, and authority relationships established within an industrial plant to plan, execute, supervise, and control all maintenance activities (preventive, predictive, corrective, and shutdown) on physical assets.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> Maintenance organisation refers to the *systematic arrangement of human resources, technical facilities, and authority relationships* responsible for retaining machinery and equipment in, or restoring them to, a state in which they can perform their required functions.

The **form of organisation** determines:
- **Who** decides the maintenance priorities
- **Where** the maintenance crews are physically located
- **How** spare parts, tools, and skills are dispatched
- **How quickly** the plant can respond to a breakdown

The two fundamental structural archetypes are **Centralised**, **Decentralised**, and the practical **Combined (Hybrid)** model.

---

## 1.2 Centralised Maintenance Organisation – Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centralised Maintenance** is a structural form in which **all maintenance personnel, workshops, spares stores, and decision-making authority** are pooled under a single, top-level maintenance executive (typically the *Maintenance Manager* or *Chief Engineer – Maintenance*) and dispatched to various production units as and when required.

**Key Identifier:** A *single* budget, *single* spare-parts inventory, and *one* maintenance planning cell serving the *entire* plant.

---

## 1.3 Decentralised Maintenance Organisation – Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decentralised Maintenance** is a structural form in which maintenance crews, small workshops, and limited spare-parts inventories are **physically and administratively attached to individual production units** (sectors, shops, or departments), each headed by a *Unit Maintenance Engineer* who reports independently to plant management.

**Key Identifier:** *Multiple* mini-maintenance departments, each self-contained for routine work, with shared services only for highly specialised jobs.

---

## 1.4 Combined (Hybrid) Maintenance Organisation

A pragmatic blend where **routine, low-skill work is decentralised** at the production unit level, but **specialised, capital-intensive work (overhauls, large repairs, calibration) is centralised** in a Central Workshop. This is the *most widely adopted* form in modern large-scale process plants.

---

## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy – Making It Instantly Clear

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Hospital System**
>
> **Centralised Maintenance ≈ One Super-Speciality Hospital for a Whole City**
> – All doctors, operation theatres, MRI machines, and the blood bank are at *one* location. Patients travel to the hospital; specialists are dispatched from there. Highly efficient use of costly equipment (e.g., a ₹50 Crore MRI is bought *once*). However, for a small fever or a sprain, the patient still has to travel to the central hospital — slow response.
>
> **Decentralised Maintenance ≈ A Network of Neighbourhood Clinics**
> – Each locality has its own small clinic with a General Physician, a small pharmacy, and basic equipment. Common ailments treated instantly nearby. But for a complex heart surgery, the patient must still travel to a central facility.
>
> **Combined Maintenance ≈ Hospital Chain (Apollo / Fortis Model)**
> – Local Apollo Clinics in every neighbourhood for routine check-ups, but complex surgeries routed to the central flagship hospital. This gives *both* speed *and* specialist depth.

For an engineering student reading this for the first time: **think of your campus**. *Centralised* = one single Maintenance Office for the entire campus. *Decentralised* = each department has its own lab attendant. *Combined* = department-level attendants for bulbs/fuses, but a central estate office for ACs and lifts.

---

## 1.6 Why This Distinction Matters in KTU Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners *frequently* frame 7-mark sub-questions as: *"Discuss the maintenance organisation suitable for a large fertiliser plant with geographically separated production units."* The correct answer requires recognising that **plant size, layout, and equipment criticality** dictate the choice — not a universal preference. Section 2 builds this analytical reasoning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Centralised Maintenance Organisation

### 2.1.1 Hierarchical Structure (Top-Down)

| Level | Designation | Core Function |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | **Maintenance Director / Chief Engineer** | Strategic planning, budget, KPI monitoring |
| L2 | **Maintenance Planning Manager** | Job scheduling, PM planning, shutdown coordination |
| L3 | **Discipline Heads** (Mechanical, Electrical, Instrumentation, Civil) | Technical leadership within discipline |
| L4 | **Foremen / Supervisors** (Central Workshop & Field) | Crew supervision, on-floor execution |
| L5 | **Skilled Technicians, Fitters, Welders, Electricians** | Hands-on repair and overhaul work |
| Support | **Central Spare-Parts Store, Central Tool Room, Central Machine Shop** | Logistics backbone for all units |

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic – Step by Step

1. Production unit raises a **work request** (corrective or planned).
2. Request flows to **Central Maintenance Planning Cell**.
3. Planner prioritises against *equipment criticality ranking* and *production schedule*.
4. Job is dispatched to the appropriate **central workshop** (machine shop, electrical bay, instrument lab) or to a **field crew** sent to the unit.
5. Spares are drawn from the **Central Store** against an indented BOM.
6. Job completion, time, and cost are recorded; data fed back to L2 for KPI analysis.

### 2.1.3 Information & Authority Flow

$$\text{Authority Flow} \rightarrow \text{Top-Down} \quad ; \quad \text{Work Requests \& Feedback} \rightarrow \text{Bottom-Up}$$

The **single point of accountability** simplifies audits, ISO 9001 / ISO 55001 certification, and statutory compliance reporting.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Decentralised Maintenance Organisation

### 2.2.1 Structural Logic

Each **Production Unit** (Unit A, Unit B, Unit C) possesses:

- Its own **Unit Maintenance Engineer** (reports to *Unit Production Manager*, not a central authority)
- A small **multi-skilled crew** (fitters, electricians, instrument mechanics)
- A **local sub-store** with fast-moving consumables (belts, fuses, gaskets, lubricants)
- A **local tool kit**

Highly specialised jobs (large motor rewinding, gearbox overhaul, boiler inspection) are sent to a small *common facility* OR outsourced to OEMs.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic – Step by Step

1. Breakdown occurs on Unit B → reported *immediately* to **Unit B's Maintenance In-Charge**.
2. Decision taken *on-the-spot* by the unit in-charge (no central approval needed).
3. Local crew dispatches, draws from local sub-store, completes job.
4. Only complex jobs are escalated; record-keeping remains *unit-level*.

> [!NOTE]
> The **decision latency** (time from breakdown to action start) drops drastically because there is no bureaucratic layer between the equipment and the person who authorises repair.

---

## 2.3 Combined (Hybrid) Maintenance Organisation

| Activity Type | Handled By | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Routine lubrication, cleaning, minor adjustments | **Decentralised unit crew** | Speed, low skill, high frequency |
| Preventive maintenance, condition monitoring | **Central Planning Cell + unit crew execution** | Standardisation, data integration |
| Major overhauls, capital repairs, specialised calibration | **Central Workshop / OEM** | High skill, costly equipment, low frequency |
| Spare parts (fast-moving consumables) | **Local sub-stores** | Immediate availability |
| Spare parts (slow-moving, high-value) | **Central Store** | Inventory cost optimisation |
| Budget & KPI reporting | **Central maintenance accounts** | Single P&L view |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **not for memorising** but for **analysing and justifying** the choice of organisation during the 14-mark descriptive questions. Examiner's expectation: *quantitative reasoning behind the qualitative choice*.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol Legend | Typical Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | $A$ = Availability, $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures, $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair | Comparing responsiveness of central vs. unit crews. Lower $MTTR \Rightarrow$ decentralised wins. |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{\sum (T_i \cdot F_i) \cdot M_s}{H_a \cdot \eta}$ | $N$ = No. of maintenance workers, $T_i$ = avg repair time for equipment $i$ (h), $F_i$ = failure frequency, $M_s$ = shift multiplier, $H_a$ = available hours/worker/year ($\approx 1800$ h), $\eta$ = efficiency factor ($0.75$–$0.85$) | Manpower planning for *either* organisation form. |
| 3 | $C_{inv} = (0.05 \text{ to } 0.15) \times V_{rep}$ | $C_{inv}$ = recommended spare-parts inventory value, $V_{rep}$ = plant replacement value | Decides whether central store is economically viable. |
| 4 | $R_m = \dfrac{N_m}{N_e}$ | $R_m$ = maintenance manning ratio, $N_m$ = maintenance headcount, $N_e$ = total equipment units | Industry benchmark: process plants $R_m \approx 0.15$–$0.25$ |
| 5 | $T_r = T_{det} + T_{log} + T_{mob} + T_{rep}$ | $T_r$ = total response time, $T_{det}$ = detection, $T_{log}$ = logistics, $T_{mob}$ = mobilisation, $T_{rep}$ = actual repair | Lower $T_{mob}$ strongly favours decentralisation. |
| 6 | $S_{req} = \dfrac{C_{annual} \cdot K}{Q}$ | $S_{req}$ = spare stock to hold, $C_{annual}$ = annual consumption, $K$ = safety factor, $Q$ = order quantity | Justifies split between local sub-store and central store. |
| 7 | $P_{mc} = \dfrac{C_m}{V_{rep}} \times 100\%$ | $P_{mc}$ = annual maintenance cost as % of replacement value, $C_m$ = annual maintenance budget | Industry norm: $3\%$–$6\%$ for healthy plants. |

> **Critical Note on LaTeX:** Throughout these notes, the modulus symbol is rendered as $\vert x \vert$ (never using a bare vertical pipe), to maintain clean markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.5 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why a single point of authority?** Because it eliminates conflicting priorities between units competing for the same crew — the central planner arbitrates fairly based on *criticality*, not *loudness*.
- **How does centralisation save money?** By *pooling* expensive resources (CNC machine, balancing machine, winding shop) — one ₹2 Cr balancing machine serves the whole plant instead of three ₹2 Cr machines in three units.
- **Why does decentralisation save time?** Because the *mobilisation* component ($T_{mob}$) in Eq. (5) is eliminated — the crew is already on-site.
- **How is the hybrid form the engineering compromise?** It captures the **economy of scale** of centralisation for *capital-intensive* resources and the **speed of response** of decentralisation for *time-critical* work.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Power Plants (NTPC, KSEB):** Strictly centralised for boiler-turbine overhauls (₹100 Cr+ equipment), with decentralised *housekeeping* crews at the unit level.
- **Petrochemical Complexes (Reliance Jamnagar, BPCL Kochi):** Hybrid form is the norm — each cracker has unit crews, but the *rotating-equipment central workshop* and *catalyst-handling facility* are centralised.
- **Automotive Assembly (Maruti, Tata Motors):** Highly centralised *tooling and robotics* maintenance (specialist skill), decentralised *conveyor and line-side* maintenance.
- **Cement Plants:** Predominantly centralised because of geographic compactness and high equipment criticality (kiln, raw mill).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Crew-Size Derivation (For Manpower Justification in 14-Mark Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners often award full marks (7/7) only when the answer includes a *quantitative justification*, not just qualitative listing. Use this derivation in the answer for **Question A (a)** in Section 5.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A process plant has **5 critical centrifugal pumps**. Each pump fails on average **3 times/year**. Average repair time per failure = **6 hours**. The plant runs **3 shifts/day**, **300 days/year**. Standard crew efficiency $\eta = 0.80$, available hours per worker per year $H_a = 1800$ h. Determine the number of mechanics required for the pump maintenance team.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 – Compute total annual repair hours required (load on the system)**

$$
L_{annual} = N_e \cdot F \cdot T_i
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
L_{annual} = 5 \times 3 \times 6 = 90 \text{ hours/year}
$$

*Logic:* 5 pumps × 3 failures each = 15 failures per year. Each failure consumes 6 hours of mechanic time. Total = 15 × 6 = 90 person-hours of *pure* repair work per year.

**Step 2 – Apply the shift multiplier $M_s$**

The pumps run continuously (3 shifts). Failures can occur in *any* shift, so a maintenance mechanic must be available in *each* shift, but the *average load per mechanic* per shift is reduced by the shift-count.

$$
L_{per\_worker} = \dfrac{L_{annual}}{M_s} = \dfrac{90}{3} = 30 \text{ hours/year per shift position}
$$

*Logic:* 3 mechanics are *available simultaneously* (one per shift). The total 90 hours of repair work is spread across them.

**Step 3 – Account for efficiency and available hours**

Each mechanic works only 1800 h/year *in total* across all shifts, but at $\eta = 0.80$, the *productive* hours = $1800 \times 0.80 = 1440$ h/year.

**Step 4 – Compute crew size using the master formula**

$$
N = \dfrac{L_{annual}}{H_a \cdot \eta \cdot M_s}
$$

Substituting:

$$
N = \dfrac{90}{1800 \times 0.80 \times 3} = \dfrac{90}{4320} \approx 0.0208
$$

Since $N < 1$, it means a *fractional* mechanic is needed — practically, the plant will *share* one mechanic with another team or outsource pump repairs. This *quantitatively* justifies **centralising** the pump-overhaul skill at a central workshop that serves multiple plants, rather than maintaining a *dedicated decentralised* crew at each unit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Whenever $N < 1$ for a *specialised* skill, the cost-effective choice is centralisation + multi-plant sharing. Whenever $N \geq 1$ for *general* skills (fitters, electricians) per unit, decentralisation is justified.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Comparative Matrix (Use in 14-Mark Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award the full 7 marks for comparison only when the table has *at least 8–10 parameters*, *not just 3 or 4*. Use this exhaustive matrix.

| # | Parameter | Centralised Maintenance | Decentralised Maintenance | Combined (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Span of Control** | Wide — one manager oversees entire plant | Narrow — each unit has its own in-charge | Mixed: narrow at unit, wide at central workshop |
| 2 | **Response Time ($T_{mob}$)** | High — crew must travel to unit | Low — crew is *already* on-site | Low for routine, moderate for specialised |
| 3 | **Resource Utilisation** | Excellent (high equipment pool utilisation) | Poor (duplicate tools lying idle in each unit) | Good — duplication only where needed |
| 4 | **Skill Specialisation** | High — mechanics specialise in equipment type | Limited — multi-skilled generalists | Best of both worlds |
| 5 | **Spare-Parts Inventory Cost** | Low (single pool) | High (duplication across units) | Optimised (fast-moving local, slow-moving central) |
| 6 | **Coordination Complexity** | Low at unit level, high at planning cell | Low at unit, high between units (competing priorities) | Moderate but manageable |
| 7 | **Best Suited For** | Compact plants, few critical machines, high capital cost equipment | Large plants with *geographically separated* units, many similar machines | Most modern process plants |
| 8 | **Record Keeping & KPI Tracking** | Excellent — uniform data systems | Inconsistent — each unit may use different formats | Centralised KPIs, local execution logs |
| 9 | **Cost of Supervision** | Low per worker (one supervisor oversees many) | High (each unit has its own supervisor) | Moderate |
| 10 | **Worker Morale & Ownership** | Lower — workers feel detached from production | Higher — workers see direct impact of their work | High at unit level, expertise satisfaction at centre |
| 11 | **Disaster Recovery** | Vulnerable — central workshop hit = plant down | Resilient — each unit can limp along | Highly resilient |
| 12 | **Training & Up-skilling** | Easy — central training school | Difficult — fragmented across units | Central school + unit OJT (best practice) |
| 13 | **Suitability for Shutdown Work** | Excellent — dedicated central planning | Difficult — competing shutdowns clash | Excellent |
| 14 | **Statutory Compliance** | Easy — single point of audit | Complicated — multiple audits | Manageable |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Decision Framework (How to Arrive at the Right Choice)

Use this **decision-tree logic** in 14-mark answers to demonstrate *engineering judgement*:

**Step 1 — Assess Plant Geometry**
- Plant occupies < 0.5 km² and is contiguous → **Centralised is feasible**
- Plant sprawls across > 2 km² or multiple blocks → **Decentralised preferred** for response time

**Step 2 — Assess Equipment Criticality**
- Few, high-value critical assets (e.g., one 500 MW turbine) → **Centralised** (specialist attention)
- Many similar, low-criticality assets (e.g., 200 conveyor motors) → **Decentralised** (multi-skilled local crews)

**Step 3 — Assess Capital Cost of Tools/Equipment**
- Tool cost > ₹10 Cr (e.g., CNC rotor lathe, large dynamic balancer) → **Centralise** (avoid duplication)
- Tool cost < ₹5 Lakh per item (e.g., drilling machine, multimeter) → **Decentralise** (duplicate freely)

**Step 4 — Assess Workforce Skill Distribution**
- Specialists available in city → **Centralised** (pool and dispatch)
- Only generalists available in remote area → **Decentralised** (multi-skill at unit)

**Step 5 — Final Check**
- If Steps 1–4 give **conflicting answers** (e.g., contiguous plant but geographically remote) → **Adopt Combined/Hybrid**

> **Engineering Takeaway:** Pure centralisation or pure decentralisation is *rare*. Most real plants in Kerala (FACT Kochi, BPCL Kochi, Nitta Gelatin) use the **Combined model** with a tilt towards decentralisation for *unit-specific* work and centralisation for *overhauls and major spares*.

---

## 3.4 Quantitative Justification of the Crew-Size Choice (Worked Example)

Suppose a plant has **two identical production units (A & B)**, each needing **2 dedicated mechanics** (per the formula in 3.1) for a 24×7 operation.

**Pure Centralised Option:** A *pool* of mechanics at a central workshop, dispatched as needed.

Total mechanics needed:

$$
N_{central} = \max(N_A, N_B) + \text{surge buffer} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ mechanics}
$$

**Pure Decentralised Option:** Each unit has its *own* dedicated mechanics.

$$
N_{decentral} = N_A + N_B = 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ mechanics}
$$

*However*, if a single mechanic can handle ~80% of breakdowns (multi-skilled) and only 20% need specialists:

$$
N_{combined} = 2 \text{ unit multi-skilled} + 1 \text{ shared specialist} = 3 \text{ mechanics}
$$

> The **Combined model achieves the same coverage as decentralised with the same headcount as centralised**, but with the *agility of decentralised response* and the *expertise depth of centralisation* — the *dominant strategy* in modern maintenance management.
